questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q1: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
1. The sum of the power dissipated by the three resistors must be the same as the power supplied by the battery, regardless of how the resistors are combined. This may be seen by applying Kirchhoff's loop rule (Eq. 19.15) around the circuit shown below, in which the resistors have been combined into their equivalent resistance. The result is

$$
\sum V = 0 = V _ {\mathrm {B}} - V _ {\mathrm {e q}} \Rightarrow V _ {\mathrm {B}} = V _ {\mathrm {e q}}
$$

The current $I$ through each circuit element is the same, so we can multiply each side of the equation by $I$ to get

$$
V _ {\mathrm {B}} I = V _ {\mathrm {e q}} I
$$

Because $VI$ is the power through each circuit element, we obtain

$$
P _ {\mathrm {B}} = P _ {\mathrm {e q}}
$$

which shows that the power dissipated in the equivalent resistance is the same as the power supplied by the battery.

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q2: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 2. All real batteries have an internal resistance $r$ (see Eq. 19.7) that dissipates into heat some of the power supplied by the battery. Thus, the energy that can be extracted from the battery is always less than the energy stored in the battery because some power is lost to heat in the internal resistance.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q3: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 3. The resistivity $\rho$ does not depend on the length or diameter of the rod, so it does not change when these quantities are tripled.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q4: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
4. (a) False: Adding resistance to a circuit can lower the resistance if the resistance is added in parallel with another resistance. For example, adding the resistance $R_{2}$ to the circuit on the left in the drawing below gives the circuit on the right. The equivalent resistance of the circuit on the right is $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)^{-1} = \frac{R_1R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{R_1}{1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}} < R_1$. Thus, the resistance of the circuit decreases upon adding the resistance $R_{2}$ to the circuit.

![img-1.jpeg](img-1.jpeg)

(b) False: Removing a resistance that is in parallel with another resistance can increase the resistance of a circuit. Consider removing the resistance $R_{2}$ from the circuit above on the right. The result is that the resistance of the circuit increases from $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \frac{R_1}{1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}}$ to $R_{1}$.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q5: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 5. An increase in temperature increases the vibrations of atoms in the conductor, which increases the rate of collisions with free electrons as they migrate through the conductor. These collisions remove energy from the electrons, which increases the resistance of the material.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q6: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 6. A capacitor stores energy in the electric field between its plates. A resistor cannot store energy because all the energy consumed by the resistor is converted irreversibly into heat. This energy is thus lost and cannot be stored.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q7: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 7. If a power supply is short-circuited, the current flow will be $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{r}$, where $\mathcal{E}$ is the battery emf and $r$ is the internal resistance of the battery. A large current flowing through the battery could damage the battery. Thus, to prevent excessive current from flowing through the battery in the case of a short circuit, high-voltage batteries have a larger internal resistance $r$.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q8: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 8. The energy taken out of the battery is not all stored in the capacitor because some of it is converted to thermal energy in the resistor. This is an irreversible process, so this energy cannot be recuperated.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q9: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 9. Many practical networks cannot be reduced to simple series-parallel combinations. Figure 19.24 shows two examples.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q10: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
10. When two batteries are connected in series, the total voltage is the sum of the voltages of each battery, as shown in the figure below. Also, when the switch is open, as shown below, no current can flow through either battery, so their energy is conserved.

^{}[]

![img-2.jpeg](img-2.jpeg)

When two batteries are connected in parallel, as shown below, the voltage across the resistor when the switch is closed will be the greater of two battery voltages. When the switch is open, if  $V_{1} \neq V_{2}$ , a current will flow through the batteries, draining the batteries prematurely.

![img-3.jpeg](img-3.jpeg)

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q11: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
11. (i) For  $n$  bulbs of resistance  $R$  connected in series across a battery of voltage  $V_{\mathrm{bat}}$ , the voltage drop across each bulb is  $V_{\mathrm{bulb}} = V_{\mathrm{bat}} / n$ . Thus, adding bulbs in series to a flashlight battery will decrease the voltage drop across each bulb. The power output of each bulb is  $P_{\mathrm{bulb}} = V_{\mathrm{bulb}}^2 / R = V_{\mathrm{bat}}^2 / Rn^2$ , which shows that the bulbs will get dimmer as more bulbs are added in series. (ii) For  $n$  bulbs of resistance  $R$  connected in parallel across a battery of voltage  $V_{\mathrm{bat}}$ , the voltage drop across each bulb is  $V_{\mathrm{bulb}} = V_{\mathrm{bat}}$ . Thus, the power output of the bulbs will remain constant as more bulbs are added, provided the battery can supply the requisite current. The battery

The total power required for  $n$  bulbs in series is  $P_{\mathrm{total}}^{\mathrm{ser}} = nP_{\mathrm{bulb}} = V_{\mathrm{bat}}^2 / Rn$ . For  $n$  bulbs of resistance  $R$  connected in parallel, the equivalent resistance is  $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R / n$ . Ohm's law gives  $V_{\mathrm{bat}} = IR_{\mathrm{eq}} = IR / n$ , or  $I = V_{\mathrm{bat}}n / R$ . The power required for  $n$  bulbs in parallel is  $P_{\mathrm{total}}^{\mathrm{per}} = V_{\mathrm{bat}}I = V_{\mathrm{bat}}^2 n / R$ . Thus, the power required decreases as  $1 / n$  as you add more bulbs in series, whereas it increases linearly in  $n$  when you add more bulbs in parallel. Your battery will thus last longer if you connect the bulbs in series.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q12: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 12. The difference is the power that is transmitted through your body. When you scuff your shoes on the carpet, you accumulate a small charge that is discharged across a high voltage. This small charge makes for a very small current, so the power  $P = VI$  transmitted through your body is very small. A power line, however, can deliver a very large current, so the power transmitted through your body would be very large and could easily be fatal.

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q13: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 13. The resistance of the skin is  $1000\Omega \lesssim R_{\mathrm{skin}} \lesssim 500\mathrm{k}\Omega$ . Assuming the worst-case scenario, the current through your finger would be  $I = V / R = 9\mathrm{V} / 1000\Omega = 9\mathrm{mA}$

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q14: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
14. Power is given by  $P = VI$ , so the current flowing through the overhead power line would be

$$
I = \frac {P}{V} = \frac {3 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {6} \mathrm {W}}{7 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ {3} \mathrm {V}} = 4 0 0 \mathrm {A}
$$

questions.questionText: Chapter 19 — Conceptual Q15: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
15. Assume that the diameter of an amoeba is about $500\ \mu\mathrm{m}$, and that the amoeba is spherical. Its total capacitance would be

$$
C = \left(1\ \mu\mathrm{F/cm^2}\right) \left(4\pi r^2\right) = \left(1\ \mu\mathrm{F/cm^2}\right) \left(4\pi\right) \left(250 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{cm}\right)^2 = 8\ \mathrm{nF}
$$

questions.questionText: 19.1. $1\ \mathrm{A} = 1\ \mathrm{C/s}$, so $\Delta Q = I\Delta t$. An electron has charge $e = -1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}$. The current tells us how much charge flows in a given time.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\Delta Q = I\Delta t = (5.0\ \mathrm{A})(60\ \mathrm{s}) = 300\ \mathrm{C}$

(b) The number $n$ of electrons that flows through is $n = \left|\frac{Q}{e}\right| = \frac{-300\ \mathrm{C}}{-1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}} = 1.9 \times 10^{21}$.

Reflect: The current is positive, so the charge flow is defined to be positive. Because electrons are the charges that move, in reality, electrons are flowing in the direction opposite that of the current.

questions.questionText: 19.2. $I = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}$
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $\Delta Q = I\Delta t = (25,000\ \mathrm{A})(40 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{s}) = 1.0\ \mathrm{C}$

questions.questionText: 19.3. The number of ions that enter gives the charge that enters the axon in the specified time. $I = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\Delta Q = (5.6 \times 10^{11}\ \mathrm{ions})(1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C/ion}) = 9.0 \times 10^{-8}\ \mathrm{C}$

$$
I = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-8}\ \mathrm{C}}{10 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{s}} = 9.0\ \mu\mathrm{A}
$$

questions.questionText: 19.4. Negative charge moving from $A$ to $B$ is equivalent to an equal magnitude of positive charge going from $B$ to $A$. $I = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}$. The current direction is the direction of flow of positive charge.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The total positive charge moving from $B$ to $A$ is

$$
\Delta Q = (5.11 \times 10^{18} + 2[3.24 \times 10^{18}])(1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}) = 1.85\ \mathrm{C}. \quad I = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t} = \frac{1.85\ \mathrm{C}}{30\ \mathrm{s}} = 62\ \mathrm{mA}.
$$

Positive charge flows from $B$ to $A$ so the current is in this direction.

questions.questionText: 19.5. $V = IR$. For copper, Table 19.1 gives $\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\mathrm{m}$ and for silver, $\rho = 1.47 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\mathrm{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{(1.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\mathrm{m})(2.00\ \mathrm{m})}{\pi(0.814 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m})^2} = 1.65 \times 10^{-2}\ \Omega$

$$
V = (12.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{A})(1.65 \times 10^{-2}\ \Omega) = 2.06 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{V}.
$$

(b) $V = \frac{I\rho L}{A}$. $\frac{V}{\rho} = \frac{I L}{A} = \text{constant}$, so $\frac{V_s}{\rho_s} = \frac{V_c}{\rho_c}$.

$$
V_s = V_c \left(\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_c}\right) = (2.06 \times 10^{-4}) \left(\frac{1.47 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\mathrm{m}}{1.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\mathrm{m}}\right) = 1.76 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{V}.
$$

questions.questionText: 19.6. $R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{\rho L}{\pi(d / 2)^2}$ . From Table 19.1, copper has  $\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:  $L = \frac{\pi(d / 2)^2R}{\rho} = \frac{\pi(1.628\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m})^2(1.00\Omega)}{4(1.72\times 10^{-8}\Omega\cdot\mathrm{m})} = 121\mathrm{m}$

questions.questionText: 19.7. The resistivity of the wire can be used to identify what material it is made of.  $R = \rho L / A$  and the radius of the wire is half its diameter.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Solve for  $\rho$  and substitute the numerical values.

$$
\rho = A R / L = \pi (D / 2) ^ {2} R / L = \frac {\pi ([ 0 . 0 0 2 0 5 \mathrm {m} ] / 2) ^ {2} (0 . 0 2 9 0 \Omega)}{6 . 5 0 \mathrm {m}} = 1. 4 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 8} \Omega \cdot \mathrm {m}
$$

Reflect: This result is the same as the resistivity of silver, which suggests that the material is silver.

questions.questionText: 19.8. $R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$ . The length of the wire in the spring is the circumference  $\pi d$  of each coil times the number of coils.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $L = (75)\pi d = (75)\pi (3.50\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m}) = 8.25\mathrm{m}$

$$
A = \pi r ^ {2} = \pi d ^ {2} / 4 = \pi (3. 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m}) ^ {2} / 4 = 8. 3 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {m} ^ {2}.
$$

$$
\rho = \frac {R A}{L} = \frac {(1 . 7 4 \Omega) (8 . 3 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {m} ^ {2})}{8 . 2 5 \mathrm {m}} = 1. 7 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \Omega \cdot \mathrm {m}.
$$

Reflect: The value of  $\rho$  we calculated is about a factor of 100 times larger than  $\rho$  for copper. The metal of the spring is not a very good conductor.

questions.questionText: 19.9. $R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{\rho L}{\pi d^2 / 4}$ . For aluminum,  $\rho_{\mathrm{al}} = 2.63 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$ . For copper,  $\rho_{\mathrm{c}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $\frac{\rho}{d^2} = \frac{R\pi}{4L} =$  constant, so  $\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{al}}}{d_{\mathrm{al}}^2} = \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{c}}}{d_{\mathrm{c}}^2}$

$$
d _ {\mathrm {c}} = d _ {\mathrm {a l}} \sqrt {\frac {\rho_ {\mathrm {c}}}{\rho_ {\mathrm {a l}}}} = (3. 2 6 \mathrm {m m}) \sqrt {\frac {1 . 7 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 8} \Omega \cdot \mathrm {m}}{2 . 6 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 8} \Omega \cdot \mathrm {m}}} = 2. 6 4 \mathrm {m m}.
$$

questions.questionText: 19.10. $R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$ . For aluminum,  $\rho_{\mathrm{al}} = 2.63 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$ . For copper,  $\rho_{\mathrm{c}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $R = \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{al}}L}{A} = \frac{(2.63\times 10^{-8}\Omega\cdot\mathrm{m})(3.80\mathrm{m})}{(1.00\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m})(5.00\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m})} = 2.00\times 10^{-4}\Omega$

(b)  $R = \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{c}}L}{\pi r^2}$ .  $L = \frac{R\pi r^2}{\rho_{\mathrm{c}}} = \frac{(2.00\times 10^{-4}\Omega)\pi(0.750\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m})^2}{1.72\times 10^{-8}\Omega} = 0.0205\mathrm{m} = 2.05\mathrm{cm}$

questions.questionText: 19.11. $R = \frac{\rho L}{A_1} = \frac{\rho L}{\pi r_1^2}$ .  $3R = \frac{\rho L}{A_2} = \frac{\rho L}{\pi r_2^2}$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Taking the ratio of these equations gives  $\frac{R}{3R} = \frac{\rho L}{\pi r_1^2}\frac{\pi r_2^2}{\rho L} = \frac{r_1^2}{r_1^2}$  so  $\frac{r_1}{r_2} = \sqrt{3}$

Reflect: Because the cross-sectional area scales as the radius squared, the resistance scales as the inverse square of the radius.

questions.questionText: 19.12. $R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$ .  $L_{\mathrm{new}} = 3L$ . The volume of the wire remains the same when it is stretched.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Volume  $= LA$  so  $LA = L_{\mathrm{new}}A_{\mathrm{new}}$ .  $A_{\mathrm{new}} = \frac{L}{L_{\mathrm{new}}}A = \frac{A}{3}$ .



$$
R _ {\mathrm {n e w}} = \frac {\rho L _ {\mathrm {n e w}}}{A _ {\mathrm {n e w}}} = \frac {\rho (3 L)}{A / 3} = 9 \frac {\rho L}{A} = 9 R.
$$

Reflect: When the length increases the resistance increases, and when the area decreases the resistance increases.
Solve:  $R_{T} = (100.00\Omega)[1 + (0.00040(\mathrm{C}^{\circ})^{-1})(0.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C} - 11.5^{\circ}\mathrm{C})] = 99.54\Omega$

questions.questionText: 19.14. First use Ohm's law to find the resistance at  $20.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; then calculate the resistivity from the resistance. Finally, use the dependence of resistance on temperature to calculate the temperature coefficient of resistance. Ohm's law is  $R = V / I$ ,  $R = \rho L / A$ ,  $R = R_0[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$ , and the radius is one-half the diameter.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) At  $20.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, R = V / I = (15.0\mathrm{V}) / (18.5\mathrm{A}) = 0.811\Omega$ . Using  $R = \rho L / A$  and solving for  $\rho$  gives  $\rho = RA / L = R\pi (D / 2)^{2} / L = (0.811\Omega)\pi [(0.00500\mathrm{m}) / 2]^{2} / (1.50\mathrm{m}) = 1.06\times 10^{-5}\Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$ .

(b) At  $92.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, R = V / I = (15.0\mathrm{V}) / (17.2\mathrm{A}) = 0.872\Omega$ . Using  $R = R_0[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$  with  $T_0$  taken as  $20.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , we have  $0.872\Omega = (0.811\Omega)[1 + \alpha (92.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C} - 20.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C})]$ . This gives  $\alpha = 0.00105(^{\circ}\mathrm{C})^{-1}$

Reflect: The results are typical of ordinary metals.

Solve: When  $T = 0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the resistance is

$$
R _ {T} = R _ {0} \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T _ {0}) \right] = (1 0 0 0 \Omega) \left\{1 + \left[ + 0. 0 0 5 0 (\mathrm {^ {\circ} C}) ^ {- 1} \right] (0 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C} - 1 0 0 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C}) \right\} = 5 0 0 \Omega
$$

Reflect: This large change in resistance is due to the large change in temperature.

questions.questionText: 19.16. Ohm's law says  $V = IR$ , where  $R$  is a constant.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Ohm's law says a graph of  $V$  versus  $I$  is a straight line. That is the case here, so this object obeys Ohm's law.

(b)  $V = IR$  says  $R$  is the slope of a graph of  $V$  versus  $I$ .

(c)  $R = \frac{0.60\mathrm{V} - 0.30\mathrm{V}}{4.0\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{A} - 2.0\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{A}} = 150\Omega$

Reflect: We can always define  $R = V / I$ , but only when  $R$  is independent of  $I$  does the object obey Ohm's law.

questions.questionText: 19.17. Ohm's law says  $R = \frac{V_{ab}}{I}$  is constant.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The graph is given in the figure below

![img-4.jpeg](img-4.jpeg)

(b) The graph of  $V_{ab}$  versus  $I$  is a straight line so Nichrome™ obeys Ohm's law.



(c) $R$ is the slope of the graph in part (a). $R = \frac{15.52\,\mathrm{V} - 1.94\,\mathrm{V}}{4.00\,\mathrm{A} - 0.50\,\mathrm{A}} = 3.88\,\Omega$.

questions.questionText: 19.18. Since the battery has negligible internal resistance, $\mathcal{E} = IR$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $R = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{I} = \frac{1.50\,\mathrm{V}}{18.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{A}} = 83.3\,\Omega$

(b) $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \frac{12.0\,\mathrm{V}}{83.3\,\Omega} = 0.144\,\mathrm{A}$

(c) $\mathcal{E} = IR = (0.453\,\mathrm{A})(83.3\,\Omega) = 37.7\,\mathrm{V}$

questions.questionText: 19.19. $V = IR$, where $I$ is the current through the battery and $V$ is the potential difference applied to the person.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $V = IR = (5.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{A})(1000\,\Omega) = 5.0\,\mathrm{V}$. This is well within the range of common household voltages.

questions.questionText: 19.20. Use $R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$, where $A = \pi r^2$ and $r = 0.050\,\mathrm{m}$, to calculate $R$ and then apply $V = IR$. For copper we have $\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{(1.72 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega \cdot \mathrm{m})(100 \times 10^{2}\,\mathrm{m})}{\pi(0.050\,\mathrm{m})^{2}} = 0.219\,\Omega$. $V = IR = (125\,\mathrm{A})(0.219\,\Omega) = 27.4\,\mathrm{V}$.

Reflect: Even though the resistance of the cable is relatively low, there is a significant voltage drop due to the high current passing through the cable.

questions.questionText: 19.21. $R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{\rho L}{\pi(d / 2)^2}$. $V = IR$. Table 19.1 gives $\rho = 2.44 \times 10^{-8} \,\Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$ for gold.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $R = \frac{4\rho L}{\pi d^2} = \frac{4(2.44\times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot\mathrm{m})(6.40\,\mathrm{m})}{\pi(0.840\times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m})^2} = 0.282\,\Omega$

(b) $V = IR = (1.15\,\mathrm{A})(0.282\,\Omega) = 0.324\,\mathrm{V}$

questions.questionText: 19.22. $R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{\rho L}{\pi(D / 2)^2} = \frac{4\rho L}{\pi D^2}$. For steel, $\frac{4L}{\pi D^2} = \frac{R_{\text{steel}}}{\rho_{\text{steel}}}$. From Ohm's law $V = IR$, we get $I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{VA}{\rho_{\mathrm{Ag}}L} = \frac{V\pi(D / 2)^2}{\rho_{\mathrm{Ag}}L} = \frac{V\pi D^2}{4\rho_{\mathrm{Ag}}L} = \frac{V}{\rho_{\mathrm{Ag}}}\left(\frac{\pi D^2}{4L}\right) = \frac{V}{\rho_{\mathrm{Ag}}}\left(\frac{\rho_{\text{steel}}}{R_{\text{steel}}}\right)$. From Table 19.1, we obtain $\rho_{\mathrm{Ag}} = 1.47\times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot\mathrm{m}$ and $\rho_{\text{steel}} = 20\times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot\mathrm{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Inserting the given values gives $I = \frac{V}{\rho_{\mathrm{Ag}}}\left(\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{steel}}}{R_{\mathrm{steel}}}\right) = \frac{10.0\,\mathrm{V}}{1.47 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}}\left(\frac{20 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}}{100\,\Omega}\right) = 1.4\,\mathrm{A}$

Reflect: This result is independent of the original length and diameter of the steel wire.

questions.questionText: 19.23. The total resistance of the circuit can be calculated from Ohm's law: $R_{\text{total}} = \frac{V}{I}$. Since the internal resistance of the battery is in series with the resistance of the light bulb we have $R_{\text{total}} = 10.5\,\Omega + R_{\text{internal}}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: From the known current and voltage we have $R_{\text{total}} = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{6.00\,\mathrm{V}}{0.350\,\mathrm{A}} = 17.1\,\Omega$. Thus, the internal resistance of the battery is $R_{\text{internal}} = R_{\text{total}} - 10.5\,\Omega = 17.1\,\Omega - 10.5\,\Omega = 6.6\,\Omega$.

Reflect: The internal resistance of the battery is a significant fraction of the total resistance and limits the maximum current that this battery can provide to $I_{\max} = \frac{6.00\,\mathrm{V}}{6.6\,\Omega} \approx 0.9\,\mathrm{A}$.

questions.questionText: 19.24. When the switch is open there is no current and the terminal voltage of the battery equals its emf, $\varepsilon$. When the switch is closed, current $I$ flows and the terminal voltage $V$ of the battery is $V = \varepsilon - Ir$. The current $I$ is the same at all points of the circuit.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\varepsilon = 3.08\mathrm{V}$. $V = \varepsilon - Ir$, with $V = 2.97\mathrm{V}$ and $I = 1.65\mathrm{A}$, so $r = \frac{V - \varepsilon}{I} = \frac{3.08\mathrm{V} - 2.97\mathrm{V}}{1.65\mathrm{A}} = 0.067\Omega$.

$$
V - I R = 0 \text{ and } R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{2.97\mathrm{V}}{1.65\mathrm{A}} = 1.80\Omega.
$$

Reflect: When current flows through the battery, the terminal voltage is less than the emf because of the voltage across the internal resistance.

questions.questionText: 19.25. The emf of the battery is $10.0\mathrm{V}$. The internal resistance may be treated as an external resistance in series with an ideal battery with the given emf. Use Ohm's law $V = IR$ to find the voltage drop across the resistor $R$. The current $I$ in the circuit is also found from Ohm's law: $\mathcal{E} = I(R + r)$, where $r = 2.0\Omega$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The voltage drop across the resistor $R$ is $V_{R} = IR = \frac{\mathcal{E}R}{R + r}$. For $R = 100\Omega$, this gives

$$
V_{R} = \frac{(10.0\mathrm{V})(100\Omega)}{100\Omega + 2.0\Omega} = 9.8\mathrm{V}.
$$

(b) For $R = 10\Omega$, we obtain $V_{R} = \frac{(10.0\mathrm{V})(10\Omega)}{10\Omega + 2.0\Omega} = 8.3\mathrm{V}$.

(c) For $R = 2\Omega$, we obtain $V_{R} = \frac{(10.0\mathrm{V})(2\Omega)}{2\Omega + 2.0\Omega} = 5\mathrm{V}$.

(d) The current through the battery is $I = \frac{V_{R}}{R}$. For part (a), $R = 100\Omega$ and $V_{R} = 9.8\mathrm{V}$, so $I = \frac{9.8\mathrm{V}}{100\Omega} = 0.098\mathrm{A}$. For part (b), $R = 10\Omega$ and $V_{R} = 8.3\mathrm{V}$, so $I = \frac{8.3\mathrm{V}}{10\Omega} = 0.83\mathrm{A}$. For part (c), $R = 2\Omega$ and $V_{R} = 5\mathrm{V}$, so $I = \frac{5\mathrm{V}}{2\Omega} = 2.5\mathrm{A}$.

Reflect: Note that for an ideal battery, $r = 0$ and $V_{R} = \frac{\mathcal{E}R}{R + r} \to \mathcal{E}$, as expected.

questions.questionText: 19.26. The terminal voltage of the battery is given by $V_{ab} = \varepsilon - Ir$. The terminal voltage also equals the voltage across the resistor, so $V_{ab} = IR$. We have two unknowns $(\varepsilon, r)$ so we need two equations.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) With the $1500\mathrm{M}\Omega$ resistor, $I = \frac{V_{ab}}{R} = \frac{2.50\mathrm{V}}{1500\times 10^{6}\Omega} = 1.67\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{A}$.

$$
V_{ab} = \varepsilon - Ir \text{ gives } 2.50\mathrm{V} = \varepsilon - (1.67 \times 10^{-9}\mathrm{A})r.
$$

With the $5.00\Omega$ resistor, $I = \frac{V_{ab}}{R} = \frac{1.75\mathrm{V}}{5.00\Omega} = 0.350\mathrm{A}$.

$$
V_{ab} = \varepsilon - Ir \text{ gives } 1.75V = \varepsilon - (0.350\mathrm{A})r.
$$

Subtracting the second equation from the first gives $0.75\mathrm{V} = (0.350\mathrm{A})r$ and $r = 2.14\Omega$.

$$
\varepsilon = 1.75\mathrm{V} + (0.350\mathrm{A})r = 2.50\mathrm{V}
$$

(b) Now $R$ is $7.00\Omega$. $I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R + r} = \frac{2.50\mathrm{V}}{7.00\Omega + 2.14\Omega} = 0.274\mathrm{A}$

$$
V_{ab} = \varepsilon - Ir = 2.50\mathrm{V} - (0.274\mathrm{A})(2.14\Omega) = 1.91\mathrm{V}. \text{Or},
$$

$$
V_{ab} = IR = 1.91\mathrm{V}.
$$

Reflect: The smaller $R$ is, the larger the current and the smaller the terminal voltage. With the $1500\mathrm{M}\Omega$ resistor the current is very small and the terminal voltage differs only very little from the emf of the battery.

questions.questionText: 19.27. For a battery with internal resistance $r$ we have $V_{ab} = \varepsilon - Ir$. We know that the terminal voltage of the battery drops from $12.0\mathrm{V}$ to $7.0\mathrm{V}$ when the battery provides a current of $150\mathrm{A}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Solving for the internal resistance, $r$, we obtain $r = \frac{\mathcal{E} - V_{ab}}{I} = \frac{12.0\,\mathrm{V} - 7.0\,\mathrm{V}}{150\,\mathrm{A}} = 0.033\,\Omega$.

Reflect: The resistance of the starter motor circuit (including its cables and connectors) must be $\frac{7.0\,\mathrm{V}}{150\,\mathrm{A}} = 0.047\,\Omega$.

The internal resistance of the battery plus the resistance of the starter circuit adds to the total resistance, which is $\frac{12.0\,\mathrm{V}}{150\,\mathrm{A}} = 0.0800\,\Omega$.

questions.questionText: 19.28. When current passes through a battery in the direction from the – terminal toward the + terminal, the terminal voltage $V_{ab}$ of the battery is $V_{ab} = \mathcal{E} - Ir$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $V_{ab} = \mathcal{E} - Ir$ gives $r = \frac{\mathcal{E} - V_{ab}}{I} = \frac{24.0\,\mathrm{V} - 21.2\,\mathrm{V}}{4.00\,\mathrm{A}} = 0.700\,\Omega$.

(b) $V_{ab} - IR = 0$ so $R = \frac{V_{ab}}{I} = \frac{21.2\,\mathrm{V}}{4.00\,\mathrm{A}} = 5.30\,\Omega$.

Reflect: The total resistance in the circuit is $R + r = 6.00\,\Omega$. $I = \frac{24.0\,\mathrm{V}}{6.00\,\Omega} = 4.00\,\mathrm{A}$, which agrees with the value specified in the problem.

questions.questionText: 19.29. For a resistor, $P = VI$ and $V = IR$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{327\,\mathrm{W}}{15.0\,\mathrm{V}} = 21.8\,\mathrm{A}$

(b) $R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{15.0\,\mathrm{V}}{21.8\,\mathrm{A}} = 0.688\,\Omega$

questions.questionText: 19.30. For a resistor, $P = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R} = VI$ and $V = IR$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $I = \sqrt{\frac{P}{R}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.0\,\mathrm{W}}{15 \times 10^3\,\Omega}} = 0.0183\,\mathrm{A}$. $V = IR = (0.0183\,\mathrm{A})(15 \times 10^3\,\Omega) = 275\,\mathrm{V}$.

(b) $P = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{(120\,\mathrm{V})^2}{9.0 \times 10^3\,\Omega} = 1.6\,\mathrm{W}$

questions.questionText:
19.31. The voltmeter reads the terminal voltage of the battery, which is the potential difference across the appliance. The terminal voltage is less than $15.0\,\mathrm{V}$ because some potential is lost across the internal resistance of the battery. The equation $P = V^2 / R$ gives the power dissipated by the appliance. The equation $V_{ab} = \mathcal{E} - Ir$ relates the terminal voltage of the battery to its internal resistance.

(a)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $P = (11.3\,\mathrm{V})^2 / (75.0\,\Omega) = 1.70\,\mathrm{W}$

(b) The current in the circuit can be calculated from the terminal voltage of the battery and the resistance of the attached appliance: $I = \frac{11.3\,\mathrm{V}}{75.0\,\Omega} = 0.151\,\mathrm{A}$. The internal resistance of the battery, $r$, is given by $r = \frac{\mathcal{E} - V_{ab}}{I} = \frac{15.0\,\mathrm{V} - 11.3\,\mathrm{V}}{0.151\,\mathrm{A}} = 24.5\,\Omega$.

Reflect: The full $15.0\,\mathrm{V}$ of the battery would be available only when no current (or a very small current) is flowing in the circuit. This would be the case if the appliance had a resistance much greater than $24.5\,\Omega$.

questions.questionText: 19.32. Power is energy per unit time. The power delivered by a voltage source is $P = V_{ab} I$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $P = (25\,\mathrm{V})(12\,\mathrm{A}) = 300\,\mathrm{W}$.



(b) Energy $= Pt = (300 \mathrm{~W})(3.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}) = 0.90 \mathrm{~J}$

Solve: (a) $R = \frac{V^2}{P} = \frac{(120\mathrm{V})^2}{60\mathrm{W}} = 240\Omega$

(b) $P$ increases when $R$ decreases, so the 100 W bulb has less resistance.

$$
R = \frac {V ^ {2}}{P} = \frac {(1 2 0 \mathrm {V}) ^ {2}}{1 0 0 \mathrm {W}} = 1 4 4 \Omega
$$

Reflect: $P = I^2 R$. The bulb with smaller $R$ draws more current and this more than compensates for the smaller $R$ in $I^2 R$.

Solve: (a) $I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{120\mathrm{V}}{1.0\times 10^{3}\Omega} = 0.12\mathrm{A}$. Yes, this is very dangerous.

(b) $P = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{(120\mathrm{V})^2}{1.0\times 10^3\Omega} = 14.4\mathrm{W}$. We can also calculate $P$ as $I^2 R$ or $VI$. These expressions also give $14.4\mathrm{W}$.

questions.questionText: 19.35. $P = VI$ and energy is the product of power and time.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $P = (500\mathrm{V})(80\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{A}) = 40\mathrm{W}$

$$
\mathrm {E n e r g y} = P t = (4 0 \mathrm {W}) (1 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {s}) = 0. 4 0 \mathrm {J}.
$$

Reflect: The energy delivered depends not only on the voltage and current but also on the length of the pulse.

Solve: (a) The current through each resistor is $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{20\mathrm{V}}{500\Omega + 200\Omega} = 0.029\mathrm{A}$.

(b) The power delivered to the $500\Omega$ resistor is $P_{500} = I^2 R_{500} = (0.02857\mathrm{A})^2 (500\Omega) = 0.41\mathrm{W}$. The power delivered to the $200\Omega$ resistor is $P_{200} = I^2 R_{200} = (0.02857\mathrm{A})^2 (200\Omega) = 0.16\mathrm{W}$.

(c) By conservation of energy, the power delivered by the battery is $P_{\mathrm{bat}} = P_{500} + P_{200} = 0.57$ W.

Reflect: The power delivered by the battery may also be calculated by using $P_{\mathrm{bat}} = \frac{\mathcal{E}^2}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{(20\mathrm{V})^2}{700\Omega} = 0.57\mathrm{W}$, which is consistent with the result in part (c).

questions.questionText: 19.37. The power delivered by the battery can be calculated in two different ways: $P = \frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Time}}$ or $P = VI$. $5.25\mathrm{h} = (5.25\mathrm{h})(3600\mathrm{s/h}) = 1.89\times 10^{4}\mathrm{s}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The average power delivered by the battery is $P = \frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{3.15 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J}}{1.89 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~s}} = 1.6667 \mathrm{~W}$. Thus, the current must be $I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{1.6667 \mathrm{~W}}{3.70 \mathrm{~V}} = 0.450 \mathrm{~A}$.



19-11

Reflect: The energy stored in the battery can be expressed in joules or watt-hours: using the equation Energy = Pt, we can express the stored energy as either $3.15 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J}$ or $(1.6667 \mathrm{~W})(5.25 \mathrm{~h}) = 8.75 \mathrm{~W} \cdot \mathrm{h}$.

questions.questionText: 19.38. For an emf, $P = VI$. For a resistor, $P = I^2 R$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $12.0\mathrm{V} - I(1.0\Omega +5.0\Omega) = 0$ and $I = 2.0\mathrm{A}$. The rate at which chemical energy is being converted to electrical energy within the battery is $P_{e} = VI = (12.0\mathrm{V})(2.0\mathrm{A}) = 24.0\mathrm{W}$.

(b) $P_{r} = I^{2}r = (2.0\mathrm{A})^{2}(1.0\Omega) = 4.0\mathrm{W}$
(c) $P_{R} = I^{2}R = (2.0\mathrm{A})^{2}(5.0\Omega) = 20.0\mathrm{W}$

Note that $P_{e} = P_{r} + P_{R}$.

Solve: (a) $P = \frac{V^2}{R}$ so $R = \frac{V^2}{P} = \frac{(12.0\mathrm{V})^2}{240\mathrm{W}} = 0.600\Omega$
(b) $P = VI$ so $I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{240\mathrm{W}}{12.0\mathrm{V}} = 20.0\mathrm{A}$
(c) Assuming that $R$ remains $0.600\Omega$, $P = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{(11\mathrm{V})^2}{0.600\Omega} = 2.0\times 10^2\mathrm{W}$.

Reflect: The power is proportional to the voltage squared.

questions.questionText: 19.40. For resistors in parallel, $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \dots$ For resistors in series, $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R_1 + R_2 + \dots$. These rules may have to be applied in several ways.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) All four resistors are in parallel. $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{25\Omega} + \frac{1}{12\Omega} + \frac{1}{5.0\Omega} + \frac{1}{45\Omega}$ so $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 2.9\Omega$.

(b) The $75\Omega$ and $25\Omega$ resistors are in parallel. $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{p}}} = \frac{1}{75\Omega} + \frac{1}{25\Omega}$ so their equivalent is $R_{\mathrm{p}} = 18.8\Omega$. The $9.0\Omega$, $18.8\Omega$, and $18\Omega$ resistors are in series, so $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 9.0\Omega + 18.8\Omega + 18\Omega = 45.8\Omega$.

(c) The $13\Omega$ and $15\Omega$ resistors are in series, so their equivalent is $R_{\mathrm{s}} = 13\Omega + 15\Omega = 28\Omega$. The $32\Omega$, $28\Omega$, and $14\Omega$ resistors are in parallel, so $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{p1}}} = \frac{1}{32\Omega} + \frac{1}{28\Omega} + \frac{1}{14\Omega}$ and their equivalent is $R_{\mathrm{p1}} = 7.2\Omega$. The $72\Omega$ and $45\Omega$ resistors are in parallel so $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{p2}}} = \frac{1}{72\Omega} + \frac{1}{45\Omega}$ and their equivalent is $R_{\mathrm{p2}} = 27.7\Omega$. This gives a network with $19\Omega$, $7.2\Omega$, $27.7\Omega$ and $24\Omega$ in series. The equivalent resistance is $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 19\Omega + 7.2\Omega + 27.7\Omega + 24\Omega = 77.9\Omega$.

(d) The $12\Omega$, $13\Omega$ and $14\Omega$ resistors are in series and their equivalent is $R_{\mathrm{s}} = 12\Omega + 13\Omega + 14\Omega = 39\Omega$. $R_{\mathrm{s}}$ and the $22\Omega$ resistor are in parallel and their equivalent $R_{\mathrm{p}}$ is given by $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{p}}} = \frac{1}{39\Omega} + \frac{1}{22\Omega}$, so $R_{\mathrm{p}} = 14.1\Omega$. Then $R_{\mathrm{p}}$ and the $11\Omega$ resistor are in series and $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 14.1\Omega + 11\Omega = 25.1\Omega$.

questions.questionText:
19.41. The resistors can be placed in any combination of series and parallel. The equivalent resistance of two resistors in series is $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R_1 + R_2$, which is larger than either resistance by itself. However, the equivalent resistance of two resistors in parallel is given by $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}}$, which is always less than the smaller of the two



resistances. Thus, to obtain the maximum equivalent resistance the three resistors should all be in series and to obtain the minimum equivalent resistance they should all be in parallel.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) For three resistors in series we have $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 36\Omega + 47\Omega + 51\Omega = 134\Omega$.

(b) For three resistors in parallel we have $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{36\Omega} + \frac{1}{47\Omega} + \frac{1}{51\Omega}} = 15\Omega$.

Reflect: In addition to these two extreme cases, we can combine the three resistors in a variety of other combinations: for example, we can form a series combination of a single resistor and a pair of resistors that are connected in parallel. Can you find all possible combinations of these three resistors?

questions.questionText: 19.42. Treat each bar as a resistor of resistance $R$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The bars are welded in series, so the resistance of the bars add in series: $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 2R$.

(b) The bars are welded in parallel, so the resistance of the bars add in parallel: $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \left(\frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{R}\right)^{-1} = \frac{R}{2}$.

questions.questionText: 19.43. For a parallel connection the full line voltage appears across each resistor. The equivalent resistance is $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}$ and $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \frac{(40.0\Omega)(90.0\Omega)}{40.0\Omega + 90.0\Omega} = 27.7\Omega$

(b) $I = \frac{V}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{120\mathrm{V}}{27.7\Omega} = 4.33\mathrm{A}$

(c) For the $40.0\Omega$ resistor, $I_{1} = \frac{V}{R_{1}} = \frac{120\mathrm{V}}{40.0\Omega} = 3.00\mathrm{A}$. For the $90.0\Omega$ resistor, $I_{2} = \frac{V}{R_{2}} = \frac{120\mathrm{V}}{90.0\Omega} = 1.33\mathrm{A}$.

Reflect: If either resistor is removed, the voltage and current for the other resistor are unchanged.

questions.questionText: 19.44. Let $R_{1} = 1.60\Omega$, $R_{2} = 2.40\Omega$, $R_{3} = 4.80\Omega$. For a parallel network the full battery voltage appears across each resistor. The equivalent resistance $R_{\mathrm{eq}}$ is given by $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3}$. $P = \frac{V^2}{R}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{1.60\Omega} + \frac{1}{2.40\Omega} + \frac{1}{4.80\Omega}$ and $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 0.80\Omega$.

(b) $I_{1} = \frac{V}{R_{1}} = \frac{28.0\mathrm{V}}{1.60\Omega} = 17.5\mathrm{A}$. $I_{2} = \frac{V}{R_{2}} = \frac{28.0\mathrm{V}}{2.40\Omega} = 11.7\mathrm{A}$. $I_{3} = \frac{V}{R_{3}} = \frac{28.0\mathrm{V}}{4.80\Omega} = 5.8\mathrm{A}$.

(c) $I = \frac{V}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{28.0\mathrm{V}}{0.80\Omega} = 35.0\mathrm{A}$. This is also equal to $I_{1} + I_{2} + I_{3}$.

(d) The voltage is $28.0\mathrm{V}$ across each resistor.

(e) $P_{1} = \frac{V^{2}}{R_{1}} = \frac{(28.0\mathrm{V})^{2}}{1.60\Omega} = 490\mathrm{W}$. $P_{2} = \frac{V^{2}}{R_{2}} = \frac{(28.0\mathrm{V})^{2}}{2.40\Omega} = 327\mathrm{W}$. $P_{3} = \frac{V^{2}}{R_{3}} = \frac{(28.0\mathrm{V})^{2}}{4.80\Omega} = 163\mathrm{W}$.

(f) Since $P = \frac{V^2}{R}$ and $V$ is the same for all three resistors, the resistor with the smallest $R$ dissipates the greatest power.

questions.questionText:
19.45. Let $R_{1} = 1.60\Omega$, $R_{2} = 2.40\Omega$, $R_{3} = 4.80\Omega$. For a series network, the current is the same in each resistor and the sum of voltages for each resistor equals the battery voltage. The equivalent resistance is $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R_{1} + R_{2} + R_{3}$. $P = I^{2}R$.

^{}[]
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 1.60\Omega + 2.40\Omega + 4.80\Omega = 8.80\Omega$

(b)  $I = \frac{V}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{28.0\mathrm{V}}{8.80\Omega} = 3.18\mathrm{A}$
(c)  $I = 3.18\mathrm{A}$ , the same as for each resistor.
(d)  $V_{1} = IR_{1} = (3.18\mathrm{A})(1.60\Omega) = 5.09\mathrm{V}$ .  $V_{2} = IR_{2} = (3.18\mathrm{A})(2.40\Omega) = 7.63\mathrm{V}$ .

$V_{3} = IR_{3} = (3.18\mathrm{A})(4.80\Omega) = 15.3\mathrm{V}.$

Note that  $V_{1} + V_{2} + V_{3} = 28.0\mathrm{V}$

(e)  $P_{1} = I^{2}R_{1} = (3.18\mathrm{A})^{2}(1.60\Omega) = 16.2\mathrm{W}$ .  $P_{2} = I^{2}R_{2} = (3.18\mathrm{A})^{2}(2.40\Omega) = 24.3\mathrm{W}$ .

$P_{3} = I^{2}R_{3} = (3.18\mathrm{A})^{2}(4.80\Omega) = 48.5\mathrm{W}.$

(f) Since  $P = I^2 R$  and the current is the same for each resistor, the resistor with the greatest  $R$  dissipates the greatest power.

Reflect: When resistors are connected in parallel, the resistor with the smallest  $R$  dissipates the greatest power.

questions.questionText: 19.46. For resistors in parallel, the voltages are the same and the currents add.  $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}$  so  $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \frac{R_1R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ . For resistors in series, the currents are the same and the voltages add.  $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R_1 + R_2$ .

questions.questionText: 19.48. We can use the power rating in $P = V^2 / R$ to find the resistance of a bulb. Then we can find the voltage across each bulb when they are connected in series and in parallel.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $R = \frac{V^2}{P} = \frac{(120\mathrm{~V})^2}{100\mathrm{~W}} = 144\Omega$

(a) $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 2R = 288\Omega$. $I = \frac{V}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{120\mathrm{~V}}{288\Omega} = 0.417\mathrm{~A}$. The voltage across each bulb is $V_{1} = IR = 60\mathrm{~V}$.

$$
P = \frac {V ^ {2}}{R} = \frac {(6 0 \mathrm {V}) ^ {2}}{1 4 4 \Omega} = 2 5 \mathrm {W}
$$

(b) In parallel, the voltage across each bulb is $120\mathrm{V}$ and the power dissipated by each is $100\mathrm{W}$.

questions.questionText: 19.49. The equivalent resistance for resistors in parallel is $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}$. For resistors in series, $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R_1 + R_2$. Note that, for $n$ resistors $R$ in parallel, $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \frac{R}{n}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) A $40\Omega$ resistor in series with two $10\Omega$ resistors in parallel gives $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 40\Omega + \left(\frac{10\Omega}{2}\right) = 45\Omega$

(b) A $10\Omega$ resistor in series with five $40\Omega$ resistors in parallel gives $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 10\Omega + \left(\frac{40\Omega}{5}\right) = 18\Omega$

(c) Nine $40\Omega$ resistors in parallel give $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = \left(\frac{40\Omega}{9}\right) = 4.44\Omega$

questions.questionText:
19.50. Assume the unknown currents have the directions shown in the figure below. We have used the junction rule to write the current through the $10.0\mathrm{V}$ battery as $I_{1} + I_{2}$. There are two unknowns, $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$, so we will need two equations. Three possible circuit loops are shown in the figure.

![img-11.jpeg](img-11.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Apply the loop rule to loop (1), going around the loop in the direction shown: $+10.0 \, \text{V} - (30.0 \, \Omega) I_1 = 0$ and $I_1 = 0.333 \, \text{A}$.



(b) Apply the loop rule to loop (3):  $+10.0\mathrm{V} - (20.0\Omega)I_2 - 5.00\mathrm{V} = 0$  and  $I_{2} = 0.250\mathrm{A}$
(c)  $I_{1} + I_{2} = 0.333\mathrm{A} + 0.250\mathrm{A} = 0.583\mathrm{A}$

Reflect: For loop (2) we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} + 5. 0 0 \mathrm {V} + I _ {2} (2 0. 0 \Omega) - I _ {1} (3 0. 0 \Omega) = 5. 0 0 \mathrm {V} + (0. 2 5 0 \mathrm {A}) (2 0. 0 \Omega) - (0. 3 3 3 \mathrm {A}) (3 0. 0 \Omega) = \\ 5. 0 0 \mathrm {V} + 5. 0 0 \mathrm {V} - 1 0. 0 \mathrm {V} = 0, \\ \end{array}
$$

so that with the currents we have calculated the loop rule is satisfied for this third loop.

questions.questionText:
19.51. The circuit diagram is given in the figure below. The junction rule has been used to find the magnitude and direction of the current in the middle branch of the circuit. There are no remaining unknown currents.

![img-12.jpeg](img-12.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The loop rule applied to loop (1) gives:

$$
\begin{array}{l} + 2 0. 0 \mathrm {V} - (1. 0 0 \mathrm {A}) (1. 0 0 \Omega) + (1. 0 0 \mathrm {A}) (4. 0 0 \Omega) + (1. 0 0 \mathrm {A}) (1. 0 0 \Omega) - \mathcal {E} _ {1} - (1. 0 0 \mathrm {A}) (6. 0 0 \Omega) = 0 \\ \mathcal {E} _ {1} = 2 0. 0 \mathrm {V} - 1. 0 0 \mathrm {V} + 4. 0 0 \mathrm {V} + 1. 0 0 \mathrm {V} - 6. 0 0 \mathrm {V} = 1 8. 0 \mathrm {V}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The loop rule applied to loop (2) gives:

$$
\begin{array}{l} + 2 0. 0 \mathrm {V} - (1. 0 0 \mathrm {A}) (1. 0 0 \Omega) - (2. 0 0 \mathrm {A}) (1. 0 0 \Omega) - \mathcal {E} _ {2} - (2. 0 0 \mathrm {A}) (2. 0 0 \Omega) - (1. 0 0 \mathrm {A}) (6. 0 0 \Omega) = 0 \\ \mathcal {E} _ {2} = 2 0. 0 \mathrm {V} - 1. 0 0 \mathrm {V} - 2. 0 0 \mathrm {V} - 4. 0 0 \mathrm {V} - 6. 0 0 \mathrm {V} = 7. 0 \mathrm {V}. \\ \end{array}
$$

questions.questionText:
19.52. The circuit is sketched in the figure below. When we go around loop (1) in the direction shown there is a potential rise across the  $200.0\mathrm{V}$  battery, so there must be a drop across  $R$  and the current in  $R$  must be in the direction shown in the figure. A similar analysis of loops (2) and (3) tell us that currents  $I_{2}$  and  $I_{5}$  must be in the directions shown. The junction rule has been used to label the currents in all the other branches of the circuit.



![img-13.jpeg](img-13.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Apply the loop rule to loop (1):  $+200.0 \, \text{V} - I_1 R = 0$ .  $R = \frac{+200 \, \text{V}}{I_1} = \frac{+200 \, \text{V}}{10 \, \text{A}} = 20 \, \Omega$

(b) Loop (2):  $+160\mathrm{V} - I_2(40\Omega) = 0$ $I_{2} = \frac{160\mathrm{V}}{40\Omega} = 4.0\mathrm{A}$
(c) Loop (3):  $+160\mathrm{V} - I_3(20\Omega) = 0$ $I_{3} = \frac{160\mathrm{V}}{20\Omega} = 8.0\mathrm{A}$

The current through the  $160\mathrm{V}$  battery is  $I = I_{2} + I_{3} = 12\mathrm{A}$  so the power supplied by the battery is  $P = VI = (160\mathrm{V})(12\mathrm{A}) = 1920\mathrm{W}$ .

Reflect: The sum of potential changes around loop (4) is

$$
\begin{array}{l} + 2 0 0 \mathrm {V} - I _ {1} R + I _ {2} (4 0 \Omega) - I _ {3} (2 0 \Omega) \\ = 2 0 0 \mathrm {V} - (1 0 \mathrm {A}) (2 0 \Omega) + (4. 0 \mathrm {A}) (4 0 \Omega) - (8. 0 \mathrm {A}) (2 0 \Omega) \\ = 2 0 0 \mathrm {V} - 2 0 0 \mathrm {V} + 1 6 0 \mathrm {V} - 1 6 0 \mathrm {V} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

The loop rule is satisfied for loop (4) and this is a good check of our calculations.

questions.questionText:
19.53. The circuit diagram is given in the figure below. The junction rule has been used to find the magnitude and direction of the current in the upper branch of the circuit. There are no remaining unknown currents.



19-17

![img-14.jpeg](img-14.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The junction rule gives that the current in $R$ is 2.00 A to the left.

(b) The loop rule applied to loop (1) gives:

$$
-(2.00 \mathrm{A}) R + 28.0 \mathrm{V} - (6.00 \mathrm{A}) (3.00 \Omega) = 0.
$$

$$
R = \frac{28.0 \mathrm{V} - 18.0 \mathrm{V}}{2.00 \mathrm{A}} = 5.00 \Omega.
$$

(c) The loop rule applied to loop (2) gives:

$$
-(4.00 \mathrm{A}) (6.00 \Omega) + \varepsilon - (6.00 \mathrm{A}) (3.00 \Omega) = 0.
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 24.0 \mathrm{V} + 18.0 \mathrm{V} = 42.0 \mathrm{V}.
$$

questions.questionText:
19.54. We can use the power consumption in the $5.00\,\Omega$ resistor to find the current through it. The circuit, unknown currents, and a circuit loop are all shown in the figure below.

![img-15.jpeg](img-15.jpeg)

^{}[]
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $P = I^2 R$ so $I_1 = \sqrt{\frac{20.0\,\mathrm{W}}{5.00\,\Omega}} = 2.00\,\mathrm{A}$. The loop rule for loop (1) gives

$$
+15.0\,\mathrm{V} - I_2(7.00\,\Omega) - (2.00\,\mathrm{A})(5.00\,\Omega) = 0.
$$

$I_2 = \frac{15.0\,\mathrm{V} - 10.0\,\mathrm{V}}{7.00\,\Omega} = 0.714\,\mathrm{A}$. The ammeter reads $0.714\,\mathrm{A}$.

questions.questionText:
19.55. $P = I^2 R$ so the power consumption of the $6.0\,\Omega$ resistor allows us to calculate the current through it. Unknown currents $I_1$, $I_2$ and $I_3$ are shown in the figure below. The junction rule says that $I_1 = I_2 + I_3$. In the figure below the two $20.0\,\Omega$ resistors in parallel have been replaced by their equivalent ($10.0\,\Omega$).

![img-16.jpeg](img-16.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $P = I^2 R$ gives $I_1 = \sqrt{\frac{P}{R}} = \sqrt{\frac{24\,\mathrm{J/s}}{6.0\,\Omega}} = 2.0\,\mathrm{A}$. The loop rule applied to loop (1) gives:

$$
-(2.0\,\mathrm{A})(3.0\,\Omega) - (2.0\,\mathrm{A})(6.0\,\Omega) + 25\,\mathrm{V} - I_2(10.0\,\Omega + 19.0\,\Omega + 1.0\,\Omega) = 0.
$$

$$
I_2 = \frac{25\,\mathrm{V} - 18\,\mathrm{V}}{30.0\,\Omega} = 0.233\,\mathrm{A}.
$$

(b) $I_3 = I_1 - I_2 = 2.0\,\mathrm{A} - 0.233\,\mathrm{A} = 1.77\,\mathrm{A}$. The loop rule applied to loop (2) gives:

$$
-(2.0\,\mathrm{A})(3.0\,\Omega + 6.0\,\Omega) + 25\,\mathrm{V} - (1.77\,\mathrm{A})(17\,\Omega) - \mathcal{E} - (1.77\,\mathrm{A})(13\,\Omega) = 0.
$$

$\mathcal{E} = 25\,\mathrm{V} - 18\,\mathrm{V} - 53.1\,\mathrm{V} = -46.1\,\mathrm{V}$. The emf is $46.1\,\mathrm{V}$ and the polarity of the battery is opposite to what is shown in the figure in the problem; the $+$ terminal is adjacent to the $13\,\Omega$ resistor.

questions.questionText: 19.56. The time constant is $\tau = RC$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $C = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{2.00\,\mathrm{s}}{500.0\,\Omega} = 4.00 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{F} = 4.00\,\mathrm{mF}$

questions.questionText: 19.57. We know that $q = Q_0 e^{2t/\tau}$, where $\tau = RC$. The fractional charge remaining at any time is thus $\frac{q}{Q_0} = e^{2t/\tau}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The time constant for this circuit is  $\tau = RC = (1.5 \times 10^{5} \Omega)(6.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}) = 0.90 \mathrm{~s}$ . At  $t = 1.8 \mathrm{~s}$ , which is  $2 \tau$ , the fractional charge remaining is  $\frac{q}{Q_0} = e^{2t / \tau} = e^{-(1.8 \mathrm{~s}) / (0.90 \mathrm{~s})} = e^{-2} = 0.14$  or  $14\%$ .

Reflect: This result can be applied to any  $RC$  circuit: After two time constants,  $14\%$  of the initial charge remains on any capacitor discharging through a simple  $RC$  circuit.

questions.questionText: 19.58. The time constant is  $RC = (0.895 \times 10^{6} \, \Omega)(12.4 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{F}) = 11.1 \, \mathrm{s}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) At  $t = 0$  s:  $q = C\mathcal{E}(1 - e^{-t / RC}) = 0$ .

At  $t = 5$  s:  $q = C\mathcal{E}(1 - e^{-t / RC}) = (12.4\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{F})(60.0\mathrm{V})(1 - e^{-(5.0\mathrm{s}) / (11.1\mathrm{s})}) = 2.70\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{C}.$

At  $t = 10$  s:  $q = C\mathcal{E}(1 - e^{-t / RC}) = (12.4\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{F})(60.0\mathrm{V})(1 - e^{-(10.0\mathrm{s}) / (11.1\mathrm{s})}) = 4.42\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{C}.$

At  $t = 20$  s:  $q = C\mathcal{E}(1 - e^{-t / RC}) = (12.4\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{F})(60.0\mathrm{V})(1 - e^{-(20.0\mathrm{s}) / (11.1\mathrm{s})}) = 6.21\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{C}.$

At  $t = 100$  s:  $q = C\mathcal{E}(1 - e^{-t / RC}) = (12.4\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{F})(60.0\mathrm{V})(1 - e^{-(100\mathrm{s}) / (11.1\mathrm{s})}) = 7.44\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{C}.$

(b) The current at time  $t$  is given by:  $i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} e^{-t / RC}$ .

At  $t = 0$  s:  $i = \frac{60.0\mathrm{V}}{8.95\times 10^5\Omega} e^{-0 / 11.1} = 6.70\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{A}.$

At  $t = 5$  s:  $i = \frac{60.0\mathrm{V}}{8.95\times 10^5\Omega} e^{-5 / 11.1} = 4.27\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{A}.$

At  $t = 10$  s:  $i = \frac{60.0\mathrm{V}}{8.95\times 10^5\Omega} e^{-10 / 11.1} = 2.72\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{A}.$

At  $t = 20$  s:  $i = \frac{60.0\mathrm{V}}{8.95\times 10^5\Omega} e^{-20 / 11.1} = 1.11\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{A}.$

At  $t = 100$  s:  $i = \frac{60.0\mathrm{V}}{8.95\times 10^5\Omega} e^{-100 / 11.1} = 8.20\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{A}.$

(c) The graphs of  $q(t)$  and  $i(t)$  are given in Figures (a) and (b) below.

Reflect: The charge on the capacitor increases in time as the current decreases.

![img-17.jpeg](img-17.jpeg)
(a)

![img-18.jpeg](img-18.jpeg)
(b)

questions.questionText: 19.59. The loop rule gives $\mathcal{E} = iR + \frac{q}{C}$. Just after the circuit is completed, $q = 0$. After a long time the capacitor is fully charged and $i = 0$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $q = 0$ so the voltage across the capacitor is zero.

(b) $q = 0$ so $iR = \mathcal{E}$; the voltage drop across the resistor is $500\mathrm{V}$.

(c) $q = 0$

(d) $i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \frac{500\mathrm{V}}{4500\Omega} = 0.111\mathrm{A}$

(e) (a) $i = 0$ so $\frac{q}{C} = \mathcal{E}$ and the voltage across the capacitor is $500\mathrm{V}$.

(b) $i = 0$ so the voltage across the resistor is zero.

(c) $q = \mathcal{E}C = (500\mathrm{V})(6.00\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{F}) = 3.00\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{C} = 3.00\mathrm{mC}$.

(d) $i = 0$

questions.questionText: 19.60. The capacitor discharges exponentially through the voltmeter. Since the potential difference across the capacitor is directly proportional to the charge on the plates, the voltage across the plates decreases exponentially with the same time constant as the charge. The reading of the voltmeter obeys the equation $V = V_{0}e^{-t / RC}$, where $RC$ is the time constant.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Solving for $C$ and evaluating the result when $t = 4.00 \, \text{s}$ gives

$$
C = \frac {t}{R \ln \left(V / V _ {0}\right)} = \frac {4 . 0 0 \mathrm {s}}{\left(3 . 4 0 \times 1 0 ^ {6} \Omega\right) \ln \left(\frac {1 2 . 0 \mathrm {V}}{3 . 0 0 \mathrm {V}}\right)} = 8. 4 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {F}
$$

(b) $\tau = RC = (3.40\times 10^{6}\Omega)(8.49\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{F}) = 2.89\mathrm{s}$

Reflect: In most laboratory circuits, time constants are much shorter than this one.

questions.questionText: 19.61. Use Eq. 19.17, $q = Q_{\mathrm{final}}(1 - e^{-t / RC})$ with $Q = \mathcal{E}C$. The potential across the capacitor is $V(t) = q(t) / C$. The time constant is $\tau = RC = (10\mathrm{M}\Omega)(1.0\mu \mathrm{F}) = 10\mathrm{s}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) For $t = 1.0 \, \text{s}, V(1.0 \, \text{s}) = \mathcal{E}\left(1 - e^{-(1.0 \, \text{s}) / RC}\right) = (9.0 \, \text{V})\left(1 - e^{-(1.0 \, \text{s}) / (10 \, \text{s})}\right) = 0.86 \, \text{V}$

(b) For $t = 5.0 \, \text{s}, V(5.0 \, \text{s}) = \mathcal{E}\left(1 - e^{-(5.0 \, \text{s}) / RC}\right) = (9.0 \, \text{V})\left(1 - e^{-(5.0 \, \text{s}) / (10 \, \text{s})}\right) = 3.5 \, \text{V}$

(c) For $t = 20 \, \text{s}, V(20 \, \text{s}) = \mathcal{E}\left(1 - e^{-(20 \, \text{s}) / RC}\right) = (9.0 \, \text{V})\left(1 - e^{-(20 \, \text{s}) / (10 \, \text{s})}\right) = 7.8 \, \text{V}$

Reflect: As time progresses, the voltage across the capacitor approaches that of the battery.

questions.questionText: 19.62. $q = C / V$. After a long time the current has dropped to zero, there is no potential drop across the resistor and the full battery voltage is applied to the capacitor network. Just after the switch is closed the charge and voltage across each capacitor is zero and the battery voltage equals the voltage drop across the resistor. The time constant is $\tau = RC$, where $C$ is the equivalent capacitance of the network. $i = I_0 e^{-t / RC}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The $20.0\mathrm{pF}$, $30.0\mathrm{pF}$, and $40.0\mathrm{pF}$ capacitors are in series and their equivalent capacitance $C_{\mathrm{s}}$ is given by

$$
\frac {1}{C _ {\mathrm {s}}} = \frac {1}{2 0 . 0 \mathrm {p F}} + \frac {1}{3 0 . 0 \mathrm {p F}} + \frac {1}{4 0 . 0 \mathrm {p F}}.
$$



$C_{\mathrm{x}} = 9.23 \mu \mathrm{F}$. After a long time the voltage across the $10.0 \mathrm{pF}$ capacitor is $50.0 \mathrm{~V}$. The charge on this capacitor is $q_{10} = C_{10} V_{10} = (10.0 \mathrm{pF})(50.0 \mathrm{~V}) = 500 \mathrm{pC}$. The voltage across $C_{\mathrm{x}}$ is $50.0 \mathrm{~V}$ and

$$
q _ {5} = C _ {5} V _ {5} = (9.23 \mathrm{pF})(50.0 \mathrm{~V}) = 461 \mathrm{pC}.
$$

This is the charge on each of the capacitors in series, so $q_{20} = q_{30} = q_{40} = 461$ pC.

(b) $V_{10} = 50.0 \mathrm{~V}$. $V_{20} = \frac{q_{20}}{C_{20}} = \frac{461 \mathrm{pC}}{20.0 \mathrm{pF}} = 23.1 \mathrm{~V}$. $V_{30} = \frac{q_{30}}{C_{30}} = \frac{461 \mathrm{pC}}{30.0 \mathrm{pF}} = 15.4 \mathrm{~V}$.

$$
V _ {4 0} = \frac {q _ {4 0}}{C _ {4 0}} = \frac {4 6 1 \mathrm{pC}}{4 0. 0 \mathrm{pF}} = 1 1. 5 \mathrm{~V}.
$$

Note that $V_{20} + V_{30} + V_{40} = 50.0 \mathrm{~V}$, as it should.

(c) $I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R} = \frac{50.0\mathrm{V}}{20.0\Omega} = 2.50\mathrm{A}$

(d) The $10.0\mathrm{pF}$ capacitor and $C_{\mathrm{x}}$ are in parallel so their equivalent is $C_{\mathrm{eq}} = 10.0\mathrm{pF} + 9.23\mathrm{pF} = 19.2\mathrm{pF}$. $\tau = RC = (20.0\Omega)(19.2\mathrm{pF}) = 384$ ps

Reflect: The charges on the capacitors start at zero and increase to their final values. The current starts at its maximum value and then decays to zero.

questions.questionText: 19.63. The time constant for the circuit is $\tau = RC$. While charging, $q = Q_{\mathrm{final}}(1 - e^{-t / RC})$ and $i = I_0e^{-t / RC}$. In the discharging circuit, both the charge and current decrease to $1 / e$ of their initial values in time $t = \tau$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\tau = RC = (125\Omega)(1.50\mu \mathrm{F}) = 188\mu \mathrm{s}$

(a) $q = Q_{\mathrm{final}}(1 - e^{-t / RC})$, $q = \frac{Q_{\mathrm{final}}}{e}$ gives $\frac{1}{e} = 1 - e^{-t / RC}$. $e^{-t / RC} = 1 - \frac{1}{e}$ and

$$
t = - R C (\ln [ 1 - 1 / e ]) = - (1 8 8 \mu \mathrm{s}) (- 0. 4 5 8) = + 8 6. 1 \mu \mathrm{s}
$$

(b) $i = I_0e^{-t / RC} = I_0e^{-(86.1\mu s) / (188\mu s)} = 0.633I_0$

(c) $t = \tau = 188\mu \mathrm{s}$

questions.questionText: 19.64. Equations (19.17) give $i$ and $q$ as functions of time during charging. During discharging, both $i$ and $q$ decay exponentially, as in Eq. (19.19) for $i$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The graphs are the same as in Figure 19.34a and 19.34b in the textbook and are given in Figure (a) below.

(b) The graphs of $i$ and $q$ versus $t$ for a discharging capacitor are sketched in Figure (b) below.

![img-21.jpeg](img-21.jpeg)

questions.questionText: 19.65. There is a single current path so the current is the same at all points in the circuit. Assume the current is counterclockwise.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Apply the loop rule, traveling around the circuit in the direction of the current:

$$
+ 16.0 \, \mathrm{V} - I(1.6 \, \Omega + 5.0 \, \Omega + 1.4 \, \Omega + 9.0 \, \Omega) - 8.0 \, \mathrm{V} = 0.
$$

$$
I = \frac{16.0 \, \mathrm{V} - 8.0 \, \mathrm{V}}{17.0 \, \Omega} = 0.471 \, \mathrm{A}.
$$

Our calculated $I$ is positive so $I$ is counterclockwise, as we assumed.

(b) $V_{b} + 16.0 \, \mathrm{V} - I(1.6 \, \Omega) = V_{a}$. $V_{ab} = 16.0 \, \mathrm{V} - (0.471 \, \mathrm{A})(1.6 \, \Omega) = 15.2 \, \mathrm{V}$

questions.questionText: 19.66. The ohmmeter reads the equivalent resistance between points $a$ and $b$. Replace series and parallel combinations by their equivalent. For resistors in parallel we have $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}$. For resistors in series we have $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R_1 + R_2$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Circuit (a): The $75.0 \, \Omega$ and $40.0 \, \Omega$ resistors are in parallel and have equivalent resistance $26.09 \, \Omega$. The $25.0 \, \Omega$ and $50.0 \, \Omega$ resistors are in parallel and have an equivalent resistance of $16.67 \, \Omega$. The equivalent network is given in Figure (a) below. $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{100.0 \, \Omega} + \frac{1}{23.05 \, \Omega}$, so $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 18.7 \, \Omega$.
