questions.questionText: 19.67. We know that the power used by the refrigerator is given by $P = VI$, and the corresponding energy used by the refrigerator when operated for a time $t$ is $E = Pt$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: When the refrigerator is operating the power used is $P = VI = (120\,\mathrm{V})(3.5\,\mathrm{A}) = 420\,\mathrm{W}$, which is $0.42\,\mathrm{kW}$. In a 30-day month there are (30 days) $(24\,\mathrm{h/day}) = 720\,\mathrm{h}$. Since the refrigerator operates $50\%$ of the time, the refrigerator will be in use for $360\,\mathrm{h}$ over the 30-day period. Thus, the amount of energy used during the month is $E = Pt = (0.42\,\mathrm{kW})(360\,\mathrm{h}) = 150\,\mathrm{kW}\cdot\mathrm{h}$. The total cost to run the refrigerator for the month is $(150\,\mathrm{kW}\cdot\mathrm{h})$ $(\$0.12/\mathrm{kW}\cdot\mathrm{h}) = \$18$.

Reflect: Since most household appliances operate at $120\,\mathrm{V}$, the cost of operation of a given appliance is roughly proportional to its current draw and the percent of time it is in use. For example, an appliance that uses $7.0\,\mathrm{A}$ of current and operates $5\%$ of the time during the month would cost roughly $\frac{7.0\,\mathrm{A}}{3.5\,\mathrm{A}} \times \frac{5\%}{50\%} \times \$18 = \$3.60$ per month to operate.

questions.questionText: 19.68. $R_{T} = R_{0}(1 + \alpha[T - T_{0}])$. $R = \frac{V}{I}$. $P = VI$. When the temperature increases the resistance increases and the current decreases.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{V}{I_T} = \frac{V}{I_0} (1 + \alpha[T - T_0])$. $I_0 = I_T (1 + \alpha[T - T_0])$.

$$
\begin{array}{l}
T - T _ {0} = \frac {I _ {0} - I _ {T}}{\alpha I _ {T}} = \frac {1 . 3 5 \mathrm {A} - 1 . 2 3 \mathrm {A}}{(1 . 2 3 \mathrm {A}) (4 . 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} (\mathrm {C} ^ {\circ}) ^ {- 1})} = 2 1 7 \mathrm {C} ^ {\circ}. \\
T = 2 0 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C} + 2 1 7 \mathrm {C} ^ {\circ} = 2 3 7 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C} \\
\end{array}
$$

(b) (i) $P = VI = (120\,\mathrm{V})(1.35\,\mathrm{A}) = 162\,\mathrm{W}$

(ii) $P = (120\,\mathrm{V})(1.23\,\mathrm{A}) = 148\,\mathrm{W}$

questions.questionText: 19.69. $R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$  so each piece has resistance  $R / 3$ . For resistors in parallel,  $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:  $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = 3\left(\frac{1}{R / 3}\right) = \frac{9}{R}$ .  $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R / 9$ . This can also be obtained as decreasing  $L$  by  $\frac{1}{3}$  and increasing  $A$  by 3, for an overall decrease of  $\frac{1}{9}$ .

questions.questionText:
19.70. The circuit is sketched in the figure below.  $r_{\mathrm{total}}$  is the combined internal resistance of both batteries. The power delivered to the bulb is  $I^2 R$ . Energy = Pt.

![img-30.jpeg](img-30.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $r_{\mathrm{total}} = 0$ . The loop rule gives

$$
1. 5 \mathrm {V} + 1. 5 \mathrm {V} - I (1 7 \Omega) = 0. I = 0. 1 7 6 5 \mathrm {A}.
$$

$$
P = I ^ {2} R = (0. 1 7 6 5 \mathrm {A}) ^ {2} (1 7 \Omega) = 0. 5 3 0 \mathrm {W}.
$$

This is also  $(3.0\mathrm{V})(0.1765\mathrm{A})$

(b) Energy  $= (0.530\mathrm{W})(5.0\mathrm{h})(3600\mathrm{s / h}) = 9540\mathrm{J}$
(c)  $P = \frac{0.530\mathrm{W}}{2} = 0.265\mathrm{W}$ .  $P = I^2 R$  so  $I = \sqrt{\frac{P}{R}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.265\mathrm{W}}{17\Omega}} = 0.125\mathrm{A}$ .

The loop rule gives  $1.5\mathrm{V} + 1.5\mathrm{V} - IR - Ir_{\mathrm{total}}$ .  $r_{\mathrm{total}} = \frac{3.0\mathrm{V} - (0.125\mathrm{A})(17\Omega)}{0.125\mathrm{A}} = 7.0\Omega$ .

Reflect: When the power to the bulb has decreased to half its initial value, the total internal resistance of the two batteries is nearly half the resistance of the bulb. Compared to a single battery, using two identical batteries in series doubles the emf but also doubles the total internal resistance.

questions.questionText: 19.71. The power dissipated in a resistor is  $I^2 R$ . The power for an emf is  $\mathcal{E}I$ . When the current passes through an emf from the - to the + terminal, chemical energy is converted to electrical energy. When the current passes through the emf from the + to the - terminal, electrical energy is converted to chemical energy.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Assume the current in the circuit is counterclockwise. The loop rule gives:

$$
1 2. 0 \mathrm {V} - 8. 0 \mathrm {V} - I (1. 0 \Omega + 8. 0 \Omega + 1. 0 \Omega) = 0.
$$

$$
I = 0. 4 0 \mathrm {A}.
$$

(b) The power dissipated in all three resistors is

$$
P = I ^ {2} R + I ^ {2} r _ {1} + I ^ {2} r _ {2} = I ^ {2} \left(R + r _ {1} + r _ {2}\right) = (0. 4 0 \mathrm {A}) ^ {2} (8. 0 \Omega + 1. 0 \Omega + 1. 0 \Omega) = 1. 6 \mathrm {W}
$$

(c) The total power delivered by the batteries is  $P = \mathcal{E}_1I + (-\mathcal{E}_2)I = I(\mathcal{E}_1 - \mathcal{E}_2) = (0.40\mathrm{A})(12.0\mathrm{V} - 8.0\mathrm{V}) = 1.6\mathrm{W}$ .

Reflect: In part (c), the power dissipated by  $\mathcal{E}_2$  is negative because the current runs from the low-potential lead to the high-potential lead.

questions.questionText: 19.72. Electrical energy is deposited in his body at the rate  $P = I^2 R$ . Heat energy  $Q$  produces a temperature change  $\Delta T$  according to  $Q = mc\Delta T$ , where  $c = 4190 \, \mathrm{J/kg} \cdot \mathrm{C}^\circ$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $P = I^2 R = (25,000\mathrm{A})^2 (1.0\mathrm{k}\Omega) = 6.25\times 10^{11}\mathrm{W}$ . The energy deposited is

$$
P t = (6. 1 5 \times 1 0 ^ {1 1} \mathrm {W}) (4 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {s}) = 2. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {7} \mathrm {J}.
$$



Find $\Delta T$ when $Q = 2.5 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~J}$.

$$
\Delta T = \frac{Q}{mc} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~J}}{(75 \mathrm{~kg})(4190 \mathrm{~J/kg} \cdot \mathrm{C}^{\circ})} = 80 \mathrm{C}^{\circ}
$$

(b) An increase of only $63~\mathrm{C}^{\circ}$ brings the water in the body to the boiling point; part of the person's body will be vaporized.

Reflect: Even this approximate calculation shows that being hit by lightning is very dangerous.

questions.questionText:
19.73. The network is sketched in the figure below. If the current in $R_{1}$ is $I$, it is $I/2$ for $R_{2}$ and $R_{3}$. $P = I^{2}R$. Since the current is greatest in $R_{1}$, that resistor dissipates the most power.

![img-31.jpeg](img-31.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $P_{1} = 32.0 \mathrm{~W}$ so $I = \sqrt{\frac{P}{R}} = \sqrt{\frac{32.0 \mathrm{~W}}{2.00 \Omega}} = 4.00 \mathrm{~A}$. The total power dissipated therefore is

$$
(4.00 \mathrm{A})^{2}(2.00 \Omega) + (2.00 \mathrm{A})^{2}(2.00 \Omega) + (2.00 \mathrm{A})^{2}(2.00 \Omega) = 48.0 \mathrm{W}.
$$

Reflect: $R_{1}$ is dissipating its maximum of $32.0\mathrm{W}$ but $R_{2}$ and $R_{3}$ are dissipating only $8.0\mathrm{W}$ each.

questions.questionText: 19.74. Since they are in parallel, the voltage across each device is $120\mathrm{V}$. $P = VI$. The total current drawn from the outlet is the sum of the individual currents.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Toaster: $I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{1800\mathrm{W}}{120\mathrm{V}} = 15.0\mathrm{A}$. Frypan: $I = \frac{1400\mathrm{W}}{120\mathrm{V}} = 11.7\mathrm{A}$. Lamp: $I = \frac{75\mathrm{W}}{120\mathrm{V}} = 0.625\mathrm{A}$.

(b) $I = 15.0\mathrm{A} + 11.7\mathrm{A} + 0.625\mathrm{A} = 27.3\mathrm{A}$. This is greater than $20\mathrm{A}$ and the circuit breaker will blow.

questions.questionText:
19.75. The resistance of each half of a wire is $0.500\Omega$. The combination can be represented by the resistor network shown in the figure below.

![img-32.jpeg](img-32.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: The two resistors in parallel have an equivalent resistance of $0.250\Omega$. Then the three in series have an equivalent resistance of $0.500\Omega + 0.250\Omega + 0.500\Omega = 1.25\Omega$.

questions.questionText: 19.76. Let $R$ be the resistance of each resistor. $P_{\mathrm{tot}} = \frac{V^2}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: When the resistors are in series, $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = 3R$ and $P_{\mathrm{s}} = \frac{V^2}{3R}$. When the resistors are in parallel, $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R/3$.

$$
P_{\mathrm{p}} = \frac{V^2}{R/3} = 3 \frac{V^2}{R} = 9 P_{\mathrm{s}} = 9 (27 \mathrm{W}) = 243 \mathrm{W}.
$$



Reflect: In parallel, the voltage across each resistor is the full applied voltage $V$. In series, the voltage across each resistor is $V/3$ and each resistor dissipates less power.

questions.questionText: 19.77. For resistors in series, the currents are the same and the voltages add. $R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R_1 + R_2 + \dots$ For resistors in parallel, the currents add and the voltages are the same. $\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \dots$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Replacing series and parallel combinations of resistors by their equivalents gives the equivalent networks as shown in the figure below. The equivalent resistance of the network is $8.0\Omega$.

![img-33.jpeg](img-33.jpeg)
(a)

![img-34.jpeg](img-34.jpeg)
(b)

![img-35.jpeg](img-35.jpeg)
(c)

![img-36.jpeg](img-36.jpeg)
(d)

(b) The voltage across the $8.0\Omega$ resistor in Figure (a) above is $(2.4\mathrm{A})(8.0\Omega) = 19.2\mathrm{V}$. This is the voltage across the $4.0\Omega$ resistor in Figure (b) above. The current through the $4.0\Omega$ resistor is $4.8\mathrm{A}$. This is also the current through the $20.0\Omega$ resistor and the voltage across that resistor is $96.0\mathrm{V}$. Therefore, the voltage between points $x$ and $y$ is $19.2\mathrm{V} + 96.0\mathrm{V} = 115.2\mathrm{V}$. This voltage is divided equally between the $6.0\Omega$ resistors in Figure (b), so the voltage between points $x$ and $y$ is $(115.2\mathrm{V}) / 2 = 57.6\mathrm{V}$. The voltmeter will read $57.6\mathrm{V}$.

questions.questionText: 19.78. The current delivered by the power plant can be calculated from its power and the known voltage: $I = \frac{P}{V}$. The power dissipated by the wires can be calculated from the current and resistance of the wires: $P = I^2 R$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) With a voltage of $220\mathrm{V}$ the current is $I = \frac{150\times 10^{3}\mathrm{W}}{220\mathrm{V}} = 681.8\mathrm{A}$. With this current the power dissipated by the wire is $P = I^2 R = (681.8\mathrm{A})^2 (0.25\Omega) = 116\times 10^3\mathrm{W}$. Thus, the percentage of the total power dissipated by the wire is $\frac{116\mathrm{kW}}{150\mathrm{kW}}\times 100\% = 77\%$.

(b) We can repeat the steps above—or notice that $22\mathrm{kV}$ is 100 times the previous voltage. Since current is inversely proportional to voltage and the power dissipated is proportional to current squared the power dissipated will now be $\left(\frac{1}{100}\right)^2\times 77\% = 0.0077\%$.

Reflect: Power is most efficiently transferred at relatively low current and high voltage.

questions.questionText:
19.79. Because of the polarity of each emf, the current in the  $7.00\Omega$  resistor must be in the direction shown in the figure below. Let  $I$  be the current in the  $24.0\mathrm{V}$  battery.

![img-37.jpeg](img-37.jpeg)
(a)

![img-38.jpeg](img-38.jpeg)
(b)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The loop rule applied to loop (1) gives:  $+24.0\mathrm{V} - (1.80\mathrm{A})(7.00\Omega) - I(3.00\Omega) = 0$ .  $I = 3.80\mathrm{A}$ . The junction rule then says that the current in the middle branch is  $2.00\mathrm{A}$ , as shown in Figure (b) above. The loop rule applied to loop (2) gives:

$$
+ \mathcal {E} - (1. 8 0 \mathrm {A}) (7. 0 0 \Omega) + (2. 0 0 \mathrm {A}) (2. 0 0 \Omega) = 0 \text {a n d} \mathcal {E} = 8. 6 \mathrm {V}.
$$

questions.questionText:
19.80. There is no current in the middle branch because there is not a complete conducting path for that branch. There is only a single current in the circuit, as shown in the figure below. To find  $V_{ab} = V_{a} - V_{b}$ , start at point  $b$  and travel to point  $a$ .

![img-39.jpeg](img-39.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $12.0\mathrm{V} - 8.0\mathrm{V} - I(2.00\Omega +2.00\Omega +1.00\Omega +2.00\Omega +1.00\Omega +1.00\Omega) = 0$ $I = 0.444\mathrm{A}$ . Going from  $a$  to  $b$  through the  $12.0\mathrm{V}$  battery gives

$$
V _ {a} + I (2. 0 0 \Omega) + I (1. 0 0 \Omega) - 1 2. 0 \mathrm {V} + I (1. 0 0 \Omega) + 1 0. 0 \mathrm {V} = V _ {b}.
$$

$$
V _ {a} - V _ {b} = 1 2. 0 \mathrm {V} - 1 0. 0 \mathrm {V} - (0. 4 4 4 \mathrm {A}) (4. 0 0 \Omega) = + 0. 2 2 \mathrm {V}.
$$

Or, going from  $a$  to  $b$  through the  $8.0\mathrm{V}$  battery gives

$$
V _ {a} - I (2. 0 0 \Omega) - I (1. 0 0 \Omega) - 8. 0 \mathrm {V} - I (2. 0 0 \Omega) + 1 0. 0 \mathrm {V} = V _ {b} \text {a n d}
$$

$$
V _ {a} - V _ {b} = - 2. 0 \mathrm {V} + (0. 4 4 4 \mathrm {A}) (5. 0 0 \Omega) = + 0. 2 2 \mathrm {V}.
$$



The voltmeter reads $0.22\mathrm{V}$. $V_{a} - V_{b}$ is positive, so point $a$ is at a higher potential.

**Reflect:** Since there is no current in the middle branch, the two resistances in that branch could be removed without affecting the potential difference $V_{ab}$. The $10.0\mathrm{V}$ emf doesn't affect the current but does affect $V_{ab}$

questions.questionText: 19.81. $iR = \frac{q}{C}$. The charge is reduced to $1/e$ of its maximum value when $t = \tau = RC$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) At $t = 0$, $q = iRC = (0.250\mathrm{A})(4600\Omega)(0.800\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{F}) = 9.20\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{C} = 0.920\mu \mathrm{C}$.

(b) $t = \tau = (4600\Omega)(0.800\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{F}) = 3.68\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{s} = 3.68\mu \mathrm{s}.$

questions.questionText: 19.82. $\mathcal{E} = iR + \frac{q}{C}$. At $t = 0$, $q = 0$. $\tau = RC$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) At $t = 0$, $\mathcal{E} = iR$ and $R = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{i} = \frac{400.0\mathrm{V}}{0.800\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{A}} = 5.00\times 10^{5}\Omega$.

(b) $\tau = RC$ so $C = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{6.00\mathrm{s}}{5.00\times 10^{5}\Omega} = 1.20\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{F}$.

questions.questionText: 19.83. As the resistance $R$ varies, the current in the circuit also varies, which causes the potential drop across the internal resistance of the battery to vary. The largest current will occur when $R = 0$, and the smallest current will occur when $R \to \infty$. The largest terminal voltage will occur when the current is zero ($R \to \infty$) and the smallest terminal voltage will be when the current is a maximum ($R = 0$).
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) As $R \to \infty$, $I \to 0$, so $V_{ab} \to \mathcal{E} = 15.0\mathrm{V}$, which is the largest reading of the voltmeter. When $R = 0$, the current is largest at $(15.0\mathrm{V}) / (4.00\Omega) = 3.75\mathrm{A}$, so the smallest terminal voltage is $V_{ab} = \mathcal{E} - rI = 15.0\mathrm{V} - (4.00\Omega)(3.75\mathrm{A}) = 0$.

(b) From part (a), the maximum current is $3.75\mathrm{A}$ when $R = 0$, and the minimum current is $0.00\mathrm{A}$ when $R \to \infty$.

(c) The graphs are sketched in the figure below.

**Reflect:** Increasing the resistance $R$ increases the terminal voltage, but at the same time it decreases the current in the circuit.

![img-40.jpeg](img-40.jpeg)

![img-41.jpeg](img-41.jpeg)

# Solutions to Passage Problems

questions.questionText: 19.84. We know that resistance can be calculated from the dimensions of the conductor according to $R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$. Only the outer sheath of the thread conducts.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: As the thread stretches, the outer sheath must become thinner. This will increase its resistance. As the thread returns to its original length, the sheath will return to nearly its original thickness, so the resistance will decrease from its maximum value. The correct answer is C.

**Reflect:** Answer A is incorrect because our thread is stretched, so it must become thinner as it gets longer. Answer B is incorrect because, if the thread cracks as it is elongated, it will become permanently damaged and its resistance will not recover when it regains its original length. Answer D is incorrect because the thread is uniform.

questions.questionText: 19.85. Resistance is given by $ R = \rho \frac{L}{A} $.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: The resistance for $ L_{1} = 5\mathrm{mm} $ is $ R_{1} = \rho \frac{L_{1}}{A_{1}} $ with $ R_{1} = 9\times 10^{9}\Omega $. The resistance for $ L_{2} = 13\mathrm{mm} $ is $ R_{2} = \rho \frac{L_{2}}{A_{2}} $ with $ R_{2} = 102\times 10^{9}\Omega $. Taking the ratio gives $ \frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{L_1}{A_1}\frac{A_2}{L_2} $ or $ \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{R_1L_2}{R_2L_1} = \frac{(9\times 10^9\Omega)(13\mathrm{mm})}{(102\times 10^9\Omega)(5\mathrm{mm})} = 0.23 = \frac{1}{4} $. The correct answer is B.

questions.questionText: 19.86. Use Ohm's law, $ V = IR $. We are given that $ V = 9\mathrm{V} $. The maximum current will flow when resistance is minimal.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: The minimum resistance is $ R = 9 \times 10^{9} \Omega $. The current is $ I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{9 \mathrm{~V}}{9 \times 10^{9} \Omega} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~A} = 1 \mathrm{nA} $. The correct answer is D.

questions.questionText: 19.87. If the thread were charged, it would be attracted to one charge and repulsed by the opposite charge. Thus, the thread is neutral.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The correct answer is D.

Reflect: A neutral conductor becomes polarized when subject to an external electric field.

questions.questionText: 19.88. The resistivity $ \rho $ is defined by $ R = \rho \frac{L}{A} $, where $ R = 1 \times 10^{11} \Omega $ is the resistance. We are given that $ L = 12 \mathrm{~nm} $ and $ r = 0.3 \mathrm{~nm} $.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The resistivity $ \rho = R \frac{A}{L} = R \frac{\pi r^2}{L} = \left(1 \times 10^{11} \Omega\right) \frac{\pi \left(0.3 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}\right)^2}{12 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}} = 2 \Omega \cdot \mathrm{m} $. The correct answer is C.

Reflect: The resistance is very high because the cross-sectional area is very small.

questions.questionText: 19.89. Consider the ion channels as resistors connected in parallel across the membrane. The voltage across each resistor is $ V = 50\mathrm{mV} $, and the resistance of each resistor (from the previous problem) is $ R = 1\times 10^{11}\Omega $. The current density $ J = 5\mathrm{mA / cm^2} $ is the sum of the current through each resistor per unit area, or $ J = \frac{NI}{A} $, where $ I = \frac{V}{R} $ is the current through a single resistor and $ N $ is the number of resistors in an area $ A $. $ N / A $ is therefore the number density of the resistors (i.e., of open ion channels).
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: Solving for $ N / A $ gives $ \frac{N}{A} = \frac{J}{I} = \frac{JR}{V} = \frac{(5\mathrm{mA / cm^2})(1\times 10^{11}\Omega)}{50\mathrm{mV}}\left(\frac{1\mathrm{cm}}{10^4\mu\mathrm{m}}\right)^2 = 100\mu \mathrm{m}^{-2} $. The correct answer is D.

questions.questionText: 19.90. Treat the membrane as an $ RC $ circuit. Its time constant is $ \tau = RC $. Because we are given the capacitance per unit area $ C_{A} = 1\mu \mathrm{F / cm}^{2} $, we must multiply the resistance by the area per resistance, which from the previous problem is $ R_{A} = \frac{R}{N / A} = \frac{1\times 10^{11}\Omega}{1\times 10^{10}\mathrm{cm}^{-2}} = 10\Omega \cdot \mathrm{cm}^2 $.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The time constant is $ \tau = RC = R_{A}C_{A} = \left(10\Omega \cdot \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)\left(1\mu \mathrm{F / cm}^{2}\right) = 10\mu \mathrm{s} $. The correct answer is B.

Reflect: Dimensional analysis is useful in solving this problem.

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q1: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 1. Equation 20.1, $F = \left|q\right|vB\sin \phi$, gives the magnitude of the force exerted on an electron in a magnetic field $B$. Zero force will be exerted on the electron if its velocity is parallel to the magnetic field (i.e., $\phi = 0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$). Thus, an electron can move in a straight line in a magnetic field provided it moves parallel or antiparallel to the magnetic field. Therefore, we cannot be sure that the magnetic field in the cathode ray tube is zero just because an electron moves in a straight line.

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q2: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 2. The magnetic field is tangent at all points to the magnetic field lines, but the force exerted on a moving charge by the magnetic field is perpendicular to both the local magnetic field and to the velocity of the charge. Thus, calling the magnetic field lines "magnetic lines of force" would be misleading because it implies that the force exerted by the magnetic field is parallel to these lines, when in reality the force is perpendicular to these lines.

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q3: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 3. If a force does no work on a charged particle, it cannot change the kinetic energy of the particle, which amounts to saying that the force cannot change the magnitude of the velocity. However, the force can change the direction of the particle's velocity, as occurs in the case of centripetal acceleration. Examples of this include the force exerted by the sun on the planets, which keep the planets in orbit, or the force exerted on a car to accelerate the car around a turn.

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q4: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 4. Some materials contain atoms or molecules that have small current loops, or magnetic dipoles. In zero external magnetic field, these magnetic dipoles can be randomly ordered or can be ordered within magnetic domains which themselves are randomly ordered. The net result, in zero external magnetic field, is that the material is nonmagnetized. However, once an external magnetic field is applied, the magnetic dipoles or domains may align somewhat with the external magnetic field, which magnetizes the material. The facility with which this happens is reflected in the relative permeability $K_{\mathrm{m}}$ of the material. Materials with large $K_{\mathrm{m}}$, such as ferric materials, can be strongly magnetized by an external magnetic field. Such materials are thus subject to magnetic forces just like permanent magnets. Nails, tacks, and paper clips all have relatively large permeabilities, so they are easily magnetize by an external magnet and are therefore subjected to magnetic forces, causing them to string together like so many permanent magnets. This is analogous to electrically neutral objects becoming electrically polarized in an electric field, which allows the electric field to exert a force on them even though they are electrically neutral.

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q5: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 5. A stream of charged particles ejected from the sun constitutes an electric current, which creates a magnetic field, analogous to the magnetic field created by electric current through a wire (see Eq. 20.10). This magnetic field is superimposed on and perturbs the magnetic field of earth, thereby causing a "magnetic storm."

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q6: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
6. As shown in Fig. 20.3, breaking a magnetic in half only produces two magnets, each with a N and S pole. It does not produce an object with two N poles or two S poles. Fundamentally, this occurs because the magnetic field of a magnetic is due to the superposition of the magnetic fields coming from many little current loops (i.e., magnetic dipoles) in the material, as shown in the figure below. When the macroscopic magnet is broken in two, the magnetic dipoles produce two new magnets, each with N and S poles.

![img-42.jpeg](img-42.jpeg)

![img-43.jpeg](img-43.jpeg)

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q7: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 7. The force exerted by a magnetic field on a moving charged particle is perpendicular to the magnetic field, so the acceleration of the charged particle is also perpendicular to the magnetic field. However, the velocity (and therefore the trajectory) need not be perpendicular to the magnetic field.

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q8: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 8. To be considered infinite, the length of the wire must be much, much greater than the perpendicular distance $r$ from the wire to the point at which you are calculating the magnetic field. In addition, the measurement point must be a distance $d \gg r$ from the ends of the straight section of the wire.

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q9: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
9. Consider the figure below showing two wires with current $I$ flowing to the right in each. The charge carriers in each wire experience a force due to the magnetic field generated by the current in the opposite wire. This force deviates the trajectory of these charges toward the opposite wire, but the charges are stopped at the wire boundary by electrostatic forces exerted by the atoms in the lattice of the wires. By Newton's third law, the charges exert a force of equal magnitude in the opposite direction, and it is this force that does the work to accelerate the wire toward each other. The energy to do this work comes from the emf that drives the current.

Note that the charges must continue to move forward, on average, so that the magnetic force continues to act on them in a direction perpendicular to their velocity (if the charges did not move forward, the current would go to zero).



![img-44.jpeg](img-44.jpeg)

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q10: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 10. Household wiring consists of pairs of wire carrying opposite currents. The magnetic field due to these currents cancel each other, giving zero field unless you are very close to the wires (where “close” means closer than a wire diameter).

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q11: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 11. Equation 20.1, $F = |q| \upsilon B \sin \phi$, gives the magnitude of the force exerted on an electron in a magnetic field $B$. Thus, a charged particle can move through a magnetic field and experience zero force if its trajectory is parallel or antiparallel to the magnetic field (i.e., $\phi = 0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$). The force exerted by an electric field does not depend on the angle between the field and the trajectory of the charged particle, nor on whether the particle is moving, so any charged particle in an electric field will experience a force.

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q12: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 12. The magnetic field due to a long, straight current-carrying wire is given by Eq. 20.10, $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$. The magnitude of the magnetic field is inversely proportional to the perpendicular distance $r$ from the wire. Thus, to increase the magnetic-field magnitude by a factor of three, you must decrease the perpendicular distance $r$ by a factor of three. Explicitly, you can use $B' = 3B = 3\left(\frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\right) = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi\left(\frac{r}{3}\right)}$ $\Rightarrow$ $r' = \frac{r}{3}$.

questions.questionText: Chapter 20 — Conceptual Q13: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
13. The magnetic field due to a long, straight current-carrying wire is given by Eq. 20.10, $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$. Given $I = 200 \mathrm{~A}$ and $B_{\max} = 1 \mathrm{mT}$, the closest a person with a pacemaker could stand from such a wire is

$$
B_{\max} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}
$$

$$
r = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi B_{\max}} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A})(200 \mathrm{A})}{2\pi(1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{T})} = 4 \mathrm{cm}
$$

questions.questionText:
20.1. The force $\vec{F}$ on the particle is in the direction of the deflection of the particle. The directions of $\vec{v}$, $\vec{B}$, and $\vec{F}$ are shown in the figure below. Apply the right-hand rule to the directions of $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$. See if your thumb is in the direction of $\vec{F}$ or opposite to that direction. Use $F = |q| \upsilon B \sin \phi$ with $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ to calculate $F$.



![img-45.jpeg](img-45.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) When you apply the right-hand rule to  $\vec{v}$  and  $\vec{B}$ , your thumb points east.  $\vec{F}$  is in this direction, so the charge is positive.

(b)  $F = |q|\upsilon B\sin \phi = (8.50\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{C})(4.75\times 10^{3}\mathrm{m / s})(1.25\mathrm{T})\sin 90^{\circ} = 0.0505\mathrm{N}$

questions.questionText:
20.2. The force  $\vec{F}$  on the ion is downward, in the direction of the deflection. The directions of  $\vec{v}$  and  $\vec{F}$  are shown in Figure (a) below. Use the right-hand rule to relate the directions of  $\vec{v}$ ,  $\vec{B}$ , and  $\vec{F}$ . The charge is positive. Use  $F = |q|\nu B\sin \phi$  with  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$  to calculate  $B$ .  $e = 1.60\times 10^{-19}\mathrm{C}$ .

![img-46.jpeg](img-46.jpeg)

![img-47.jpeg](img-47.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $\vec{B}$  is perpendicular to both  $\vec{v}$  and  $\vec{F}$ , so  $\vec{B}$  is either toward the top of the page or toward the bottom of the page. If  $\vec{B}$  is toward the top of the page, as shown in Figure (b) above, then when you apply the right-hand rule your thumb points into the page. This is the specified direction of  $\vec{F}$ , so  $\vec{B}$  is directed as shown in Figure (b).  $F = |q|\nu B\sin \phi$  gives

$$
B = \frac {F}{| q | \upsilon \sin \phi} = \frac {6 . 9 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 5} \mathrm {N}}{6 (1 . 6 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 9} \mathrm {C}) (8 . 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ {3} \mathrm {m / s}) \sin 9 0 ^ {\circ}} = 0. 8 5 0 \mathrm {T}
$$

questions.questionText:
20.3. The directions of  $\vec{v}$  and  $\vec{B}$  are shown in the figure below. A proton has charge  $e = +1.60 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{C}$ .  $F = |q| \nu B \sin \phi$ . In part (a),  $\phi = 55.0^{\circ}$ . An electron has charge  $-e$ .

![img-48.jpeg](img-48.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The right-hand rule says  $\vec{F}$  is into the page in the figure above.

$$
F = | q | \upsilon B \sin \phi = (1. 6 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 9} \mathrm {C}) (3. 6 0 \times 1 0 ^ {3} \mathrm {m / s}) (0. 7 5 0 \mathrm {T}) \sin 5 5. 0 ^ {\circ} = 3. 5 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 6} \mathrm {N}
$$

(b)  $F$  is maximum when  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ , when  $\vec{v}$  is perpendicular to  $\vec{B} F_{\mathrm{max}} = |q|\nu B = 4.32\times 10^{-16}\mathrm{N}$ .  $F$  is minimum when  $\phi = 0^{\circ}$  or  $180^{\circ}$ , when  $\vec{v}$  is either parallel or antiparallel to  $\vec{B} F_{\mathrm{min}} = 0$ .



(c) $|q|$ is the same for an electron and a proton, so $F = 3.45 \times 10^{-16} \ \mathrm{N}$, the same as for a proton. Since the proton and electron have charges of opposite sign, the forces on them are in opposite directions. The force on the electron is directed out of the page in the figure above.

**Reflect:** Only the component of $\vec{\pmb{v}}$ perpendicular to $\vec{B}$ contributes to the magnetic force. Therefore, this force is zero when the charged particle moves along the direction of $\vec{B}$ and the force is maximum when the particle moves in a direction perpendicular to $\vec{B}$. When the sign of the charge changes, the force reverses direction. And, even though the force magnitude is the same for the electron and proton, the effect of the force (the acceleration) for the electron would be much greater, because of its smaller mass.

questions.questionText:
20.4. The gravity force is downward so the force from the magnetic field must be upward. The charge's velocity and the forces are shown in the figure below. Since the charge is negative, the magnetic force is opposite to the right-hand rule direction. The minimum magnetic field is when the field is perpendicular to $\vec{\pmb{v}}$. The force is also perpendicular to $\vec{B}$, so $\vec{B}$ is either eastward or westward.

![img-49.jpeg](img-49.jpeg)

**
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:** If $\vec{B}$ is eastward, the right-hand rule direction is into the page and $\vec{F}_B$ is out of the page, as required. Therefore, $\vec{B}$ is eastward. $mg = |q|\upsilon B\sin \phi$. $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ and $B = \frac{mg}{\upsilon|q|} = \frac{(0.195\times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{kg})(9.80\ \mathrm{m/s}^2)}{(4.00\times 10^4\ \mathrm{m/s})(2.50\times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{C})} = 1.91\ \mathrm{T}$.

questions.questionText:
20.5. The charge is positive, so the magnetic force is in the right-hand rule direction. The figure below shows $\vec{\pmb{v}}$ and $\vec{B}$. $\vec{F} = m\vec{\pmb{a}}$ relates the force and acceleration.

![img-50.jpeg](img-50.jpeg)

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** (a) The right-hand rule applied to $\vec{\pmb{v}}$ and $\vec{B}$ gives that $\vec{F}_B$ is in the $+z$ direction.

$$
F_B = |q|\upsilon B\sin \phi = (3.50 \times 10^{-8} \ \mathrm{C})(2.00 \times 10^5\ \mathrm{m/s})(0.80\ \mathrm{T})\sin 90^{\circ} = 5.60 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{N}
$$

(b) $a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{5.60 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{N}}{5.00 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{kg}} = 1.12\ \mathrm{m/s}^2$. $\vec{\pmb{a}}$ is in the $+z$ direction.

**Reflect:** The force and acceleration are perpendicular to the plane formed by the directions of $\vec{\pmb{v}}$ and $\vec{B}$.

questions.questionText: 20.6. Use $F = |q|\upsilon B\sin \phi$ to relate the force $F$ to the angle $\phi$ between the direction of $\vec{\pmb{v}}$ and the direction of $\vec{B}$. Since $|q|$, $\upsilon$, and $B$ are unknown and constant, set up a ratio that involves just $F$ and $\phi$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{F}{\sin\phi} = |q|vB$, which is constant. Therefore, $\frac{F_1}{\sin\phi_1} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\phi_2}$.

$$
F_2 = F_1 \left(\frac{\sin\phi_2}{\sin\phi_1}\right) = F \left(\frac{\sin 30.0^\circ}{\sin 15.0^\circ}\right) = 1.93F
$$

questions.questionText: 20.7. Use $F = |q|vB_{\perp}$ to relate the force $F$ to the angle $\phi$ between the direction of $\vec{v}$ and the direction of $\vec{B}$. Use Newton’s second law, $\vec{F} = m\vec{u}$ to find the speed.
20.8. $F = |q|vB\sin\phi$. Since the particle has negative charge, the force is opposite to the right-hand rule (right-hand rule) direction.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The directions of $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ are shown in Figure (a) below. The angle $\phi$ is $130^\circ$.

$$
F = (2.50 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C})(40.0 \times 10^3 \text{ m/s})(2.00 \text{ T})\sin 130^\circ = 1.53 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}
$$

The right-hand-rule direction is in the $+z$ direction so the force is in the $-z$ direction.

![img-51.jpeg](img-51.jpeg)
(a)

![img-52.jpeg](img-52.jpeg)
(b)

(b) The directions of $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ are shown in Figure (b) above. $\phi = 90^\circ$

$$
F = |q|vB\sin\phi = (2.50 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C})(40.0 \times 10^3 \text{ m/s})(2.00 \text{ T})\sin 90^\circ = 2.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}
$$

The right-hand rule direction and the direction of $\vec{F}$ are also shown in Figure (b); $\vec{F}$ is $140^\circ$ counterclockwise from the $+x$ axis, so is $50^\circ$ counterclockwise from the $+y$ axis.

questions.questionText: 20.9. Take $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ as shown in the textbook. The direction of the magnetic force at each point is given by the right-hand rule. We may use $F = |q|vB\sin\phi$ to find the magnitude of the force and $a = F/m$ to find the acceleration of the charge. According to the figure $\phi = 45^{\circ}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: According to the right-hand rule, $\vec{F}$ is in the $-z$ direction. The acceleration of the charge is given by $a = |q|vB\sin\phi/m = (1.22 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C})(3.00 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~m/s})(1.25 \mathrm{T})\sin 45^{\circ}/(1.81 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}) = 1.79 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~m/s}^2$.

Reflect: For a given velocity and magnetic field strength, the maximum acceleration of the charge occurs when the magnetic field is perpendicular to the velocity of the charge. In this case, the maximum acceleration would be $0.253\mathrm{m/s}^2$. On the other hand, if the magnetic field were parallel to the velocity of the charge, the charge would have an acceleration of zero.

questions.questionText:
20.10. For the alpha particles to emerge from the plates undeflected, the magnetic force on them must exactly cancel the electric force. The battery produces an electric field between the plates, which acts on the alpha particles. First use energy conservation to find the speed of the alpha particles as they enter the plates: $qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$.

The electric field between the plates due to the battery is $E = V_{\mathrm{b}} / d$. For the alpha particles not to be deflected, the magnetic force must cancel the electric force, so $qvB = qE$, giving $B = E / v$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Solve for the speed of the alpha particles just as they enter the region between the plates. Their charge is $2e$.

$$
v_{a} = \sqrt{\frac{2(2e)V}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C})(1750 \mathrm{V})}{6.64 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{kg}}} = 4.11 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{m/s}
$$

The electric field between the plates, produced by the battery, is

$$
E = V_{\mathrm{b}} / d = (150 \mathrm{V}) / (0.00820 \mathrm{m}) = 18,300 \mathrm{V/m}
$$

The magnetic force must cancel the electric force:

$$
B = E / v_{a} = (18,300 \mathrm{V/m}) / (4.11 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{m/s}) = 0.0445 \mathrm{T}
$$

The magnetic field is perpendicular to the electric field. If the charges are moving to the right and the electric field points upward, the magnetic field is out of the page.

Reflect: The sign of the charge of the alpha particle does not enter the problem, so negative charges of the same magnitude would also not be deflected.

questions.questionText: 20.11. The relation between $v, B$, and $E$ is given by Eq. (20.3). $\vec{E}$ points from the positive plate to the negative plate, so is directed upward in the figure. The electric field is related to the potential difference $V$ between the plates by $V = Ed$, where $d$ is the separation between the plates.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $E = \frac{V}{d} = \frac{150\mathrm{V}}{4.50\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{m}} = 3.33\times 10^{1}\mathrm{V/m}$. $B = \frac{E}{v} = \frac{3.33\times 10^{3}\mathrm{V/m}}{3.25\times 10^{5}\mathrm{m/s}} = 1.02\mathrm{T}$.

(b) The forces and fields are shown in the figure below. Assume a positive charge; the same field directions also work for a negative charge. $\vec{E}$ is upward so the electric force $\vec{F}_{E}$ is upward. In order for $\vec{F}_{B}$ to be downward, to oppose $\vec{F}_{E}$, $\vec{B}$ must be out of the page, as shown.



![img-53.jpeg](img-53.jpeg)

Reflect: Ions with speed  $\upsilon = E / B$  are undeflected no matter what is the sign or magnitude of their charge. If  $\mathcal{V}$  is not equal to  $E / B$ , then either  $F_{B}$  or  $F_{E}$  is larger than the other, there is a net force on the charge and the charge does not travel in a straight line.

questions.questionText: 20.12. Apply  $F = |q| \upsilon B \sin \phi$ , where  $\phi$  is the angle between the direction of  $\vec{\nu}$  and the direction of the magnetic field,  $\vec{B}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The smallest magnitude force possible is zero, when the motion is parallel to the magnetic field  $(\phi = 0)$ . The greatest magnitude force is when the velocity and magnetic field are at right angles  $(\phi = 90^{\circ})$ :

$$
F _ {\max } = | q | \upsilon B \sin \phi = (1. 6 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 9} \mathrm {C}) (2. 5 0 \times 1 0 ^ {6} \mathrm {m / s}) (7. 4 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {T}) \sin 9 0 ^ {\circ} = 3. 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 4} \mathrm {N}
$$

(b) If  $F = \frac{1}{2} F_{\max}$  then  $\sin \phi = \frac{1}{2}$  and  $\phi = 30^{\circ}$ .

Reflect: The magnitude of the force decreases as  $\phi \rightarrow 0,180^{\circ}$

questions.questionText:
20.13. Eq. (20.4) says  $R = \frac{m\nu}{|q|B}$ . A proton has mass  $m = 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \, \mathrm{kg}$  and charge

$$
q = + e = 1. 6 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 9} \mathrm {C}.
$$
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:  $\upsilon = \frac{R|q|B}{m} = \frac{(0.0613\mathrm{m})(1.60\times 10^{-19}\mathrm{C})(0.250\mathrm{T})}{1.67\times 10^{-27}\mathrm{kg}} = 1.47\times 10^{6}\mathrm{m / s}$

questions.questionText:
20.14. Since the particle moves perpendicular to the uniform magnetic field, the radius of its path is  $R = \frac{m\nu}{|q|B}$ . The magnetic force is perpendicular to both  $\vec{\nu}$  and  $\vec{B}$ . The alpha particle has charge

$$
q = - 2 e = 3. 2 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 9} \mathrm {C}
$$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $R = \frac{(6.64 \times 10^{-27} \, \mathrm{kg})(35.6 \times 10^{3} \, \mathrm{m/s})}{(3.20 \times 10^{-19} \, \mathrm{C})(1.10 \, \mathrm{T})} = 6.73 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{m} = 0.673 \, \mathrm{mm}$

The alpha particle moves in a circular arc of diameter  $2R = 1.35\mathrm{mm}$

(b) For a very short time interval the displacement of the particle is in the direction of the velocity. The magnetic force is always perpendicular to this direction so it does no work. The work-energy theorem therefore says that the kinetic energy of the particle, and hence its speed, is constant.

(c) The acceleration is

$$
a = \frac {F _ {B}}{m} = \frac {| q | \upsilon B \sin \phi}{m} = \frac {(3 . 2 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 9} \mathrm {C}) (3 5 . 6 \times 1 0 ^ {3} \mathrm {m / s}) (1 . 1 0 \mathrm {T}) \sin 9 0 ^ {\circ}}{6 . 6 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2 7} \mathrm {k g}} = 1. 8 8 \times 1 0 ^ {1 2} \mathrm {m / s} ^ {2}
$$

We can also use  $a = \frac{\upsilon^2}{R}$  and the result of part (a) to calculate



$$
a = \frac{(35.6 \times 10^3 \ \mathrm{m/s})^2}{6.73 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{m}} = 1.88 \times 10^{12} \ \mathrm{m/s^2}
$$

which is the same result. The acceleration is perpendicular to $\vec{\pmb{v}}$ and $\vec{B}$ and so is horizontal, toward the center of curvature of the particle's path.

(d) The unbalanced force $(\vec{F}_B)$ is perpendicular to $\vec{\pmb{v}}$ so it changes the direction of $\vec{\pmb{v}}$ but not its magnitude, which is the speed.

questions.questionText: 20.15. A proton and a deuteron each have charge $+e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \ \mathrm{C}$, but they have different masses. The mass of a proton is $1.67 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg}$. The radius of the circular arc that the particle moves in is $R = \frac{mv}{|q|B}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $v = \frac{R|q|B}{m} = \frac{(0.556 \ \mathrm{m})(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \ \mathrm{C})(0.500 \ \mathrm{T})}{3.34 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg}} = 1.33 \times 10^7 \ \mathrm{m/s}$

The speed is not changed by the magnetic field so the deuteron has this speed both just before it enters the field and just after it leaves the field.

(b) Only the mass changes. $\frac{R}{m} = \frac{v}{|q|B} = \text{constant}$ and $\frac{R_1}{m_1} = \frac{R_2}{m_2}$.

$$
R_2 = R_1 \left(\frac{m_2}{m_1}\right) = (55.6 \ \mathrm{cm}) \left(\frac{1.67 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg}}{3.34 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg}}\right) = 27.7 \ \mathrm{cm}
$$

Reflect: The magnetic force does no work on the moving charge so the motion in a magnetic field is at constant speed. The radius of the path decreases as $m$ decreases.

questions.questionText: 20.16. The magnetic force on the beam bends it through a quarter circle. The distance that particles in the beam travel is $s = Rq$, and the radius of the quarter circle is $R = m\nu / qB$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Solving for $R$ gives $R = s/q = s / (\mathrm{p}/2) = (1.18 \ \mathrm{cm}) / (\pi/2) = 0.751 \ \mathrm{cm}$. Solving for the magnetic field gives

$$
B = \frac{mv}{qR} = \frac{(1.67 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg})(1200 \ \mathrm{m/s})}{(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \ \mathrm{C})(0.00751 \ \mathrm{m})} = 1.67 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{T}
$$

Reflect: This field is about 10 times stronger than the earth's magnetic field, but much weaker than many laboratory fields.

Solve: $RB = \frac{mv}{|q|} = \text{constant}$. $R_1B_1 = R_2B_2$ and $R_2 = R_1\left(\frac{B_1}{B_2}\right) = R\left(\frac{B_1}{3B_1}\right) = R/3$.

Reflect: The radius of the circular path decreases when $B$ increases.

questions.questionText:
20.18. For motion in an arc of a circle, $a = \frac{v^2}{R}$ and the net force is radially inward, toward the center of the circle. The direction of the force is shown in the figure below. Since the electron has negative charge, the force on it is opposite to the right-hand rule direction. The magnetic force does no work and the electron moves at constant speed. The distance from $A$ to $B$ along the path is $\pi R$, where $R = 5.00 \ \mathrm{cm}$. An electron has charge $q = -e = -1.60 \times 10^{-19} \ \mathrm{C}$ and mass $9.11 \times 10^{-31} \ \mathrm{kg}$. A proton has charge $q = +e$ and mass $1.67 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg}$.



![img-54.jpeg](img-54.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) To produce a force in the direction shown, the magnetic field must be directed into the page. $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ gives $|q|vB\sin \phi = m\frac{v^2}{R}$. $\phi = 90^\circ$ and

$$
B = \frac{mv}{|q|R} = \frac{(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \ \mathrm{kg})(1.41 \times 10^6 \ \mathrm{m/s})}{(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \ \mathrm{C})(5.00 \times 10^{-2} \ \mathrm{m})} = 1.61 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{T}
$$

(b) $t = \frac{\pi R}{v} = \frac{\pi(5.00 \times 10^{-2} \ \mathrm{m})}{1.41 \times 10^6 \ \mathrm{m/s}} = 1.11 \times 10^{-7} \ \mathrm{s}$

(c) Since the proton has positive charge, $\vec{B}$ must be directed out of the page to produce a force in the required direction.

$$
B = \frac{mv}{|q|R} = \frac{(1.67 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg})(1.41 \times 10^6 \ \mathrm{m/s})}{(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \ \mathrm{C})(5.00 \times 10^{-2} \ \mathrm{m})} = 0.294 \ \mathrm{T}
$$

questions.questionText:
20.19. In the acceleration process the electric potential energy decrease, $|q|V$, equals the increase in kinetic energy, $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$. Use this to find the speed $v$ of the protons after they have been accelerated. In the magnetic field,

$$
R = \frac{mv}{|q|B}
$$

Protons have charge $-e$ and mass $1.67 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg}$. Electrons have charge $-e$ and mass $9.11 \times 10^{-31} \ \mathrm{kg}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $|q|V = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. $v = \sqrt{\frac{2|q|V}{m}} = \frac{\sqrt{2(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \ \mathrm{C})(0.745 \times 10^3 \ \mathrm{V})}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg}} = 3.78 \times 10^3 \ \mathrm{m/s}$. $R = \frac{mv}{|q|B}$ and

$$
B = \frac{mv}{|q|R} = \frac{(1.67 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg})(3.78 \times 10^5 \ \mathrm{m/s})}{(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \ \mathrm{C})(0.875 \ \mathrm{m})} = 4.51 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{T}
$$

(b) $B = \frac{mv}{|q|R}$. In $\frac{mv}{|q|R}$ only $m$ changes. $\frac{B}{m} = \frac{v}{|q|R} = \text{constant}$ and $\frac{B_p}{m_p} = \frac{B_s}{m_s}$.

$$
B_s = \left(\frac{m_s}{m_p}\right) B_p = \left(\frac{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \ \mathrm{kg}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27} \ \mathrm{kg}}\right) (4.51 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{T}) = 2.46 \times 10^{-6} \ \mathrm{T}
$$

Reflect: For lighter particles a smaller force and hence smaller $B$ is needed to produce the same path. To produce electrons of the same speed as the protons a smaller accelerating voltage would be required.

questions.questionText: 20.20. The magnitude of the magnetic force on the bullet is given by $F = |q|vB\sin \phi$ and the acceleration of the bullet is given by $a = F/m$. We know that the magnetic field is perpendicular to the velocity of the bullet so that $\phi = 90^\circ$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $F = |q|vB\sin \phi = (1.65 \times 10^{-6} \ \mathrm{C})(425 \ \mathrm{m/s})(5.50 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{T})\sin 90^\circ = 3.86 \times 10^{-7} \ \mathrm{N}$

(b) Assuming that only the magnetic force acts on the bullet, we have $a = F/m = (3.86 \times 10^{-7} \ \mathrm{N}) / (3.25 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{kg}) = 1.19 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{m/s^2}$.

^{}[]

**Reflect:** The acceleration calculated in part (b) ignores the gravitational force acting on the bullet, which would produce an acceleration that is nearly $10^{5}$ times larger.

questions.questionText:
20.21. An electron has charge $q = -e = -1.60 \times 10^{-19} \, \mathrm{C}$ and mass $9.11 \times 10^{-31} \, \mathrm{kg}$. When it moves in a circular path of radius $R$, its acceleration is $\frac{\upsilon^2}{R}$. Combine $F = |q|\upsilon B_{\perp}$ with Newton’s second law $F = ma$ to find the speed, from which you can find the kinetic energy $K = \frac{1}{2} m\upsilon^2$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** $F = |q|\upsilon B_{\perp} = m\frac{\upsilon^2}{r}$, so $\upsilon = \frac{|q|B_{\perp}r}{m} = \frac{|q|B_{\perp}d}{2m}$. The kinetic energy is

$$
K = \frac{1}{2} m \upsilon^2 = \frac{1}{2} m \left(\frac{|q|B_{\perp}d}{2m}\right)^2 = \frac{(|q|B_{\perp}d)^3}{8m} = \frac{\left[(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \, \mathrm{C})(0.3 \, \mathrm{T})(0.020 \, \mathrm{m})\right]^2}{8(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \, \mathrm{kg})} = 1.3 \times 10^{-13} \, \mathrm{J}
$$

**Reflect:** This is almost a MeV, which is a significant kinetic energy for an electron.

questions.questionText:
20.22. For no deflection the magnetic and electric forces must be equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. $\upsilon = E / B$ for no deflection. With only the magnetic force, $|q|\upsilon B = m\upsilon^2 /R$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** (a) $\upsilon = E / B = (1.56 \times 10^4 \, \mathrm{V/m}) / (4.62 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{T}) = 3.38 \times 10^6 \, \mathrm{m/s}$

(b) The directions of the three vectors $\vec{\pmb{v}}$, $\vec{E}$, and $\vec{B}$ are sketched in the figure below.

(c) $R = \frac{m\nu}{|q|B} = \frac{(9.11\times 10^{-31}\mathrm{kg})(3.38\times 10^{6}\mathrm{m / s})}{(1.60\times 10^{-19}\mathrm{C})(4.62\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{T})} = 4.17\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m}$

$$
T = \frac{2\pi m}{|q|B} = \frac{2\pi R}{\nu} = \frac{2\pi (4.17\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m})}{(3.38\times 10^{6}\mathrm{m / s})} = 7.74\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{s}
$$

**Reflect:** For the field directions shown in the figure below, the electric force is toward the top of the page and the magnetic force is toward the bottom of the page.

![img-55.jpeg](img-55.jpeg)

questions.questionText:
20.23. In a velocity selector, $E = \upsilon B$ (Section 20.2). For motion in a circular arc in a magnetic field $B'$, $R = \frac{m\nu}{|q|B'}$ (Section 20.3). The ions have charge $+e$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** (a) $\upsilon = \frac{E}{B} = \frac{155 \, \mathrm{V/m}}{0.0315 \, \mathrm{T}} = 4.92 \times 10^{3} \, \mathrm{m/s}$

(b) $m = \frac{R|q|B'}{\nu} = \frac{(0.175 \, \mathrm{m})(1.60 \times 10^{-19} \, \mathrm{C})(0.0175 \, \mathrm{T})}{4.92 \times 10^{3} \, \mathrm{m/s}} = 9.96 \times 10^{-26} \, \mathrm{kg}$

**Reflect:** Ions with larger $m / |q|$ move in a path of larger radius.

questions.questionText:
20.24. For $m = 1.99 \times 10^{-26} \, \mathrm{kg}$ (${}^12\mathrm{C}$), $R_{12} = 12.5 \, \mathrm{cm}$. The separation of the isotopes at the detector is $2(R_{15} - R_{14})$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** $R = \frac{m\nu}{|q|B} \cdot \frac{R}{m} = \frac{\upsilon}{|q|B} = \text{constant}$. $\frac{R_{14}}{m_{14}} = \frac{R_{12}}{m_{12}}$ and



$$
R_{14} = R_{15} \left(\frac{m_{14}}{m_{12}}\right) = (12.5 \mathrm{~cm}) \left(\frac{2.32 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}}{1.99 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}}\right) = 14.6 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

$$
R_{15} = R_{12} \left(\frac{m_{15}}{m_{12}}\right) = (12.5 \mathrm{~cm}) \left(\frac{2.49 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}}{1.99 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}}\right) = 15.6 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

The separation of the isotopes at the detector is $2(R_{15} - R_{14}) = 2(15.6 \, \mathrm{cm} - 14.6 \, \mathrm{cm}) = 2.0 \, \mathrm{cm}$.

**Reflect:** The separation is large enough to be easily detectable. Since the diameter of the ion path is large, about $30\mathrm{cm}$, the uniform magnetic field within the instrument must extend over a large area.

questions.questionText:
20.25. $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$. The direction of $\vec{F}$ is given by applying the right-hand rule to the directions of $I$ and $\vec{B}$.

questions.expectedAnswer:

(c) The current and field directions are shown in Figure (c) above. The right-hand rule gives that $\vec{F}$ is $60.0^{\circ}$ north of west. $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ so $F = 7.06 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}$, the same as in part (a).

questions.questionText:
20.26. $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$. The current and field are perpendicular, so $\phi = 90^{\circ}$. 1 gauss = $10^{-4}$ T.

questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:** $F = (50,000\mathrm{A})(1\mathrm{m})(0.5\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{T})\sin 90^{\circ} = 2.5\mathrm{N}$

questions.questionText:
20.27. $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$. Since the field is perpendicular to the rod it is perpendicular to the current and $\phi = 90^{\circ}$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:** $I = \frac{F}{\ell B} = \frac{0.13\mathrm{N}}{(0.200\mathrm{m})(0.067\mathrm{T})} = 9.7\mathrm{A}$

questions.questionText:
20.28. $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$. The direction of $\vec{F}$ is determined by applying the right-hand rule to the directions of $I$ and $\vec{B}$. 1 gauss = $10^{-4}$ T.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** (a) The directions of $I$ and $\vec{B}$ are sketched in Figure (a) below. $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ and

$$
F = (1.5 \mathrm{A})(2.5 \mathrm{m})(0.55 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{T}) = 2.1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{N}
$$

The right-hand rule says that $\vec{F}$ is directed out of the page, so is upward.



![img-60.jpeg](img-60.jpeg)
(a)

![img-61.jpeg](img-61.jpeg)
(b)

(b) The directions of $I$ and $\vec{B}$ are sketched in Figure (b) above. $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ and $F = 2.1\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{N}$. $\vec{F}$ is directed east to west.
(c) $\vec{B}$ and the direction of the current are antiparallel. $\phi = 180^{\circ}$ and $F = 0$.
(d) The magnetic force of $2.1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~N}$ is not large enough to cause significant effects.

**Reflect:** The magnetic force is a maximum when the directions of $I$ and $\vec{B}$ are perpendicular and it is zero when the current and magnetic field are either parallel or antiparallel.

**20.29. Set Up:** The magnetic force is $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$. To suspend the wire, this force must have the same magnitude as the force $mg$ due to gravity. The minimum magnetic field required is for $\phi = 90^{\circ}$.

**Solve:** $I\ell B\sin \phi = mg$ so $B = \frac{mg}{I\ell} = \frac{(0.02\mathrm{kg})(9.8\mathrm{m / s}^2)}{(10\mathrm{A})(0.15\mathrm{m})} = 0.1\mathrm{T}$

**Reflect:** Note that the magnetic field must be oriented horizontally to create an upward force on the wire.

**20.30. Set Up:** $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$. The direction of $\vec{F}$ is given by the right-hand rule applied to the directions of $I$ and $\vec{B}$.

**Solve:** (a) segment $da$: $\phi = 180^{\circ}$ and $F = 0$ segment $ab$: $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ and $F = I\ell B = (8.0\mathrm{A})(0.200\mathrm{m})(0.750\mathrm{T}) = 1.2\mathrm{N}$. The directions of $I$, $\vec{B}$ and $\vec{F}$ are shown in Figure (a) below. $\vec{F}$ is directed into the page. segment $bc$: $\phi = 0^{\circ}$ and $F = 0$ segment $cd$: $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ and $F = 1.2\mathrm{N}$. The directions of $I$, $\vec{B}$ and $\vec{F}$ are shown in Figure (b) below. $\vec{F}$ is directed out of the page.

![img-62.jpeg](img-62.jpeg)
(a)

![img-63.jpeg](img-63.jpeg)
(b)

(b) $\vec{F}_{ab}$ and $\vec{F}_{cd}$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. Their vector sum is zero and the net force on the entire circuit is zero.

^{}[]

Reflect: The net force on any current loop in a uniform magnetic field is zero.

questions.questionText:
20.31. The direction of the magnetic force on each current segment in the field is shown in the figure below. By symmetry,  $F_{a} = F_{b}$ .  $\vec{F}_{a}$  and  $\vec{F}_{b}$  are in opposite directions, so their vector sum is zero. The net force equals  $F_{c}$ .  $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$ . For  $F_{c}$ ,  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$  and  $\ell = 0.450\mathrm{m}$ .

![img-64.jpeg](img-64.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:  $F_{c} = I\ell B = (6.00\mathrm{A})(0.450\mathrm{m})(0.666\mathrm{T}) = 1.80\mathrm{N}$ . The net force is  $1.80\mathrm{N}$ , directed to the left.

questions.questionText:
20.32. Label the three segments in the field as  $a$ ,  $b$ , and  $c$ . Let  $x$  be the length of segment  $a$ . Segment  $b$  has length  $0.300 \, \text{m}$  and segment  $c$  has length  $0.600 \, \text{cm} - x$ . Figure (a) below shows the direction of the force on each segment. For each segment,  $\phi = 90^\circ$ . The total force on the wire is the vector sum of the forces on each segment.

![img-65.jpeg](img-65.jpeg)
(a)

![img-66.jpeg](img-66.jpeg)
(b)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $F_{a} = I\ell B = (4.50\mathrm{A})x(0.240\mathrm{T})$ .  $F_{c} = (4.50\mathrm{A})(0.600\mathrm{m} - x)(0.240\mathrm{T})$ . Since  $\vec{F}_{a}$  and  $\vec{F}_{c}$  are in the same direction their vector sum has magnitude  $F_{ac} = F_{a} + F_{c} = (4.50\mathrm{A})(0.600\mathrm{m})(0.240\mathrm{T}) = 0.648\mathrm{N}$  and is directed toward the bottom of the page in Figure (a).  $F_{b} = (4.50\mathrm{A})(0.300\mathrm{m})(0.240\mathrm{T}) = 0.324\mathrm{N}$  and is directed to the right. The vector addition diagram for  $\vec{F}_{ac}$  and  $\vec{F}_{b}$  is given in Figure (b) above.

$$
F = \sqrt {F _ {a c} ^ {2} + F _ {b} ^ {2}} = \sqrt {(0 . 6 4 8 \mathrm {N}) ^ {2} + (0 . 3 2 4 \mathrm {N}) ^ {2}} = 0. 7 2 4 \mathrm {N}. \tan \theta = \frac {F _ {a c}}{F _ {b}} = \frac {0 . 6 4 8 \mathrm {N}}{0 . 3 2 4 \mathrm {N}} \text {a n d}
$$

$$
\theta = 6 3. 4 ^ {\circ}
$$

The net force has magnitude  $0.724\mathrm{N}$  and its direction is specified by  $\theta = 63.4^{\circ}$  in Figure (b).

Reflect: All three current segments are perpendicular to the magnetic field, so  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$  for each in the force equation. The direction of the force on a segment depends on the direction of the current for that segment.

questions.questionText: 20.33. $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$ . The direction of the magnetic force is given by the right-hand rule applied to the directions of  $I$  and  $\vec{B}$ . The torque due to a force equals the force times the moment arm, the perpendicular distance between the axis and the line of action of the force.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The direction of the magnetic force on each segment of the circuit is shown in the figure below. For segments  $bc$  and  $da$  the current is parallel or antiparallel to the field and the force on these segments is zero.

![img-67.jpeg](img-67.jpeg)

(b)  $\vec{F}_{ab}$  acts at the hinge and therefore produces no torque.  $\vec{F}_{cd}$  tends to rotate the loop about the hinge so it does produce a torque about this axis.  $F_{cd} = I\ell B\sin \phi = (5.00\mathrm{A})(0.200\mathrm{m})(1.20\mathrm{T})\sin 90^{\circ} = 1.20\mathrm{N}$ .
(c)  $\tau = F\ell = (1.20\mathrm{N})(0.350\mathrm{m}) = 0.420\mathrm{N}\cdot \mathrm{m}$ . The torque is directed so as to rotate side  $cd$  out of the plane of the page in the figure above.

questions.questionText: 20.34. $\tau = IAB\sin \phi$ . Since the plane of the loop is parallel to the field, the field is perpendicular to the normal to the loop and  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ . The magnetic moment of the loop is  $\mu = IA$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $\tau = IAB = (6.2\mathrm{A})(0.050\mathrm{m})(0.080\mathrm{m})(0.19\mathrm{T}) = 4.7\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{N}\cdot \mathrm{m}$

(b)  $\mu = IA = (6.2\mathrm{A})(0.050\mathrm{m})(0.080\mathrm{m}) = 0.025\mathrm{A}\cdot \mathrm{m}^2$

Reflect: The torque is a maximum when the field is in the plane of the loop and  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$

questions.questionText: 20.35. $\tau = IAB\sin \phi$ . The maximum torque is for  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ , where  $\phi$  is the angle between the field and the normal to the plane of the loop. The coil has area  $A = \pi r^{2} = \pi (4.3\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m})^{2} = 5.81\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m}^{2}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $\tau = (15)(2.7\mathrm{A})(5.81\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m}^2)(0.56\mathrm{T}) = 0.132\mathrm{N}\cdot \mathrm{m}^2$ . The plane of the coil is parallel to the field.

(b)  $\tau = 0.71\tau_{\mathrm{max}}$  means  $\sin \phi = 0.71$  and  $\phi = 45^{\circ}$ . The plane of the coil makes an angle of  $45^{\circ}$  with the field direction.

questions.questionText: 20.36. $\tau = NIAB\sin \phi$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) If the coil normal is perpendicular to the field, then  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ . The torque is  $\tau = NIAB\sin \phi = (165)(1.20\mathrm{A})\pi (0.012\mathrm{m})^{2}(3.0\mathrm{T}) = 0.27\mathrm{N}\cdot \mathrm{m}$ .

(b) If the coil normal is parallel to the field, then  $\phi = 0^{\circ}$  and  $\sin \phi = 0$  so the torque is zero.

(c) If the coil normal is at  $30^{\circ}$  to the field, then  $\phi = 30^{\circ}$  and  $\sin \phi = \frac{1}{2}$ . From part (a), the torque is  $\tau = \frac{0.2687}{2} = 0.13\mathrm{N}\cdot \mathrm{m}$ .

Reflect: For any current loop in a uniform magnetic field the net force on the loop is zero. The torque on the loop depends on the orientation of the plane of the loop relative to the magnetic field direction.

questions.questionText: 20.37. We know that $\tau = NIAB\sin\phi$, where $A = \pi r^2$. The direction of the force on each section of the loop is given by the right-hand rule.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\tau = NI(\pi r^2)B\sin\phi = 50(2.50\mathrm{A})\pi (0.100\mathrm{m})^2 (0.450\mathrm{T})\sin 45^\circ = 1.25\mathrm{N}\cdot \mathrm{m}$.

(b) Imagine that you are standing to the south and looking down on the coil. The current in the north side of the coil is to your right. Since the magnetic field points down and away from you, the right-hand rule shows that the magnetic force on the north section of the coil must be up and away from you. A similar analysis shows that the magnetic force on the south side of the coil must be down and toward you. Thus, the net torque on the coil will tend to raise the north side of the coil. It will also tend to lower the south side of the coil, which cannot happen since the coil is sitting on the tabletop.

Reflect: The net magnetic force on the coil is zero but the net magnetic torque on the coil is not zero.

questions.questionText: 20.38. For a long, straight wire, $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $I = \frac{2\pi r B}{\mu_0} = \frac{(2\pi)(0.040\mathrm{m})(5.50\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{T})}{4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A}} = 110\mathrm{A}$

(b) $B = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m / A})(110 \mathrm{~A})}{2\pi (8.00 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m})} = 2.75 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{T}$. $B$ is proportional to $1 / r$ so the field is half what it is in part (a).

questions.questionText: 20.39. For a long straight wire, $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$. The magnetic field of the earth is of the order of $10^{-4}$ T. $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T·m/A.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $B = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m / A})(10 \mathrm{~A})}{2\pi (0.050 \mathrm{~m})} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{T}$. This field is the same order of magnitude as the earth's field.

questions.questionText: 20.40. The long current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field. The magnetic field due to a long wire is $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: First solve for the current, then substitute the numbers using the above equation.

(a) Solving for the current gives

$$
I = 2 \pi r B / \mu_ {0} = 2 \pi (0. 0 2 0 0 \mathrm {m}) (1. 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {T}) / (4 \pi \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {T} \cdot \mathrm {m} / \mathrm {A}) = 1 0. 0 \mathrm {A}
$$

(b) The earth's horizontal field points northward, so at all points directly above the wire the field of the wire would point northward.

(c) At all points directly east of the wire, its field would point northward.

Reflect: Even though the earth's magnetic field is rather weak, it requires a fairly large current to cancel this field.

questions.questionText: 20.41. 1 gauss $= 10^{-4}$ T. For a long straight wire, $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $I = \frac{2\pi r B}{\mu_0} = \frac{2\pi (0.050\mathrm{m})(1.0\times 10^{-10}\mathrm{T})}{4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A}} = 2.5\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{A} = 25\mu \mathrm{A}$

questions.questionText: 20.42. $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $B = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m / A})(12 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{A})}{2\pi (0.020 \mathrm{~m})} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} = 0.12 \mu \mathrm{T}$

^{}[]

questions.questionText: 20.43. For a long, thin, straight wire we have $ B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} $. $ \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A} $.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $ B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A})(100 \mathrm{~A})}{2\pi(0.3 \mathrm{~m})} = 7 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{T} $

Reflect: $ B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} $ applies precisely only to an infinitely long straight wire. If the distance from the wire (0.3 m in this case) is comparable to the length of the wire (~2 m) in this case, then this approximation does not work.

questions.questionText: 20.44. $ B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} $. $ B_1 = B $ when $ r_1 = R $. Calculate $ r_2 $ when $ B_2 = B/3 $.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $ Br = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi} = \text{constant} $ and $ B_1r_1 = B_2r_2 $. $ r_2 = r_1\left(\frac{B_1}{B_2}\right) = R\left(\frac{B}{B/3}\right) = 3R $.

Solve: (a) At $ r = \frac{r_0}{2} $ the magnetic field is $ B = \frac{\mu_0 I_0}{2\pi (r_0 / 2)} = 2\frac{\mu_0 I_0}{2\pi r_0} = 2B_0 $.

(b) At $ r = 3r_0 $ and with current $ I = 6I_0 $, the magnetic field is $ B = \frac{\mu_0(6I_0)}{2\pi(3r_0)} = 2\frac{\mu_0I_0}{2\pi r_0} = 2B_0 $.

Reflect: $ B $ is proportional to $ I $ and to $ 1/r $. When $ I $ is doubled, $ B $ is doubled. When $ r $ is doubled, $ B $ is halved.

questions.questionText: 20.46. $ B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} $. $ \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A} $
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Assume that the lines are $ 10\mathrm{m} $ off the ground. At this distance

$$
B = \frac {(4 \pi \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {T} \cdot \mathrm {m} / \mathrm {A}) (1 0 0 \mathrm {A})}{2 \pi (1 0 \mathrm {m})} = 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {T}
$$

This is about $ 4\% $ of the earth's magnetic field. The magnetic field is too small to be of concern.

questions.questionText: 20.47. For a long, straight wire $ B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} $. If the currents are in the same direction, their fields add. If a pair of wires carry current in opposite directions, their fields are in opposite directions and cancel.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)

$$
B = 6 \frac {\mu_ {0} I}{2 \pi r} = 6 \frac {(4 \pi \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {T} \cdot \mathrm {m / A}) (0 . 3 0 0 \mathrm {A})}{2 \pi (2 . 5 0 \mathrm {m})} = 1. 4 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {T}
$$

(b) The fields of two pairs cancel and the net field is due to the remaining two wires.

$$
B = 2 \frac {\mu_ {0} I}{2 \pi r} = \frac {1}{3} (1. 4 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {T}) = 4. 8 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 8} \mathrm {T}
$$

Reflect: In a typical household appliance cord there are two closely spaced conductors carrying current in opposite directions and the currents in the cord produce very little net magnetic field.

questions.questionText: 20.48. The net magnetic field at each point is the vector sum of the fields due to each wire. For each wire $ B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} $ and the direction of $ \vec{B} $ is determined by the right-hand rule described in Section 20.7. Let the wire with $ 12.0\mathrm{A} $ be wire 1 and the wire with $ 10.0\mathrm{A} $ be wire 2.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Point $ Q $: $ B_{1} = \frac{\mu_{0}I_{1}}{2\pi r_{1}} = \frac{(4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A})(12.0\mathrm{A})}{2\pi(0.15\mathrm{m})} = 1.6\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{T} $. The direction of $ \vec{B}_{1} $ is out of the page.

$$
B _ {2} = \frac {\mu_ {0} I _ {2}}{2 \pi r _ {2}} = \frac {(4 \pi \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {T} \cdot \mathrm {m / A}) (1 0 . 0 \mathrm {A})}{2 \pi (0 . 0 8 0 \mathrm {m})} = 2. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {T}
$$



The direction of  $\vec{B}_2$  is out of the page. Since  $\vec{B}_1$  and  $\vec{B}_2$  are in the same direction,  $B = B_1 + B_2 = 4.1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{T}$  and  $\vec{B}$  is directed out of the page.

Point  $P$ :  $B_{1} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{T}$ , directed into the page.  $B_{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{T}$ , directed into the page.

$$
B = B _ {1} + B _ {2} = 4. 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {T}
$$

and  $\vec{B}$  is directed into the page.

(b)  $\vec{B}_1$  is the same as in part (a), out of the page at  $Q$  and into the page at  $P$ . The direction of  $\vec{B}_2$  is reversed from what it was in (a) so  $\vec{B}$  is into the page at  $Q$  and out of the page at  $P$ .

Point  $Q$ :  $\vec{B}_1$  and  $\vec{B}_2$  are in opposite directions so  $B = B_2 - B_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \mathrm{T} - 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \, \mathrm{T} = 9.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{T}$  and  $\vec{B}$  is directed into the page.

Point  $P$ :  $\vec{B}_1$  and  $\vec{B}_2$  are in opposite directions so  $B = B_2 - B_1 = 9.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{T}$  and  $\vec{B}$  is directed out of the page.

questions.questionText:
20.49. $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$ . The direction of  $\vec{B}$  is given by the right-hand rule in Section 20.7. Call the wires  $a$  and  $b$ , as indicated in the figure below. The magnetic fields of each wire at points  $P_1$  and  $P_2$  are also shown in the figure.

![img-68.jpeg](img-68.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) At  $P_{1}$ ,  $B_{a} = B_{b}$  and the two fields are in opposite directions, so the net field is zero.

(b)  $B_{a} = \frac{\mu_{0}I}{2\pi r_{a}}$ $B_{b} = \frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi r_b}$ $\vec{B}_a$  and  $\vec{B}_b$  are in the same direction so

$$
B = B _ {a} + B _ {b} = \frac {\mu_ {0} I}{2 \pi} \left(\frac {1}{r _ {a}} + \frac {1}{r _ {b}}\right) = \frac {(4 \pi \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {T} \cdot \mathrm {m} / \mathrm {A}) (4 . 0 0 \mathrm {A})}{2 \pi} \left[ \frac {1}{0 . 3 0 0 \mathrm {m}} + \frac {1}{0 . 2 0 0 \mathrm {m}} \right] = 6. 6 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {T}
$$

$\vec{B}$  has magnitude  $6.67\mu \mathrm{T}$  and is directed toward the top of the page.

Reflect: At points directly to the left of both wires the net field is directed toward the bottom of the page.

questions.questionText: 20.50. $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$ . The direction of  $\vec{B}$  is given by the right-hand rule in Section 20.7.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The magnetic fields of the two wires are in opposite directions at points between the wires, as shown in Figure (a) below. Consider a point that is a distance  $x$  from wire 1 and hence  $0.400\mathrm{m} - x$  from wire 2.  $B_{1} = B_{2}$  gives

$$
\frac {\mu_ {0} I _ {1}}{2 \pi x} = \frac {\mu_ {0} I _ {2}}{2 \pi (0 . 4 0 0 \mathrm {m} - x)}. \frac {2 5 . 0 \mathrm {A}}{x} = \frac {7 5 . 0 \mathrm {A}}{(0 . 4 0 0 \mathrm {m} - x)}. 3. 0 0 x = 0. 4 0 0 \mathrm {m} - x \text {a n d} x = 0. 1 0 0 \mathrm {m}
$$

The net field is zero at a point between the wires,  $10.0\mathrm{cm}$  from the wire carrying  $25.0\mathrm{A}$  and  $30.0\mathrm{cm}$  from the wire carrying  $75.0\mathrm{A}$ .

![img-69.jpeg](img-69.jpeg)
(a)

![img-70.jpeg](img-70.jpeg)
(b)



(b) The magnetic fields of the two wires are in opposite directions at points along the extension of the line connecting the wires and either to the right or to the left of both wires. But for the fields to cancel, they must have equal magnitudes and this happens only at points closer to the wire carrying the smaller current. Therefore, consider a point $P$ that is a distance $x$ to the left of wire 1 and hence $0.400\,\mathrm{m} + x$ to the left of wire 2, as shown in Figure (b) above.

$$
\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi x} = \frac{\mu_0 I_2}{2\pi(0.400\,\mathrm{m} + x)}, \quad \frac{25.0\,\mathrm{A}}{x} = \frac{75.0\,\mathrm{A}}{(0.400\,\mathrm{m} + x)}
$$

$3.00x = 0.400\,\mathrm{m} + x$ and $x = 0.200\,\mathrm{m}$. The net field is zero at a point $20.0\,\mathrm{cm}$ from the wire carrying $25.0\,\mathrm{A}$ and $60.0\,\mathrm{cm}$ from the wire carrying $75.0\,\mathrm{A}$.

questions.questionText: 20.51. $F = \frac{\mu_0 \ell H'}{2\pi r}$. $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{N/A^2}$. Parallel conductors carrying currents in opposite directions repel each other. Parallel conductors carrying currents in the same direction attract each other.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{N/A^2})(15\,\mathrm{m})(25\,\mathrm{A})(75\,\mathrm{A})}{2\pi(0.35\,\mathrm{m})} = 0.0161\,\mathrm{N}$. Since the currents are in the same direction the force is attractive.

Reflect: The currents are large but the force per meter on each wire is very small.

questions.questionText: 20.52. Label wires 1 and 2. Find the direction of the magnetic field of one wire at the location of the other. Then use the right-hand rule to find the direction of the force on the second wire due to this magnetic field.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Figure (a) below shows the magnetic fields at the wires for the case where the currents are both to the left. The directions of the forces on the wires exerted by these fields are also shown. The wires attract.

![img-71.jpeg](img-71.jpeg)
(a)

![img-72.jpeg](img-72.jpeg)

![img-73.jpeg](img-73.jpeg)
(b)

Figure (b) above shows the magnetic fields at the wires for the case where one current is to the left and the other is to the right. The directions of the forces on the wires exerted by these fields are also shown. The wires repel.

questions.questionText: 20.53. Measurements on a typical extension cord give that the centers of the two wires are separated by about $4\,\mathrm{mm}$. $F = \frac{\mu_0 \ell H'}{2\pi r}$. $4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{N/A^2}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{N/A^2})(2.0\,\mathrm{m})(5.0\,\mathrm{A})^2}{2\pi(4.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m})} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{N}$. Since the currents are in opposite directions the wires repel.

questions.questionText: 20.54. The attractive force between two parallel wires is $F = \frac{\mu_0 \ell I_1 I_2}{2\pi r}$. The power input of the motor is 65 hp. We know that $1\,\mathrm{hp} = 746\,\mathrm{W}$. The relation between power, voltage, and current is $P = V I$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) We can find the current from $I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{(65\,\mathrm{hp})(746\,\mathrm{W/hp})}{600\,\mathrm{V}} = \underline{80.8}\,\mathrm{A}$.



(b) The attractive force between the wires per unit length is  $F / \ell = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A})(80.8 \, \mathrm{A})^2}{2\pi(0.55 \, \mathrm{m})} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{N/m}$ .

Reflect: If the current from the cables is in the same direction, the force will be attractive; however, if the current runs in opposite directions the force will be repulsive.

questions.questionText: 20.55. At the center of a circular loop  $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$ .  $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A}$ . The direction of  $\vec{B}$  can be found by the right-hand rule: with the thumb of your right hand in the direction of the current your fingers curl around the wire in the direction of the magnetic field.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $I = \frac{2RB}{\mu_0} = \frac{2(0.11\mathrm{m})(0.50\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{T})}{4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A}} = 8.8\mathrm{A}$

(b) The plane of the loop should be perpendicular to the earth's field and the current should be directed as shown in the figure below.

![img-74.jpeg](img-74.jpeg)

Reflect: A large current is required. Note that  $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$  only applies at the center of the circular loop. The current produces magnetic field at other points but only at the center of the loop do we have a simple expression for the magnitude of the field.

questions.questionText: 20.56. The magnetic field at the center of  $N$  circular loops is  $B = \frac{N\mu_0I}{2R}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:  $B = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A})(600)(0.500 \, \mathrm{A})}{2(2.00 \times 10^{-2} \, \mathrm{m})} = 9.42 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{T}$

questions.questionText: 20.57. The magnetic field at the center of  $N$  circular loops is  $B = \frac{N\mu_0I}{2R}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:  $N = \frac{2RB}{\mu_0I} = \frac{2(6.00\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m})(6.39\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{T})}{(4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A})(2.50\mathrm{A})} = 24.4.$  Therefore, 24 turns are required.
Solve:  $I = \frac{2RB}{\mu_0} = \frac{2(8\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m})(3.0\times 10^{-12}\mathrm{T})}{4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A}} = 3.8\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{A}.$

questions.questionText: 20.59. Apply Eq. (20.13), where the radius of the coil is  $R = 0.0240 \, \mathrm{m}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $B = \frac{\mu_0NI}{2R}$ , so  $I = \frac{2aB}{\mu_0N} = \frac{2(0.0240\mathrm{m})(0.0580\mathrm{T})}{(4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A})(800)} = 2.77\mathrm{A}$

Reflect: Despite the large number of turns, a sizable current is necessary to produce the given magnetic field.

questions.questionText: 20.60. Let wire 1 be the inner wire with diameter $20.0\mathrm{cm}$ and let wire 2 be the outer wire with diameter $30.0\mathrm{cm}$. To produce zero net field, the fields $\vec{B}_1$ and $\vec{B}_2$ of the two wires must have equal magnitudes and opposite directions. At the center of a wire loop we have $B = \frac{\mu_0I}{2R}$. The direction of $\vec{B}$ is given by the right-hand rule applied to the current direction.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $B_{1} = \frac{\mu_{0}I}{2R_{1}}$, $B_{2} = \frac{\mu_{0}I}{2R_{2}}$, $B_{1} = B_{2}$ gives $\frac{\mu_0I_1}{2R_1} = \frac{\mu_0I_2}{2R_2}$ and $I_{2} = \left(\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}\right)I_{1} = \left(\frac{15.0\mathrm{cm}}{10.0\mathrm{cm}}\right)(12.0\mathrm{A}) = 18.0\mathrm{A}$. The directions of $I_{1}$ and of its field are shown in the figure below. Since $\vec{B}_1$ is directed into the page we need $\vec{B}_2$ to be directed out of the page, and so $I_{2}$ is counterclockwise.

![img-75.jpeg](img-75.jpeg)

questions.questionText: 20.61. The magnetic field at the center of a circular loop is $B = \frac{\mu_0I}{2R}$. By symmetry each segment of the loop that has length $\Delta l$ contributes equally to the field, so the field at the center of a semicircle is $\frac{1}{2}$ that of a full loop. Since the straight sections produce no field at $P$, the field at $P$ is $B = \frac{\mu_0I}{4R}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $B = \frac{\mu_0I}{4R}$. The direction of $\vec{B}$ is given by the right-hand rule: $\vec{B}$ is directed into the page.

Reflect: Symmetry is very useful for many problems. For example, for a quarter-circle section of wire the magnetic field at its center of curvature is $B = \frac{\mu_0I}{8R}$.
