questions.questionText: 20.62. $B = \mu_0 n I$. $n = \frac{N}{L} = \frac{750 \, \text{coils}}{0.150 \, \text{m}} = 5000 \, \text{coils/m}$
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A})(5000 \, \text{coils/m})(7.00 \, \mathrm{A}) = 0.0440 \, \mathrm{T}$

questions.questionText: 20.63. At the center of a long solenoid $B = \mu_0 n I = \mu_0 \frac{N}{L} I$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $I = \frac{BL}{\mu_0N} = \frac{(0.150\mathrm{T})(1.40\mathrm{m})}{(4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A})(4000)} = 41.8\mathrm{A}$

questions.questionText: 20.64. At the center of a long solenoid $B = \mu_0 \frac{N}{L} I$. The total length of wire is $N(2\pi r)$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $N = \frac{BL}{\mu_0I} = \frac{(0.0279\mathrm{T})(0.400\mathrm{m})}{(4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A})(12.0\mathrm{A})} = 740$ turns

(b) The total length of wire is $N(2\pi r) = (740)(2\pi)(1.40 \times 10^{-2} \, \mathrm{m}) = 65.1 \, \mathrm{m}$.

questions.questionText: 20.65. The magnetic field at the center of a circular loop is $B_{\mathrm{loop}} = \frac{\mu_0 I_{\mathrm{loop}}}{2R}$, where $I_{\mathrm{loop}} = 2.0 \, \mathrm{A}$ and $R = 5.0 \, \mathrm{cm}$. The magnetic field from the wire is $B_{\mathrm{wire}} = \frac{\mu_0 I_{\mathrm{wire}}}{2\pi r}$, where $r = 20.0 \, \mathrm{cm}$. Use the right-hand rule to determine the direction of the magnetic field of the loop and the wire.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Curling the right-hand finger in the direction of the current in the loop, the thumb points into the page, so the magnetic field inside the loop points into the page. Pointing the right-hand thumb down along the wire, the fingers point up out of the page, so the magnetic field from the wire points out of the page. Thus, the current in the wire should flow downward.

(b) $B_{\mathrm{loop}} = B_{\mathrm{wire}}$ so $\frac{\mu_0 I_{\mathrm{loop}}}{2R} = \frac{\mu_0 I_{\mathrm{wire}}}{2\pi r}$ and $I_{\mathrm{wire}} = \frac{\pi r I_{\mathrm{loop}}}{R} = \frac{\pi (20.0 \, \mathrm{cm}) (2.0 \, \mathrm{A})}{5.0 \, \mathrm{cm}} = 25 \, \mathrm{A}$

Reflect: The current in the wire must be stronger than that in the loop because the wire is far from the center of the loop compared with the radius of the loop.

questions.questionText:
20.66. At the center of a solenoid, $B_{\mathrm{solenoid}} = \mu_0 n I = \mu_0 \frac{N}{L} I$. A distance $R$ from a long straight wire,

$$
B_{\mathrm{wire}} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi R}.
$$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $B_{\mathrm{solenoid}} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T \cdot m / A}) \left( \frac{450}{0.35 \, \mathrm{m}} \right) (1.75 \, \mathrm{A}) = 2.83 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{T}$

(b) $B_{\mathrm{wire}} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T \cdot m / A})(1.75 \, \mathrm{A})}{2\pi(1.0 \times 10^{-2} \, \mathrm{m})} = 3.50 \times 10^{-5} \, \mathrm{T}$

The magnetic field due to the wire is much less than the field at the center of the solenoid. For the solenoid, the fields of all the wires add to give a much larger field.

questions.questionText: 20.67. The magnetic field at the center of $N$ circular loops is $B = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2R}$. The magnetic field of the earth is given to be $0.55 \, \mathrm{G} = 5.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \mathrm{T}$. The number of turns can be determined from the length of the wire and the diameter of the coil: $N = L / (\pi D)$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The number of turns is $N = L / (\pi D) = \frac{25 \, \mathrm{m}}{\pi (0.44 \, \mathrm{m})} = 18$. The required current is

$$
I = \frac{2R B}{\mu_0 N} = \frac{2(0.22 \, \mathrm{m}) (5.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \mathrm{T})}{18(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T \cdot m / A})} = 1.1 \, \mathrm{A}
$$

Reflect: For a fixed current, the magnetic field, $B = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2R}$, is determined by the ratio $\frac{N}{R}$. If a given length of wire is used to construct the coil, we know that halving $R$ would double the number of turns $(N)$ that we can make—and so the $B$ at the center of the coil would be quadrupled.

questions.questionText: 20.68. Figure 20.42 in the text shows that, outside a toroidal solenoid, there is no magnetic field. According to Eq. (20.15), the magnetic field inside the toroidal solenoid is given by $B = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2\pi r}$. The torus extends from $r_1 = 15.0 \, \mathrm{cm}$ to $r_2 = 18.0 \, \mathrm{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $r = 0.12 \, \mathrm{m}$, which is outside the torus, so $B = 0$.

(b) $r = 0.16 \, \mathrm{m}$, so $B = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2\pi r} = \frac{\mu_0 (250) (8.50 \, \mathrm{A})}{2\pi (0.160 \, \mathrm{m})} = 2.66 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{T}$.

(c) $r = 0.20 \, \mathrm{m}$, which is outside the torus, so $B = 0$.

Reflect: The magnetic field inside the torus is proportional to $1/r$, so it varies somewhat over the cross-section of the torus.

questions.questionText: 20.69. Assume that the tightly bound wires have negligible diameters. By symmetry the field lines are circles centered on the wires and the magnitude of the field depends only on the distance from the wires. Ampere’s law says $\sum B_{j}\Delta s = \mu_{0}I_{\mathrm{encl}}$. Apply this law to a circular path with radius $r = 10\mathrm{m}$ and with the wires at its center.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: As we move along the chosen circular path $\widehat{B}$ has a constant magnitude and is tangent to the path. If we face southward as we look at the circular path, the net current points toward us and (according to the right-hand rule) the magnetic field circulates in a counterclockwise direction around the circular path. Thus, if we traverse the circular path in the counterclockwise direction we have $\sum B_{j}\Delta s = B\sum \Delta s = B(2\pi r)$, with $I_{\mathrm{encl}} = 17.5\mathrm{A} + 11.3\mathrm{A} - 23.0\mathrm{A} = 5.8\mathrm{A}$. Ampere’s law therefore gives $B(2\pi r) = \mu_0I_{\mathrm{encl}}$ and $B \approx \frac{\mu_0I_{\mathrm{encl}}}{2\pi r} = \frac{(4\pi\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{m / A})(5.8\mathrm{A})}{2\pi(10.0\mathrm{m})} = \underline{1.16\times 10^{-7}} = 0.17\mu \mathrm{T}$.

Reflect: We could also obtain this result by using the superposition principle since each current $I$ produces a known magnetic field equal to $B \approx \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$. As we face southward, northbound currents create counterclockwise magnetic field and southbound currents create clockwise fields. Thus, the net magnetic field has a magnitude equal to $B \approx \frac{\mu_0 |I_{\mathrm{south}} - I_{\mathrm{south}}|}{2\pi r}$, with the direction of circulation determined by the larger current.

questions.questionText: 20.70. By symmetry the magnetic field lines of a cylindrical wire are similar to that of a long, straight conductor—and this is true at points both inside and outside of the conductor. Thus, applying Ampere’s law we obtain $\sum B_{j}\Delta s = B\sum \Delta s = B(2\pi r) = \mu_{0}I_{\mathrm{encl}}$. This reduces to $B = \frac{\mu_0I_{\mathrm{encl}}}{2\pi r}$ at points both inside and outside the conductor.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Outside the wire, where $r > R$, we have $I_{\mathrm{encl}} = I$, where $I$ is the current carried by the wire. Thus, we have $B_{\mathrm{outside}} = \frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi r}$ at points outside the wire. Inside the wire, where $r < R$, only a fraction of the total current passes through our path: since the current is uniformly distributed over the cross-sectional area we have $I_{\mathrm{encl}} = I\frac{\pi r^2}{\pi R^2} = I\left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2$. Thus, we have $B_{\mathrm{inside}} = \frac{\mu_0I_{\mathrm{encl}}}{2\pi r} = \frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi r}\left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu_0Ir}{2\pi R^2}$ at points inside the wire. Note that the magnitude of $B_{\mathrm{outside}}$ decreases as $r$ increases while the magnitude of $B_{\mathrm{inside}}$ increases as $r$ increases. Thus, the maximum value of $B$ occurs at the surface, where $r = R$, and we have $B_{\mathrm{max}} = \frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi R}$. For points inside the wire, the field has half its maximum value when, $\frac{\mu_0Ir}{2\pi R^2} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi R}\right)$, which gives $r = R/2$. Outside the wire, we have $\frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi r} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mu_0I}{2\pi R}\right)$, which gives $r = 2R$.

Reflect: The magnetic field increases from $r = 0$ to $r = R$, and decreases thereafter. Thus, the magnetic field has its maximum value at points on the surface of the wire, where $r = R$. Inside of the wire, the field has half the maximum value at points, where $r = R / 2$, while outside the wire the field has half its maximum value at points, where $r = 2R$.

questions.questionText: 20.71. $\mu = K_{\mathrm{m}}\mu_{0}$, with $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}~\mathrm{T}\cdot \mathrm{m / A}$. The magnetic field in the material is a factor of $K_{\mathrm{m}}$ greater than it is in vacuum.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $B_{\mathrm{inside}} = K_{\mathrm{m}}B_{\mathrm{external}} = (1.00026)(1.3500\mathrm{T}) = 1.3504\mathrm{T}$

Reflect: The magnetic field inside the paramagnetic material is larger than the external field, but only slightly larger.

questions.questionText: 20.72. $K_{\mathrm{m}} = B_{\mathrm{inside}} / B_{\mathrm{outside}}$
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $K_{\mathrm{m}} = \frac{1.5023\mathrm{T}}{1.5000\mathrm{T}} = 1.0015$

questions.questionText: 20.73. Use  $v_{y}^{2} = v_{0y}^{2} + 2a_{y}y$  to calculate  $v_{y}$ . With  $+y$  downward,  $v_{0y} = 0$ ,  $a_{y} = +9.80\mathrm{m / s^{2}}$  and  $y = 125\mathrm{m}$ . The direction of the force is given by the right-hand rule and the magnitude is given by  $F = |q|vB\sin \phi$ . The charge of the ball is  $-(4.00\times 10^{6})e$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The directions of  $\vec{v}$  and  $\vec{B}$  are shown in the figure below, where the downward direction is into the page. The right-hand rule (right-hand rule) direction is north, but since the charge is negative the force is opposite the right-hand rule direction and is to the south. At the bottom of the shaft, the speed of the ball is

$$
v = \sqrt {2 g (1 2 5 \mathrm {m})} = \sqrt {2 (9 . 8 0 \mathrm {m} / \mathrm {s} ^ {2}) (1 2 5 \mathrm {m})} = 4 9. 5 \mathrm {m} / \mathrm {s}
$$

$$
\left| q \right| = (4. 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {8}) (1. 6 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 9} \mathrm {C}) = 6. 4 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 1} \mathrm {C}
$$

$$
\phi = 9 0 ^ {\circ}, \text {s o} F = | q | v B = (6. 4 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 1} \mathrm {C}) (4 9. 5 \mathrm {m / s}) (0. 2 5 0 \mathrm {T}) = 7. 9 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 1 0} \mathrm {N}
$$

![img-76.jpeg](img-76.jpeg)

![img-77.jpeg](img-77.jpeg)

Reflect: The magnetic force is much less than the gravitational force on the ball.

questions.questionText: 20.74. The magnetic force  $\vec{F}_B$  must be upward and equal to  $mg$ . The direction of  $\vec{F}_B$  is determined by the direction of  $I$  in the circuit.  $F_B = IIB\sin \phi$ , with  $\phi = 90^\circ$ .  $I = \frac{V}{R}$ , where  $V$  is the battery voltage.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The forces are shown in the figure below. The current  $I$  in the bar must be to the right to produce  $\vec{F}_B$  upward. To produce current in this direction, point  $a$  must be the positive terminal of the battery.

![img-78.jpeg](img-78.jpeg)

(b)  $F_{B} = mg$ .  $I\ell B = mg$ .  $m = \frac{I\ell B}{g} = \frac{V\ell B}{Rg} = \frac{(175\mathrm{V})(0.600\mathrm{m})(1.50\mathrm{T})}{(5.00\Omega)(9.80\mathrm{m/s^2})} = 3.21\mathrm{kg}$

questions.questionText: 20.75. The maximum current  $I$  is the current that results in an upward magnetic force  $F_{B}$  that equals the weight  $mg$  of the bar.  $I = \frac{V}{R}$ .  $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$ , with  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $F_{B} = mg$ .  $I\ell B = mg$ .  $\frac{V\ell B}{R} = mg$ .  $V = \frac{mgR}{\ell B} = \frac{(0.750\mathrm{kg})(9.80\mathrm{m / s^2})(25.0\Omega)}{(0.500\mathrm{m})(0.450\mathrm{T})} = 817\mathrm{V}$

Reflect: The current required to lift the bar is quite large, about 33 A. For a bar of less mass, less current would be required to lift the bar off the supports and break the circuit.

^{}[]

questions.questionText: 20.76. The magnitude of the magnetic force on a straight wire is $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$, where $\ell = 3.0\,\mathrm{m} - 2r = 3.0\,\mathrm{m} - 2(0.95\,\mathrm{m}) = 1.1\,\mathrm{m}$ and $\phi = 90^{\circ}$. The magnetic field at the center of a wire loop carrying a current $I$ is $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The force on the straight sections of the wire is $F = I\ell B\sin \phi = (4.0\,\mathrm{A})(1.1\,\mathrm{m})(2.20\,\mathrm{T})\sin 90^{\circ} = 9.7\,\mathrm{N}$. By the right-hand rule, the direction of the force is to the left.

(b) At the center of the hemisphere, the magnetic field is half of that produced by a complete loop. Thus, $B = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}\right) = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A})(4.0\,\mathrm{A})}{4(0.95\,\mathrm{m})} = 1.3 \times 10^{-6}\,\mathrm{T}$. By the right-hand rule, the field points into the page.

Reflect: Notice that the curve has no effect on the net force on the total length of the wire. All forces in the vertical direction cancel out. Thus, the net force is the same as if the wire were simply a straight wire $3.00\,\mathrm{m}$ long oriented along the $y$ axis.

questions.questionText: 20.77. The ion has charge $q = +e$. After being accelerated through a potential difference $V$ the ion has kinetic energy $qV$. The acceleration in the circular path is $\upsilon^2 / R$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $K = qV = +eV$. $\frac{1}{2} m\upsilon^2 = eV$ and

$$
\upsilon = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2(1.60 \times 10^{-19}\,\mathrm{C})(220\,\mathrm{V})}{1.16 \times 10^{-26}\,\mathrm{kg}}} = 7.79 \times 10^4\,\mathrm{m/s}. \quad F_B = |q|\upsilon B \sin \phi. \quad \phi = 90^{\circ}
$$

$\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ gives $|q|\upsilon B = m\frac{\upsilon^2}{R}$.

$$
R = \frac{m\upsilon}{|q|B} = \frac{(1.16 \times 10^{-26}\,\mathrm{kg})(7.79 \times 10^4\,\mathrm{m/s})}{(1.60 \times 10^{-19}\,\mathrm{C})(0.723\,\mathrm{T})} = 7.81 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m} = 7.81\,\mathrm{mm}
$$

questions.questionText: 20.78. The magnetic field of a long straight wire is given by $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$. The magnetic field at the center of a circular loop is $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$. Let 1 refer to the wire and let 2 refer to the loop. The center of the loop is a distance $r = 3.25\,\mathrm{cm}$ from the wire. By the right-hand rule, at the center of the loop the magnetic field $\vec{B}_1$ due to the wire is directed into the page and the magnetic field $\vec{B}_2$ due to the loop is directed out of the page.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi r} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A})(10.0\,\mathrm{A})}{2\pi(3.25 \times 10^{-2}\,\mathrm{m})} = 6.15 \times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{T}$

$$
B_2 = \frac{\mu_0 I_2}{2R} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A})(12.0\,\mathrm{A})}{2(3.25 \times 10^{-2}\,\mathrm{m})} = 2.32 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{T}
$$

Since $\vec{B}_1$ and $\vec{B}_2$ are in opposite directions, $B = B_2 - B_1 = 1.70 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{T}$. $\vec{B}_2$ is larger than $\vec{B}_1$ so the net field is in the direction of $\vec{B}_2$ out of the page.

questions.questionText:
20.79. The direction of the current is shown in the figure below. The direction of $\vec{B}$ is given by the right-hand rule. $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$.



![img-79.jpeg](img-79.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Below the line the magnetic field is toward the east.

$$
B = \frac {(4 \pi \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {T} \cdot \mathrm {m / A}) (8 0 0 \mathrm {A})}{2 \pi (5 . 5 0 \mathrm {m})} = 2. 9 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {T}
$$

(b) The field due to the current is the same order of magnitude as the earth's field so it is a problem.

questions.questionText: 20.80. The magnitude of the magnetic force on a straight wire is  $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$ , so a plot of force versus current would have a  $y$  intercept of zero and a slope of  $s = \ell B\sin \phi$ . For this experiment,  $\ell = 0.15\mathrm{m}$  and  $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The plot is shown in the figure below.

![img-80.jpeg](img-80.jpeg)

The slope of the best-fit line is  $s = 0.01187 \, \mathrm{N / A}$ . The magnitude of the magnetic field is thus

$$
B = \frac {s}{\ell \sin \phi} = \frac {0 . 0 1 1 8 7 \mathrm {N / A}}{(0 . 1 5 \mathrm {m}) \sin 9 0 ^ {\circ}} = 0. 0 7 9 \mathrm {T}
$$

Reflect: The error in the slope is  $0.00068\mathrm{N / A}$ , or about  $6\%$ .

questions.questionText: 20.81. $B = \frac{\mu_{\mathrm{e}}I}{2\pi r}$ . The direction of  $\vec{B}$  is given by the right-hand rule.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The currents are the same so points where the two fields are equal in magnitude are equidistant from the two wires. The net field is zero along the dashed line shown in Figure (a) below.



![img-81.jpeg](img-81.jpeg)
(a)

![img-82.jpeg](img-82.jpeg)
(b)

![img-83.jpeg](img-83.jpeg)
(c)

(b) For the magnitudes of the two fields to be the same at a point, the point must be 3 times closer to the wire with the smaller current. The net field is zero along the dashed line shown in Figure (b) above.
(c) As in (a), the points are equidistant from both wires. The net field is zero along the dashed line shown in Figure (c) above.

**Reflect:** The lines of zero net field consist of points at which the fields of the two wires have opposite directions and equal magnitudes.

questions.questionText:
20.82. The current in the bar is horizontal toward the bottom of the figure, so the magnetic force on it is vertically upwards (out of the page). The net force on the bar is equal to the magnetic force minus the gravitational force, so Newton’s second law gives the acceleration. The bar is in parallel with the $10.0\,\Omega$ resistor, so we must use circuit analysis to find the initial current through it. First find the current. The equivalent resistance across the battery is $30.0\,\Omega$, so the total current is $4.00\,\mathrm{A}$, half of which goes through the bar. Applying Newton’s second law to the bar gives $\sum F = ma = F_g - mg = i\ell B - mg$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** Solving for the acceleration gives

$$
a = \frac{i\ell B - mg}{m} = \frac{(2.0\,\mathrm{A})(1.50\,\mathrm{m})(1.60\,\mathrm{T}) - 3.00\,\mathrm{N}}{(3.00\,\mathrm{N}/9.80\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2)} = 5.88\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2
$$

The direction is upward.

**Reflect:** Once the bar is free of the conducting wires, its acceleration will become $9.8\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2$ downward since only gravity will be acting on it.

questions.questionText:
20.83. The wires repel each other since they carry currents in opposite directions, so the wires will move away from each other until the magnetic force is just balanced by the force due to the spring. Call $x$ the distance the springs each stretch. The force of the spring is $kx$ and the magnetic force on each wire is $F_{\mathrm{mag}} = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \frac{l^2 \ell}{x}$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** On each wire, $F_{\mathrm{spr}} = F_{\mathrm{mag}}$, and there are two spring forces on each wire. Therefore $2kx = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \frac{l^2 \ell}{x}$, which gives $x = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \frac{l^2 \ell}{k}}$.

**Reflect:** Since $\mu_0 / 2\pi$ is small, $x$ will probably be much less than the length of the wires.

^{}[]

questions.questionText: 20.84. Turning the charged loop creates a current, and the external magnetic field exerts a torque on that current. The current is $I = q / T = q / (1 / f) = qf = q(\omega / 2\pi) = q\omega / 2\pi$. The torque is $\tau = \mu B\sin \phi$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: In this case, $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ and $\mu = AB$, giving $\tau = IAB$. Combining the results for the torque and current and using $A = \pi r^2$ gives $\tau = \left(\frac{q\omega}{2\pi}\right)\pi r^2 B = \frac{1}{2} q\omega r^2 B$.

Reflect: Any moving charge creates a current—so turning the loop creates a current, which in turn causes a magnetic field.

questions.questionText:
20.85. Label the three segments in the magnetic field 1, 2, and 3, as shown in Figure (a) below. The force on a current carrying conductor is $F = I\ell B\sin \phi$, where $\phi$ is the angle between the direction of the current and the direction of the magnetic field. The direction of the force on each segment is given by the right-hand rule and is shown in the figure. The sum of $\vec{F}_1$ and $\vec{F}_3$ is the same as the force $\vec{F}_{13}$ on a wire $0.307\mathrm{m}$ long. Section 2 has length $0.800\mathrm{m}$. The current in each segment is perpendicular to the magnetic field, so $\phi = 90^{\circ}$.

![img-84.jpeg](img-84.jpeg)
(a)

![img-85.jpeg](img-85.jpeg)
(b)

![img-86.jpeg](img-86.jpeg)
(c)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $F_{13} = I\ell B\sin \phi = (6.50\mathrm{A})(0.307\mathrm{m})(0.280\mathrm{T})\sin 90^{\circ} = 0.559\mathrm{N}$

$F_{2} = I\ell B\sin \phi = (6.50\mathrm{A})(0.800\mathrm{m})(0.280\mathrm{T})\sin 90^{\circ} = 1.46\mathrm{N}$. The forces and a coordinate system are shown in Figure (b) above. $\vec{F}_2$ has been resolved into its $x$ and $y$ components.

$$
F _ {x} = F _ {2 x} + F _ {1 3 x} = - F _ {2} \cos 6 0. 0 ^ {\circ} = - (1. 4 6 \mathrm {N}) (\cos 6 0. 0 ^ {\circ}) = - 0. 7 3 0 \mathrm {N}
$$

$$
F _ {y} = F _ {2 y} + F _ {1 3 y} = F _ {2} \sin 6 0. 0 ^ {\circ} + F _ {1 3} = + (1. 4 6 \mathrm {N}) (\sin 6 0. 0 ^ {\circ}) + 0. 5 5 9 \mathrm {N} = + 1. 8 3 \mathrm {N}
$$

$F_{x}$, $F_{y}$ and the resultant total force $\vec{F}$ are shown in Figure (c). The resultant force has magnitude $1.97\mathrm{N}$ and is at $68.3^{\circ}$ clockwise from the straight left-hand segment.



# Solutions to Passage Problems

questions.questionText: 20.86. The torque on a magnetic dipole is given by $\tau = \mu B \sin \phi$. The magnetic moment of the proton is $1.4 \times 10^{-26} \, \mathrm{J/T} = 1.4 \times 10^{-26} \, \mathrm{N \cdot m/T}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: Using the given values we have $\tau = \mu B \sin \phi = (1.4 \times 10^{-26} \, \mathrm{N \cdot m/T})(2 \, \mathrm{T}) \sin 90^{\circ} = 2.8 \times 10^{-26} \, \mathrm{N \cdot m}$. Thus, the correct answer is C.

questions.questionText: 20.87. To be a candidate for MRI, the net magnetic moment of the nucleus must not be zero.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: Since the protons and neutrons will tend to pair with opposite orientations to their magnetic moments, only nuclei with an odd number of nucleons (protons and neutrons) are candidates for MRI. The only one of the listed isotopes with an odd number of nucleons is $^{31}\mathrm{P}_{15}$. The correct answer is D.

questions.questionText: 20.88. The resistance of the nerve is given by $R = \rho \frac{\ell}{A}$, and the current in the nerve may be found from Ohm's law $V = IR$. The force due to the magnetic field is $F = I\ell B \sin \phi$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The force on the nerve is

$$
F = I \ell B \sin \phi = \frac{V}{R} \ell B \sin \phi = \frac{V A}{\rho \ell} \ell B \sin \phi = \frac{V \pi R^2}{\rho} B \sin \phi = \frac{(0.100 \, \mathrm{V}) \pi (0.75 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m})^2 (2 \, \mathrm{T}) \sin 90^{\circ}}{0.6 \, \Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}} = 6 \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{N}
$$

The correct answer is A.

Reflect: Note that the use of the equation $F = I\ell B \sin \phi$ is dubious because the nerve is wider than it is long, which is not the geometry for which this equation was derived.

questions.questionText:
20.89. At the center of a solenoid, the magnetic field is $B = \mu_0 \frac{N}{L} I$. The number $N$ of turns is

$$
N = \frac{40 \, \mathrm{cm}}{0.010 \, \mathrm{cm}} = 4000.
$$
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: To obtain a magnetic field of $B = 150 \, \mu\mathrm{T}$ requires a current of $I = \frac{BL}{\mu_0 N} = \frac{(150 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{T})(0.40 \, \mathrm{m})}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T \cdot m/A})(4000)} = 0.12 \, \mathrm{A}$. The correct answer is B.

questions.questionText: 20.90. At the center of a solenoid, the magnetic field is $B = \mu_0 \frac{N}{L} I$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: The magnetic field at the center of a solenoid is independent of its radius. The current is the same in the two solenoids, as is the number of turns per unit length $(N/L)$, so the maximum torque experienced by the bacteria will be the same in the two solenoids. The correct answer is C.

questions.questionText: 20.91. The magnetic field is a vector.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: If the solenoid is aligned so that its magnetic field is parallel to earth's magnetic field, the bacteria experience a maximum magnetic field of $150 \, \mu\mathrm{T} + 50 \, \mu\mathrm{T} = 200 \, \mu\mathrm{T}$. In the opposite orientation, the bacteria experience a maximum magnetic field of $150 \, \mu\mathrm{T} - 50 \, \mu\mathrm{T} = 100 \, \mu\mathrm{T}$. The correct answer is C.

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q1: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
1. The figure below shows a circular loop on the left carrying an increasing current $I$, which generates an increasing magnetic field $\hat{B}$ (only one of many flux lines is drawn). The magnetic field line goes through the neighboring ring, causing an increase in the magnetic flux through the ring. To counter this increase in magnetic flux, the induced current $I'$ in the ring must flow in the same direction as in the loop, as shown in the drawing. This will create a magnetic field pointing upward inside the ring, thereby reducing the total magnetic flux through the ring. Were the current in the loop decreasing, the magnetic flux in the ring due to this current would decrease, so the induced current $I'$ would flow in the opposite direction around the ring in order to augment the magnetic flux through the ring.

![img-87.jpeg](img-87.jpeg)

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q2: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
2. Think of the metal tube as a solenoid with many coils. As the magnet falls down the tube, it increases the magnetic flux through the coils below it and decreases the magnetic flux through the coils above it. This induces currents in these coils to counter the change in flux. In the coils above (below) the magnet, where the magnetic flux is decreasing (increasing), Lenz's law tells us that the induced currents will produce a magnetic field in the same (opposite) direction as that of the receding magnet, thereby attracting (repelling) the magnet and slowing its fall. Thus, the induced currents both above and below the magnet act to slow the fall of the magnet.

You can arrive at the same conclusion from energy considerations. In free space, the work done by the gravitational force is converted entirely into the kinetic energy of the falling magnet (ignoring air resistance). However, when falling through the metal tube, some of the work done by the gravitational force must be converted into emf to drive the induced currents, leaving less energy available to be converted into kinetic energy of the falling magnet. Thus, the magnet falls more slowly through the metal tube.

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q3: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
3. The magnetic field lines produced by the long straight wire lie in planes perpendicular to the wire, which means that they are perpendicular to the normal to the plane of the metal ring. Thus, the angle $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ between the magnetic



field and the normal to the plane of the ring, so the flux through the ring is  $\Phi_B = BA\cos \phi = 0$  (see Eq. 21.2). Thus, increasing the current in the ring will not change the magnetic flux through the ring, so it will not induce a current in the ring.

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q4: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 4. Equation 21.12 gives the self-inductance as  $L = \left|\frac{N\Phi_B}{i}\right| = \left|\frac{N(\pi r^2)B}{i}\right|$  where the second equality is for a coil with cross-sectional area  $\pi r^2$ . From Eq. 20.14, the magnetic field at the center of such a coil is  $B = \mu_0ni = \frac{\mu_0Ni}{l}$ . Taking this to be the magnetic field everywhere inside the coil (which is a good approximation for a coil with many loops) gives  $L = \frac{\mu_0N^2\pi r^2}{l}$ , which is a strictly geometrical factor, as it should be. If the radii of the two coils are related by  $r_1 = 2r_2$ , then their self-inductances are  $L_1 = \frac{\mu_0N^2\pi r_1^2}{l} = \frac{\mu_0N^2\pi(4r_2^2)}{l} = 4L_2$ .

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q5: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 5. Energy is stored in the magnetic field of inductors. The energy stored in an inductor with self-inductance  $L$  is  $U = \frac{1}{2} LI^2$  (Eq. 21.18), where  $L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 \pi r^2}{l}$  for a solenoid. Storing maximum energy would thus require a solenoid with the maximum number  $N$  of turns, with maximum length  $l$ , and with maximum cross-sectional area  $\pi r^2$ . However, the power dissipated in a solenoid is  $P = I^2 R$ , where  $R$  is the resistance of the solenoid wire. Thus, the power lost would be proportional to the energy stored, which is not an efficient manner to store energy. For comparison, a capacitor can store an energy  $U = \frac{1}{2} CV^2$  indefinitely (in theory) with no power loss  $(I = 0)$ .

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q6: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 6. The emf induced in the coils of a transformer is proportional to the rate of change in the magnetic flux through the coils. A dc current is a steady, non-changing current, so it would produce only a constant magnetic flux through the primary and secondary coils, which would induce zero emf in the secondary coil.

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q7: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 7. No, Lenz's law says that the current in a metal loop will flow to oppose any change in magnetic flux through the loop. As explained in CQ 21.1, the induced current increases (decreases) the magnetic flux if another source is causing the flux to decrease (increase).

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q8: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 8. No, Faraday's law states that the magnitude  $\mathcal{E}$  of the induced emf in a circuit equals the absolute value of the time rate of change of magnetic flux through the circuit:  $\mathcal{E} = \left|\Delta \Phi_B / \Delta t\right|$  (Eq. 21.3).

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q9: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
9. The figure below shows an airplane seen from above flying through an upward-pointing magnetic field  $\vec{B}$ . As explained by Figure 20.10, the magnetic force  $\vec{F}$  on positive charges moving at the velocity  $\vec{v}$  of the airplane would point to the right as seen by a passenger facing forward in the plane. This force would make some excess positive charge accumulate on the right wingtip and excess negative charge accumulate on the left wingtip. Thus, an electric field would point from right to left across the airplane, and the right wingtip would be at a higher potential than the left wingtip. This result does not depend on the direction in which the airplane is flying.



![img-88.jpeg](img-88.jpeg)

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q10: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 10. A capacitor stores energy in the electric field between its plates, which is formed by the charges stored on the plates. Analogously, an inductor stores energy in the magnetic field through the inductor, which is formed by the current through the inductor. Both devices require a source of energy to input energy in the respective fields; for a capacitor, this is a voltage source, for an inductor, this is a current source.

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q11: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
11. As explained in the text accompanying Figure 21.20, the net magnetic force exerted on a loop moving into or out of a magnetic field acts to prevent this motion. Thus, you must supply a greater force to accelerate the ring into or out of the magnetic field. You must exert this force over a distance, so you do work to move the ring into or out of the magnetic field.

Considering the changing magnetic flux in the ring leads to the same conclusion. As you remove (insert) the ring from (into) the magnetic field, the magnetic flux through the ring decreases (increases), so Faraday's law tells you that a current will be induced in the ring to produce a magnetic field to augment (reduce) the decreasing (increasing) external magnetic flux. This induced magnetic field will store energy  $U = \frac{1}{2} LI^2$ , where  $I$  is the current induced in the ring of resistance  $R$ :  $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \left|\frac{\Delta\Phi_R}{\Delta t}\right| \frac{1}{R}$ . This energy is supplied by the work done to remove (insert) the ring from (into) the magnetic field.

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q12: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
12. The current through a battery-driven  $R-L$  circuit is given by Eq. 21.24,  $i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} \left(1 - e^{-(R / L)t}\right)$ . The current through a battery-driven  $R-C$  circuit is given by Eq. 19.17,  $i = I_0 e^{-t / RC}$ . The graph below shows the currents through each circuit as a function of time.

(a)  $R-L$  circuit. Just after the switch is closed, the inductor acts as an open circuit because the rapid rate of change in the current induces an emf across the inductor that is essentially equal to the battery emf  $\mathcal{E}$  (see circuit at lower left in drawing below). Thus, almost zero emf is available to drive a current through the resistor, so the current in the circuit is very small, as shown by the blue curve in the graph near the time origin.

^{}[]

R-C circuit. Just after the switch is closed, the capacitor acts as a short circuit because the current is easily transferred across it as it begins to charge up (see circuit at upper left in drawing below). This is shown by the red curve in the graph below near the time origin.

(b) R-L circuit. Long after the switch is closed, the current has reached the steady state so there is no emf across the inductor and it acts as a short circuit (see circuit in upper right in drawing below).

R-C circuit. Long after the switch is closed, the capacitor is fully charged, so it cannot transfer any current across it and therefore acts as an open circuit (see circuit in lower right in drawing below).

![img-89.jpeg](img-89.jpeg)

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q13: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 13. The ratio of the number $N_{1}$ of primary turns to the number $N_{2}$ of secondary turns is given by Eq. 21.14, $\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} = \frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}$, where $V_{1}$ is the primary voltage and $V_{2}$ is the secondary voltage. Using $V_{1} = 120\mathrm{V}$ and $V_{2} = 7500\mathrm{V}$ gives $\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{120\mathrm{V}}{7500\mathrm{V}}\approx \frac{1}{60}$.

questions.questionText: Chapter 21 — Conceptual Q14: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
14. The earth's magnetic field is about $B = 5.5 \times 10^{-5}$ T. The motional emf is given by Eq. 21.6, $\mathcal{E} = vBL$, where $v$ is the speed of the object and $L$ is its length. Assume that the earth's magnetic field is parallel to the surface of the earth and that the skydiver is oriented vertically. Given $v = 50 \, \text{m/s}$ and $L = 6 \, \text{ft} = 2 \, \text{m}$, the maximum motional emf that could develop along her body is

$$
\mathcal {E} = v B L \approx (5 0 \mathrm {m} / \mathrm {s}) \left(5. 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {T}\right) (2 \mathrm {m}) = 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {V}.
$$

questions.questionText: 21.1. $\Phi_{\pi} = BA\cos \phi$. The normal to the loop is in the $z$ direction. The loop has area $A = \pi r^2 = \pi (6.50\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m})^2 = 1.33\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m}^2$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\phi = 0^{\circ}$. $\Phi_B = BA = (0.230\ \mathrm{T})(1.33 \times 10^{-2}\ \mathrm{m}^2) = 3.06 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{Wb}$

(b) $\phi = 53.1^{\circ}$. $\Phi_B = BA\cos 53.1^{\circ} = (3.06\times 10^{3}\ \mathrm{Wb})\cos 53.1^{\circ} = 1.84\times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{Wb}$

(c) $\phi = 90^{\circ}$. $\Phi_B = 0$

Reflect: The flux is a maximum when the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop and the flux is zero when the magnetic field is parallel to the plane of the loop.

questions.questionText: 21.2. $\Phi_B = BA\cos \phi$, where $\phi$ is the angle between $\vec{B}$ and the normal to the surface.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The normal to the surface is parallel to the $x$ axis. $\phi = 90^{\circ}$ and $\Phi_B = 0$.

(b) The normal to the surface is parallel to the $z$ axis and $\phi = 0^{\circ}$. $\Phi_B = (0.128\ \mathrm{T})(0.300\ \mathrm{m})^2 = 0.0115\ \mathrm{Wb}$. The flux is into the enclosed volume.

(c) The normal to the surface and the direction of $\vec{B}$ are shown in the figure below.

$\tan \phi = \frac{40.0\ \mathrm{cm}}{30.0\ \mathrm{cm}}$ and $\phi = 53.1^{\circ}$.

$\Phi_B = BA\cos \phi = (0.128\ \mathrm{T})(0.300\ \mathrm{m})(0.500\ \mathrm{m})\cos 53.1^{\circ} = 0.0115\ \mathrm{Wb}$. The flux is out of the shaded volume.

(d) The net flux is zero since the magnitude of the flux into the volume equals the magnitude of the flux out of the volume.

![img-90.jpeg](img-90.jpeg)

questions.questionText: 21.3. The total flux through the jar with its lid on is zero because it is a closed surface. The total flux through the jar-plus-lid combination is the flux through the jar plus the flux through the lid, so the sum of these must be zero. $\Phi_{\mathrm{jar}} + \Phi_{\mathrm{lid}} = 0$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The flux through the jar without the lid is on is

$$
\Phi_{\mathrm{jar}} = - \Phi_{\mathrm{lid}} = - BA \cos \phi = - B (\pi r^2) \cos \phi = - (1.75\ \mathrm{T}) \pi (2.50 \times 10^{-2}\ \mathrm{m})^2 \cos 0^{\circ} = 3.44 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{Wb}.
$$

(b) The flux through the jar with the lid on is the same as the flux through the jar with the lid off: $3.44 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{Wb}$.

Reflect: The flux through the jar-plus-lid combination would be zero because the flux entering the combination would be the same as the flux exiting the combination.

questions.questionText: 21.4. $\mathcal{E} = \left|\frac{\Delta\Phi_B}{\Delta t}\right|$, $\Phi_B = BA\cos \phi$, $\phi = 0^{\circ}$, $A$ is constant and $B$ is changing.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\mathcal{E} = A\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t} = (0.0900\mathrm{m}^2)(0.190\mathrm{T / s}) = 0.0171\mathrm{V}$

(b) $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \frac{0.0171\mathrm{V}}{0.600\Omega} = 0.0285\mathrm{A}$

questions.questionText: 21.5. $\mathcal{E} = N\left|\frac{\Delta\Phi_B}{\Delta t}\right|$. $\Phi_B = BA\cos \phi$. $\phi = 0^\circ$, $A$ is constant and $B$ is changing.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\mathcal{E} = N\left|\frac{\Delta\Phi_B}{\Delta t}\right| = NA\left|\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}\right|$, $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \frac{NA|\Delta B / \Delta t|}{R}$.

$$
\left| \frac {\Delta B}{\Delta t} \right| = \frac {R I}{N A} = \frac {(4 0 . 0 \Omega) (0 . 1 5 0 \mathrm {A})}{(2 0 0) \pi (3 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {m}) ^ {2}} = 1 0. 6 \mathrm {T / s}.
$$

questions.questionText: 21.6. $\mathcal{E} = \left|\frac{\Delta\Phi_B}{\Delta t}\right|$. $\Phi_B = BA\cos \phi$. $\phi_{i} = 0^{\circ}$. $\phi_{i} = 90^{\circ}$. $\Phi_B$ is the flux through each turn of the coil.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\Phi_{B,1} = BA\cos 0^{\circ} = (6.0\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{T})(12\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{m}^{2})(1) = 7.2\times 10^{-8}\mathrm{Wb}$. The total flux through the coil is $N\Phi_{B,1} = (200)(7.2\times 10^{-8}\mathrm{Wb}) = 1.44\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{Wb}$. $\Phi_{B,1} = BA\cos 90^{\circ} = 0$.

(b) $\mathcal{E} = \left|\frac{N\Phi_L - N\Phi_T}{\Delta t}\right| = \frac{1.44\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{Wb}}{0.040\mathrm{s}} = 3.6\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{V} = 0.36\mathrm{mV}$

questions.questionText: 21.7. $\mathcal{E} = \left|\frac{\Delta\Phi_B}{\Delta t}\right|$. $\Phi_B = BA\cos \phi$. $\phi$ is the angle between the normal to the loop and $\vec{B}$, so $\phi_{i} = 90.0^{\circ} - 37.0^{\circ} = 53.0^{\circ}$ and $\phi_{i} = 0^{\circ}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\mathcal{E} = \frac{NBA|\cos\phi_i - \cos\phi_i|}{\Delta t} = \frac{(80)(1.10\mathrm{T})(0.250\mathrm{m})(0.400\mathrm{m})}{0.0600\mathrm{s}} |\cos 0^{\circ} - \cos 53.0^{\circ}| = 58.4\mathrm{V}$

Reflect: The flux changes because the orientation of the coil relative to the magnetic field changes.

questions.questionText: 21.8. $\mathcal{E} = N\left|\frac{\Delta\Phi_B}{\Delta t}\right|$ $\Phi_B = BA\cos \phi$. $B_{i} = \mu_{0}nI$ and $B_{i} = 0$. $\phi = 0^{\circ}$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) For $N = 5$ and $r = 2.00\mathrm{cm}$, the induced emf is

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathcal {E} = N \left| \frac {\Delta \Phi_ {B}}{\Delta t} \right| = N \left| \frac {\left(B _ {r} - B _ {r}\right) A \cos \phi}{\Delta t} \right| = N \left| \frac {\left(0 - \mu_ {0} n I\right) \pi r ^ {2} \cos \phi}{\Delta t} \right| \\
= (5) \left| \frac {\left(4 \pi \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {V} \cdot \mathrm {s} / \mathrm {A} \cdot \mathrm {m}\right) \left(4 0 0 0 \mathrm {m} ^ {- 1}\right) (0 . 2 5 0 \mathrm {A}) \pi \left(2 . 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {m}\right) ^ {2} \cos 0 ^ {\circ}}{0 . 5 \mathrm {s}} \right| \\
= 1 6 \mu \mathrm {V} \\
\end{array}
$$

(b) For $N = 10$ and $r = 4.00\mathrm{cm}$, the induced emf is

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathcal {E} = (1 0) \frac {\left(4 \pi \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {V} \cdot \mathrm {s} / \mathrm {A} \cdot \mathrm {m}\right) \left(4 0 0 0 \mathrm {m} ^ {- 1}\right) (0 . 2 5 0 \mathrm {A}) \pi \left(4 . 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {m}\right) ^ {2} \cos 0 ^ {\circ}}{0 . 5 \mathrm {s}} \\
= 1. 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {V} \\
\end{array}
$$

Reflect: Outside of the inner solenoid, the $B$ field is in the opposite direction. However, we ignore this aspect here because the inner solenoid is said to be "very" long. The $B$ field outside a solenoid goes to zero as the solenoid gets longer.

questions.questionText: 21.9. Since the field is uniform, $\Phi_B = BA\cos\phi$. Since the plane of the circuit is perpendicular to the field, $\phi = 0^\circ$ and $\cos\phi = 1$. $\mathcal{E} = \left|\frac{\Delta\Phi_B}{\Delta t}\right|$. $A$ is constant and $B$ is changing, so $\mathcal{E} = A\left|\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}\right|$ and $\left|\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}\right| = 0.25$ T/s. The current in the circuit is given by Ohm’s law, $\mathcal{E} = IR$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\mathcal{E} = (0.300\mathrm{m})(0.600\mathrm{m})(0.25\mathrm{T / s}) = 0.045\mathrm{V}$. $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \frac{0.045\mathrm{V}}{15\Omega} = 3.0\mathrm{mA}$.

Reflect: The induced emf depends on the rate of change of the flux so it depends on the rate at which $B$ is changing. It does not depend on the initial magnitude of the field.

questions.questionText: 21.10. Apply Faraday’s law. Since the loop initially rests on the table and the magnetic field is perpendicular to the table, we may assume that the initial magnetic flux is simply $\Phi_B = EA\cos\phi = EA$. The final flux is zero since the loop is removed from the field.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\mathcal{E} = \left|\frac{\Delta\Phi_B}{\Delta t}\right| = \left|\frac{0 - (1.5\mathrm{T})\pi(0.120\mathrm{m})^2}{2.0\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{s}}\right| = 34\mathrm{V}$

(b) According to Lenz’s law, the induced current will attempt to maintain the upward magnetic field in the loop; thus, the induced current is counterclockwise.

Reflect: The shorter the removal time, the larger the average induced emf.

questions.questionText: 21.11. Apply Lenz’s law, which states that the induced emf will oppose any change.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The magnetic field is increasing, so the emf will create a current to oppose that change. Thus the B field resulting from this induced current will point to the left. Using the right-hand rule, we see that this current must be from left to right.

(b) The current will be from right to left.

(c) The $B$ field is perpendicular to the area vector (i.e., $\phi = 90^{\circ}$), so there is no flux inside the windings. Thus, the flux does not change regardless of the $B$ field, so the current induced in through the resistor is zero.

Reflect: The induced emf will always oppose any changes in the circuit.

questions.questionText: 21.12. The field of the induced current is directed to oppose the change in flux.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The field is into the page and is increasing, so the flux is increasing. The field of the induced current is out of the page. To produce field out of the page, the induced current is counterclockwise.

(b) The field is into the page and is decreasing, so the flux is decreasing. The field of the induced current is into the page. To produce field into the page, the induced current is clockwise.

(c) The field is constant, so the flux is constant and there is no induced emf and no induced current.

questions.questionText: 21.13. The field of the induced current is directed to oppose the change in flux in coil $B$. When $S$ is closed the magnetic field of coil $A$ is directed to the right. The field of $A$ is stronger at points closer to $A$. The field of $A$ is proportional to the current in $A$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The flux due to field directed to the right decreases (goes to zero) when $S$ is opened. The field due to the induced current is to the right inside of coil $B$. To produce field in this direction, the induced current passes through the resistor from $a$ to $b$.

(b) The flux through coil $B$ due to the field of $A$ increases, so the field due to the induced current is to the left inside of coil $B$. To produce field in this direction, the induced current passes through the resistor from $b$ to $a$.

(c) Decreasing $R$ causes the current in coil $A$ to increase, so the field of $A$ increases. The flux through coil $B$ due to the field of $A$ increases, so the field due to the induced current is to the left inside of coil $B$. To produce field in this direction, the induced current passes through the resistor from $b$ to $a$.

questions.questionText: 21.14. Use the right-hand rule to determine the direction of the magnetic field in the solenoid and use Lenz’s law to determine the direction of the induced current in the ring.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Based on the figure, we can deduce that the magnetic field of the solenoid points toward the ring. As the solenoid moves upward, the magnitude of the flux in the ring increases. As seen from the solenoid, the induced current must then move counterclockwise to oppose the increase in flux. If the solenoid stops moving toward the ring and its current decreases, the magnetic flux in the ring will begin to decrease. To oppose this, the induced current in the ring will now flow in the clockwise direction as seen from the solenoid.

Reflect: When the flux in the ring is increasing, the currents in the ring and solenoid are in opposite directions—this creates a repulsive magnetic force as expected by Lenz's law. Similarly, when the flux in the ring is decreasing, the currents in the ring and solenoid are in the same direction, which produces an attractive magnetic force.

questions.questionText: 21.15. The magnetic force on a + charge in the moving bar is given by the right-hand rule. The direction of the current is in the direction of this force. Lenz's law says the field of the induced current opposes the change in flux through the circuit.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The directions of  $\vec{v}$  and  $\vec{B}$  are shown in the figure below. The magnetic force is toward the top of the bar, so the induced current is from  $b$  to  $a$  through the bar and counterclockwise in the circuit.

![img-91.jpeg](img-91.jpeg)

(b) The magnetic field is into the page and the flux is increasing as the area of the circuit increases. The magnetic field of the induced current therefore is out of the page inside the circuit. To produce a magnetic field in this direction, the induced current must be counterclockwise.

questions.questionText: 21.16. Use the right-hand rule to find the direction of the magnetic field due to the long wire at the location of each loop. Lenz's law says that the magnetic field of the induced current is directed to oppose the change in flux through the circuit.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The magnetic field of the long wire is directed out of the page at  $C$  and into the page at  $A$ . When the current increases, the magnetic field increases. Therefore, the magnetic field of the induced current in loop  $C$  is directed into the page inside the loop, to oppose the increase in flux out of the page due to the current in the long wire. To produce magnetic field in this direction, the induced current in  $C$  is clockwise. The magnetic field of the induced current in loop  $A$  is directed out of the page inside the loop, to oppose the increase in flux into the page due to the current in the long wire. To produce magnetic field in this direction the induced current in  $A$  is counterclockwise.

Reflect: If the direction of  $I$  were reversed the directions of the induced currents would reverse. If  $I$  were decreasing rather than increasing, the directions of the induced currents would reverse.

questions.questionText:
21.17. The magnetic field of the bar magnet is directed into the  $S$  pole so is toward the top of the page in the figure in the problem. The loop and magnetic field of the bar magnet, as viewed from below the loop, are shown in the figure below. Lenz's law says that the magnetic field of the induced current is directed to oppose the change in flux through the circuit.



![img-92.jpeg](img-92.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The strength of the magnetic field decreases with distance from the coil. When the coil is dropped it moves away from the bar magnet and the flux through the coil decreases and to oppose this decrease the flux due to the induced current is into the page. To produce magnetic field inside the coil that is into the page, the induced current must be clockwise.

(b) When the magnet is dropped, it moves toward the coil and the flux through the coil increases. To oppose this increase, the flux due to the induced current is out of the page. To produce magnetic field inside the coil that is out of the page, the induced current must be counterclockwise.
(c) Both objects fall at the same rate. The distance between them doesn't change. The flux through the coil doesn't change and there is no current induced in the coil.

questions.questionText: 21.18. By Lenz's law, the induced current flows to oppose the flux change that caused it.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Below the wire, the magnetic field is out of the page. The magnetic field grows with time, so the induced current must create a magnetic field that points into the page. By the right-hand rule, this induced current must be clockwise.

Reflect: Careful! Lenz's law does not say that the induced current flows to oppose the magnetic flux. Instead, it says that the current flows to oppose the change in flux.

questions.questionText:
21.19. For a bar magnet the magnetic field is directed into the south pole and out of the north pole. The loop and the current induced in it, as viewed by the observer, are shown in the figure below. Lenz's law says that the magnetic field of the induced current is directed to oppose the change in flux through the loop.

![img-93.jpeg](img-93.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: The magnetic field of the induced current is directed out of the page inside the loop. The magnitude of the flux decreases when the bar magnet moves away from the loop. Since the induced flux tends to oppose this decrease, the flux due to the bar magnet is directed out of the page. The magnetic field of the bar magnet is away from pole  $C$ , so pole  $C$  is a north pole and pole  $A$  is a south pole.

questions.questionText:
21.20. The bar and magnetic field are shown in the figure below.  $V_{ab} = vBL$ . To determine which end is at higher potential consider the direction of the magnetic force on a positive charge in the moving rod. Accumulation of positive charge produces higher potential.

![img-94.jpeg](img-94.jpeg)

![img-95.jpeg](img-95.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $V_{ab} = vBL = (11.5 \, \mathrm{m/s})(1.22 \, \mathrm{T})(0.150 \, \mathrm{m}) = 2.10 \, \mathrm{V}$

(b) The magnetic force on a positive charge in the bar is eastward. Positive charge accumulates at end  $a$  of the bar and that end (the east end) is at a higher potential.
(c) If the bar is moving east to west the force is to the north. The force is perpendicular to the bar and there is no charge accumulation at its ends; the potential difference between its ends is zero.

Reflect: If the bar moves parallel to the magnetic field there is no magnetic force and no potential difference is produced.

questions.questionText: Problem 21.21
questions.expectedAnswer:
21.21. Set Up: The rod moves perpendicular to the magnetic field, so Eq. (21.6) applies. To determine which end is at higher potential, consider the direction of the magnetic force on a positive charge in the moving rod. The end where positive charge accumulates is the end with the higher potential.

(a)  $V_{ab} = vBL$  so  $B = \frac{V_{ab}}{vL} = \frac{0.450\mathrm{V}}{(4.50\mathrm{m / s})(0.120\mathrm{m})} = 0.833\mathrm{T}$
(b) The rod is sketched in Figure (a) below. The magnetic force on a positive charge in the moving rod is shown. The right-hand rule applied to  $\vec{v}$  and  $\vec{B}$  says this force is toward end  $b$  and this end is at higher potential.
(c) The rod is sketched in Figure (b) below. The magnetic force now is perpendicular to the rod. There is no charge accumulation at its ends and no induced emf.

![img-96.jpeg](img-96.jpeg)
(a)

![img-97.jpeg](img-97.jpeg)
(b)

questions.questionText: 21.22. Use  $V_{ab} = vBL$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The minimum speed required is  $v = \frac{V_{ab}}{BL} = \frac{10\mathrm{V}}{(5.5\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{T})(1\mathrm{m})} = 2\times 10^{5}\mathrm{m / s}$

Reflect: This is not a practical way to create a voltage difference.

questions.questionText:
21.23. We must modify the derivation of the equation  $\mathcal{E} = vBL$ , which assumes that the velocity of the bar is perpendicular to the axis of the bar (leading to a magnetic force that is parallel to the axis of the bar). When the velocity of the bar is not perpendicular to the bar, it produces a magnetic force that is no longer aligned with the bar. The component of the magnetic force that is parallel to the bar is  $qvB\sin \theta$ , where  $\theta$  is the angle between the axis of



the bar and its velocity. Assuming that an equal electric force balances the parallel component of the magnetic force, we obtain a motional emf given by  $\mathcal{E} = vBL\sin \theta$

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** (a)  $\mathcal{E} = vBL\sin \theta = (2.50\mathrm{m / s})(1.20\mathrm{T})(1.41\mathrm{m})(\sin 0^{\circ}) = 0\mathrm{V}$

(b)  $\mathcal{E} = vBL\sin \theta = (2.50\mathrm{m / s})(1.20\mathrm{T})(1.41\mathrm{m})(\sin 90^{\circ}) = 4.23\mathrm{V},$  with the right end at the higher potential.

(c)  $\mathcal{E} = vBL\sin \theta = (2.50\mathrm{m / s})(1.20\mathrm{T})(1.41\mathrm{m})(\sin 60^{\circ}) = 3.66\mathrm{V},$  with the right end at the higher potential.

**Reflect:** The orientation of the bar with respect to the velocity affects the potential induced across its ends.

questions.questionText:
21.24. $\mathcal{E} = vBL$ . Use Lenz's law to determine the direction of the induced current. The force  $F_{ext}$  required to maintain constant speed is equal and opposite to the force  $F_{I}$  that the magnetic field exerts on the rod because of the current in the rod.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** (a)  $\mathcal{E} = vBL = (7.50\mathrm{m / s})(0.800\mathrm{T})(0.500\mathrm{m}) = 3.00\mathrm{V}$

(b)  $\vec{B}$  is into the page. The flux increases as the bar moves to the right, so the magnetic field of the induced current is out of the page inside the circuit. To produce magnetic field in this direction the induced current must be counterclockwise, so from  $b$  to  $a$  in the rod.

(c)  $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \frac{3.00\mathrm{V}}{1.50\Omega} = 2.00\mathrm{A}.$ $F_{I} = ILB\sin \phi = (2.00\mathrm{A})(0.500\mathrm{m})(0.800\mathrm{T})\sin 90^{\circ} = 0.800\mathrm{N}.$ $\vec{F}_I$  is to the left. To keep the bar moving to the right at constant speed an external force with magnitude  $F_{ext} = 0.800\mathrm{N}$  and directed to the right must be applied to the bar.

**Reflect:** The force on the rod due to the induced current is directed to oppose the motion of the rod. This agrees with Lenz's law.

questions.questionText: Problem 21.25
questions.expectedAnswer:
21.25. Set Up and Solve: (a) Each slab of flowing blood has maximum width  $d$  and is moving perpendicular to the field with speed  $v$ .  $\mathcal{E} = vBL$  becomes  $\mathcal{E} = vBd$ .

(b)  $B = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{vd} = \frac{1.0\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{V}}{(0.15\mathrm{m / s})(5.0\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m})} = 1.3\mathrm{T}$

(c) The blood vessel has cross-sectional area  $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ . The volume of blood that flows past a cross section of the vessel in time  $t$  is  $\pi (d^2 / 4) v t$ . The volume flow rate is volume/time  $= R = \pi d^2 v / 4$ .

$$
v = \frac {\mathcal {E}}{B d} \text { so } R = \frac {\pi d ^ {2}}{4} \left(\frac {\mathcal {E}}{B d}\right) = \frac {\pi d \mathcal {E}}{4 B}
$$

questions.questionText:
21.26. Let solenoid 1 have 100 turns and solenoid 2 have 500 turns.  $M = \left| \frac{N_1\Phi_{R2}}{i_1} \right|$ . The field within the turns of solenoid 1 is  $B_1 = \frac{\mu_0N_1i_1}{2\pi r}$ .

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:**  $\Phi_{R2} = B_1A = \frac{\mu_0N_1i_1A}{2\pi r}$

$$
M = \frac {\mu_ {0} N _ {1} N _ {2} A}{2 \pi r} = \frac {(4 \pi \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \mathrm {W b / m} \cdot \mathrm {A}) (1 0 0) (5 0 0) (4 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m} ^ {2})}{2 \pi (0 . 1 0 0 \mathrm {m})} = 4. 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {H}
$$

**Reflect:** If we interchange the roles of coils 1 and 2 we calculate the same value of  $M$ .

questions.questionText:
21.27. Example 21.8 derives that for this configuration of coils,  $M = \frac{\mu_0N_1N_2A}{l}$ , where  $l$  is the length of coil 1.

**
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:**  $M = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}\mathrm{Wb / m\cdot A})(800)(50)\pi(0.200\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m})^2}{0.100\mathrm{m}} = 6.32\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{H} = 6.32\mu \mathrm{H}$

questions.questionText: 21.28. $\mathcal{E}_1 = M\left|\frac{\Delta i_1}{\Delta t}\right|$  and  $\mathcal{E}_2 = M\left|\frac{\Delta i_1}{\Delta t}\right|$ ,  $M = \left|\frac{N_2\Phi_{B2}}{i_1}\right|$ , where  $\Phi_{B2}$  is the flux through one turn of the second coil.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $M = \frac{\mathcal{E}_2}{\left|\Delta i_1 / \Delta t\right|} = \frac{1.65 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V}}{0.242 \mathrm{~A/s}} = 6.82 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H} = 6.82 \mathrm{mH}$

(b)  $\Phi_{B2} = \frac{M i_1}{N_2} = \frac{(6.82 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H})(1.20 \mathrm{~A})}{25} = 3.27 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Wb}$
(c)  $\mathcal{E}_1 = M\left|\frac{\Delta i_1}{\Delta t}\right| = (6.82\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{H})(0.360\mathrm{A / s}) = 2.46\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{V} = 2.46\mathrm{mV}$

Reflect: We can express  $M$  either in terms of the total flux through one coil produced by a current in the other coil, or in terms of the emf induced in one coil by a changing current in the other coil.

questions.questionText:
21.29. The emf  $\mathcal{E}_2$  in solenoid 2 produced by changing current  $i_1$  in solenoid 1 is given by  $\mathcal{E}_2 = M\left|\frac{\Delta i_1}{\Delta t}\right|$ .

The mutual inductance of two such solenoids is derived in Example 21.8 to be  $M = \frac{\mu_0 A N_1 N_2}{l}$ , where  $A$  and  $l$  are the cross-sectional area and length of solenoid 1.  $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $M = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m/A})\pi(6.00 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{m})^2(6750)(15)}{0.500 \mathrm{~m}} = 2.88 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{H} = 0.288 \mu \mathrm{H}$
(b)  $\mathcal{E}_2 = \left|\frac{\Delta i_1}{\Delta t}\right| = (2.88\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{H})(37.5\mathrm{A / s}) = 1.08\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{V}$

questions.questionText: 21.30. The inductance  $L$  is related to the flux  $\Phi_B$  through one turn by  $N\Phi_B = Li$ . The voltage across the coil is related to the rate at which the current in it is changing by  $\mathcal{E} = L\left|\frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t}\right|$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $\Phi_B = \frac{Li}{N} = \frac{(4.5\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{H})(11.5\mathrm{A})}{125} = 4.1\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{Wb}$
(b)  $\left|\frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t}\right| = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{L} = \frac{1.16\mathrm{V}}{4.5\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{H}} = 258\mathrm{A / s}$

questions.questionText: 21.31. $\mathcal{E} = L\left|\frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t}\right|$
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:  $L = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{|\Delta i / \Delta t|} = \frac{0.0160\mathrm{V}}{0.0640\mathrm{A / s}} = 0.250\mathrm{H}$

questions.questionText: 21.32. $\mathcal{E} = L\left|\frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t}\right|$
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:  $\mathcal{E} = (0.260\mathrm{H})(18.0\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{A / s}) = 4.68\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{V} = 4.68\mathrm{mV}$

questions.questionText: 21.33. Example 21.10 shows that the inductance of a toroidal solenoid is  $L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{2\pi r}$  so  $N = \sqrt{L \frac{2\pi r}{\mu_0 A}}$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The number $N_{2L}$ of coils required for an inductance of $2L$ is $2L = \frac{\mu_0 N_{2L}^2 A}{2\pi r}$, so $N_{2L} = \sqrt{2L \frac{2\pi r}{\mu_0 A}} = \sqrt{2} N$.

The increase $\Delta N$ in the number of coils is $\Delta N = N_{2L} - N = N(\sqrt{2} - 1) = 100(\sqrt{2 - 1}) = 41$.

Reflect: The inductance is proportional to the number of coils squared.

questions.questionText: 21.34. $L = \left|\frac{N\Phi_B}{i}\right|$. The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is uniform and equal to $B = \mu_0\frac{Ni}{l}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\Phi_B = BA$. $L = \frac{N\Phi_B}{i} = \frac{\mu_0 AN^2}{l}$

Reflect: The self-inductance depends only on the geometrical parameters of the coil and is independent of the current that flows in the coil.

questions.questionText: 21.35. Combine the two expressions for $L$: $L = N\Phi_B / i$ and $L = |\mathcal{E} / (\Delta i / \Delta t)|$, where $\Phi_B$ is the average flux through one turn of the solenoid.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Solving for $N$ we have $N = \left|\mathcal{E} / \left[\Phi_B(\Delta i / \Delta t)\right]\right| = \frac{(12.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V})(1.40 \mathrm{~A})}{(0.00285 \mathrm{Wb})(0.0260 \mathrm{A/s})} = 238$ turns.

Reflect: The induced emf depends on the time rate of change of the total flux through the solenoid.

questions.questionText: 21.36. A transformer transforms voltages according to $\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$, $N_1 = 275$, $N_2 = 275$, and $V_1 = 25.0\mathrm{V}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $V_{2} = V_{1}(N_{2} / N_{1}) = (25.0\mathrm{V})(834 / 275) = 75.8\mathrm{V}$

(b) The secondary coil is completed by a resistance $R_{2} = 125\Omega$, so the current in the secondary coil is $I_{2} = \frac{V_{2}}{R_{2}} = \frac{75.8\mathrm{V}}{125\Omega} = 0.606\mathrm{A}$.

questions.questionText: 21.37. For an ideal transformer, the input power is equal to the output power. We know that the relation between power, voltage, and current is given by $P = VI$. Ohm's law, $V = IR$, gives the relation between current and resistance on both the input and output of the transformer.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Assuming that the output power is $15\mathrm{W}$, we have $I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{15\mathrm{W}}{6.0\mathrm{V}} = 2.5\mathrm{A}$.

(b) Since we know both the voltage of the secondary circuit and its current, we have $R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{6.0\mathrm{V}}{2.5\mathrm{A}} = 2.4\Omega$.

(c) Assuming that the input power is $15\mathrm{W}$, we have $I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{15\mathrm{W}}{220\mathrm{V}} = 0.068\mathrm{A}$. Thus, the equivalent resistance of the input circuit is $R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{220\mathrm{V}}{0.068\mathrm{A}} = 3.2\mathrm{k}\Omega$.

Reflect: We could also solve this problem by finding the ratio of the number of turns: $\frac{\mathcal{E}_2}{\mathcal{E}_1} = \frac{6.0\mathrm{V}}{220\mathrm{V}} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 0.027$.

Thus, we can find the equivalent resistance of the input by using Eq. (21.17): $R_{1} = \frac{2.4\Omega}{(0.27)^{2}} = 3300\Omega$, which agrees with part (c) to within the round-off errors.

Solve: (a) $\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{13,000\mathrm{V}}{120\mathrm{V}} = 108$



(b) $P = V_{2}I_{2} = (13,000\mathrm{V})(8.50\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{A}) = 110\mathrm{W}$
(c) $I_{1} = \frac{P}{V_{1}} = \frac{110\mathrm{W}}{120\mathrm{V}} = 0.917\mathrm{A}$

Reflect: Since the power supplied to the primary must equal the power delivered by the secondary, in a step-up transformer the current in the primary is greater than the current in the secondary.

questions.questionText: 21.39. Example 21.10 shows that the inductance of a toroidal solenoid is $L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{2\pi r}$. The energy stored in an inductor is $U = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $U = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{2 \pi r} \right) I^2$ and $N = \sqrt{ \frac{4 \pi r U}{\mu_0 A I^2} } = \sqrt{ \frac{4 \pi (0.150 \, \text{m}) (0.390 \, \text{J})}{(4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{Wb/m} \cdot \text{A}) (5.00 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2) (12.0 \, \text{A})^2} } = 2850$

Reflect: The stored energy is proportional to the square of the number of turns even though the magnetic field within the solenoid is directly proportional to the number of turns. We will see in Section 21.10 that the magnetic field energy depends on the square of the magnetic field.

Solve: (a) $U = \frac{1}{2} (10.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}) (1.15 \mathrm{~A})^2 = 6.74 \mathrm{mJ}$
(b) $I = \sqrt{\frac{2U}{L}} = \sqrt{\frac{2(1.0\mathrm{J})}{10.2\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{H}}} = 14\mathrm{A}$. This is not a reasonable current in a circuit; it is too large.

questions.questionText: 21.41. $U = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$. The self-inductance of a solenoid is found in Problem 21.34 to be $L = \frac{\mu_0 A N^2}{l}$. The length $l$ of the solenoid is the number of turns divided by the turns per unit length.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $L = \frac{2U}{I^2} = \frac{2(10.0\mathrm{J})}{(1.50\mathrm{A})^2} = 8.89\mathrm{H}$

(b) $L = \frac{\mu_0 A N^2}{l}$. If $\alpha$ is the number of turns per unit length, then $N = \alpha l$ and $L = \mu_0 A \alpha^2 l$. For this coil $\alpha = 10 \, \text{coils/mm} = 10 \times 10^3 \, \text{coils/m}$.

$$
l = \frac {L}{\mu_ {0} A \alpha^ {2}} = \frac {8.89 \, \mathrm{H}}{(4 \pi \times 10 ^ {- 7} \, \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}) \pi (0.0200 \, \mathrm{m}) ^ {2} (10 \times 10 ^ {3} \, \text{coils/m}) ^ {2}} = 56.3 \, \mathrm{m}
$$

This is not a practical length for laboratory use.

Reflect: The number of turns is $N = (56.3 \, \text{m})(10 \times 10^{3} \, \text{coils/m}) = 5.63 \times 10^{5}$ turns. The length of wire in the solenoid is the circumference $C$ of one turn times the number of turns. $C = \pi d = \pi (4.00 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}) = 0.126 \, \text{m}$. The length of wire is $(0.126 \, \text{m})(5.63 \times 10^{5}) = 7.1 \times 10^{4} \, \text{m} = 71 \, \text{km}$. This length of wire will have a large resistance and $I^2 R$ electrical energy losses will be very large.

questions.questionText: 21.42. (a) The magnetic field inside a solenoid is $B = \mu_0 n I$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $B = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A})(400)(80.0 \, \mathrm{A})}{0.250 \, \mathrm{m}} = 0.161 \, \mathrm{T}$

(b) Set Up: The energy density in a magnetic field is $u = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$.

Solve: $u = \frac{(0.161 \, \mathrm{T})^2}{2(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A})} = 1.03 \times 10^4 \, \mathrm{J/m^3}$



(c) Set Up: The total stored energy is $U = uV$.

Solve: $U = uV = u(IA) = (1.03 \times 10^{4} \ \mathrm{J/m^3})(0.250 \ \mathrm{m})(0.500 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{m^2}) = 0.129 \ \mathrm{J}$

(d) Set Up: The energy stored in an inductor is $U = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$.

Solve: Solving for $L$ and putting in the numbers gives

$$
L = \frac{2U}{I^2} = \frac{2(0.129 \ \mathrm{J})}{(80.0 \ \mathrm{A})^2} = 4.02 \times 10^{-5} \ \mathrm{H}
$$

Reflect: An inductor stores its energy in the magnetic field inside of it.

Solve: (a) Energy = $(200 \ \mathrm{W})(24 \ \mathrm{h})(3600 \ \mathrm{s/h}) = 1.73 \times 10^7 \ \mathrm{J}$

(b) $L = \frac{2U}{I^2} = \frac{2(1.73 \times 10^7 \ \mathrm{J})}{(80.0 \ \mathrm{A})^2} = 5.41 \times 10^3 \ \mathrm{H}$

questions.questionText: 21.44. $U = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$. $\frac{U}{I^2} = \frac{1}{2} L = \text{constant}$ and $\frac{U_1}{I_1^2} = \frac{U_2}{I_2^2}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $I_2 = I_1 \left( \frac{U_2}{U_1} \right)^{1/2} = I \left( \frac{9.0 \ \mathrm{mJ}}{3.0 \ \mathrm{mJ}} \right)^{1/2} = I \sqrt{3}$

questions.questionText: 21.45. The time constant is $\tau = L / R$. The current as a function of time is $i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} (1 - e^{-t / \tau})$. The energy stored in the inductor is $U = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{11.0 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{H}}{150.0 \ \Omega} = 7.33 \times 10^{-5} \ \mathrm{s} = 73.3 \ \mu \mathrm{s}$

(b) The maximum current is when $t \to \infty$ and is equal to $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \frac{12.0 \ \mathrm{V}}{150.0 \ \Omega} = 0.0800 \ \mathrm{A}$.

(c) $i = (0.0800 \ \mathrm{A}) (1 - e^{-73.3 \ \mu \mathrm{s} / 73.3 \ \mu \mathrm{s}}) = 0.0506 \ \mathrm{A}$

(d) $U_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 = \frac{1}{2} (11.0 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{H}) (0.0800 \ \mathrm{A})^2 = 3.52 \times 10^{-5} \ \mathrm{J} = 35.2 \ \mu \mathrm{J}$

questions.questionText: 21.46. The loop rule applied to the circuit gives $\mathcal{E} - iR - L \frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t} = 0$. $L \frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t} = v_L$, the voltage across the inductor. The current as a function of time is given by $i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} (1 - e^{-(R / L)t})$. At $t = 0$, $i = 0$. $i = i_{\max} = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R}$ at $t \to \infty$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) At $t = 0$, $i = 0$ and $\frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t} = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{L} = \frac{6.00 \ \mathrm{V}}{2.50 \ \mathrm{H}} = 2.40 \ \mathrm{A/s}$.

(b) At $t = 0$, $\frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t} = 2.40 \ \mathrm{A/s}$ and $v_L = L \frac{\Delta i}{\Delta t} = (2.50 \ \mathrm{H}) (2.40 \ \mathrm{A/s}) = 6.00 \ \mathrm{V}$. Initially, $i = 0$, the voltage across the resistor is zero, and the full battery emf appears across the inductor.

(c) The time constant is $\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2.50 \ \mathrm{H}}{8.00 \ \Omega} = 0.313 \ \mathrm{s}$. When $t = 0.313 \ \mathrm{s}$,

$$
i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} (1 - e^{-1}) = \frac{6.00 \ \mathrm{V}}{8.00 \ \Omega} (1 - e^{-1}) = 0.474 \ \mathrm{A}
$$



(d) When $ t \to \infty $, $ i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \frac{6.00\,\mathrm{V}}{8.00\,\Omega} = 0.750\,\mathrm{A} $.

Reflect: Initially, $ i = 0 $ and the full battery voltage is across the inductor. After a long time, the full battery voltage is across the resistor.

questions.questionText: 21.47. At all times $ v_{1} + v_{2} = 25.0 $ V. The voltage across the resistor depends on the current through it and the voltage across the inductor depends on the rate at which the current through it is changing. Immediately after closing the switch the current through the inductor is zero. After a long time the current is no longer changing.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $ i = 0 $ so $ v_{1} = 0 $ and $ v_{2} = 25.0 $ V. The ammeter reading is $ A = 0 $.

(b) After a long time, $ v_{2} = 0 $ and $ v_{1} = 25.0 $ V. $ v_{1} = iR $ and $ i = \frac{v_{1}}{R} = \frac{25.0\,\mathrm{V}}{15.0\,\Omega} = 1.67\,\mathrm{A} $. The ammeter reading is $ A = 1.67 $ A.

(c) None of the answers in (a) and (b) depends on $ L $ so none of them would change.

Reflect: The inductance $ L $ of the circuit affects the rate at which current reaches its final value. But after a long time the inductor doesn't affect the circuit and the final current does not depend on $ L $.

questions.questionText: 21.48. We know that $ i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} (1 - e^{-t / \tau}) $, with $ \tau = L / R $. The energy stored in the inductor is $ U = \frac{1}{2} L i^{2} $. The maximum current occurs after a long time and is equal to $ \mathcal{E} / R $.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) We know that $ i_{\max} = \mathcal{E} / R $; thus, $ i = i_{\max} / 2 $ when $ (1 - e^{-t / \tau}) = \frac{1}{2} $, which implies that $ e^{-t / \tau} = \frac{1}{2} $. Solving for $ t $ we obtain $ -t / \tau = \ln \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) $, which gives $ t = \frac{L \ln 2}{R} = \frac{(\ln 2)(1.25 \times 10^{-3} \,\mathrm{H})}{50.0\,\Omega} = 17.3\,\mu\mathrm{s} $.

(b) We know that $ U = \frac{1}{2} U_{\max} $ when $ i = i_{\max} / \sqrt{2} $. This gives us $ 1 - e^{-t / \tau} = 1 / \sqrt{2} $, so we have $ e^{-t / \tau} = 1 - 1 / \sqrt{2} = 0.2929 $. Solving for $ t $ we obtain $ t = -L \ln (0.2929) / R = 30.7\,\mu\mathrm{s} $.

Reflect: $ \tau = L / R = 2.50 \times 10^{-5} \,\mathrm{s} = 25.0\,\mu\mathrm{s} $. The time in part (a) is $ 0.692\tau $ and the time in part (b) is $ 1.23\tau $.

questions.questionText: 21.49. The current as a function of time is given by $ i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} (1 - e^{-(R / L)t}) $. The energy stored in the inductor is $ U = \frac{1}{2} L i^{2} $. $ U $ reaches $ \frac{1}{2} $ its maximum value when $ i $ is $ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $ times its maximum value.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The maximum current is $ \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} $, $ i = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} $ gives $ \frac{1}{2} = 1 - e^{-(R / L)t} $ and $ e^{-(R / L)t} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot -\left(\frac{R}{L}\right) t = \ln \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) $.

$$
t = - \frac {L \ln \left(\frac {1}{2}\right)}{R} = - \frac {\left(1.50 \times 10^{-3} \,\mathrm{H}\right) \ln \left(\frac {1}{2}\right)}{0.750 \times 10^{3} \,\Omega} = 1.39\,\mu\mathrm{s}
$$

(b) $ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 1 - e^{-(R / L)t} $ and $ t = -\frac{L \ln(1 - 1 / \sqrt{2})}{R} = -\frac{(1.50 \times 10^{-3} \,\mathrm{H}) \ln(1 - 1 / \sqrt{2})}{0.750 \times 10^{3} \,\Omega} = 2.46\,\mu\mathrm{s} $.

questions.questionText: 21.50. The resonant angular frequency of an $ L - C $ circuit is $ \omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} $.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $ \omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(5.25 \times 10^{-3} \,\mathrm{H})(12.0 \times 10^{-6} \,\mathrm{F})}} = 3980\,\mathrm{rad/s} $

questions.questionText: 21.51. The energy stored in a capacitor is $U_{C} = \frac{1}{2} C v^{2}$. The energy stored in an inductor is $U_{L} = \frac{1}{2} L i^{2}$. Energy conservation requires that the total stored energy be constant. The current is a maximum when the charge on the capacitor is zero.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Initially $\upsilon = 16.0\mathrm{V}$ and $i = 0$. $U_{L} = 0$ and

$$
U _ {C} = \frac {1}{2} C v ^ {2} = \frac {1}{2} (5.00 \times 10 ^ {-6} \mathrm{F}) (16.0 \mathrm{V}) ^ {2} = 6.40 \times 10 ^ {-4} \mathrm{J}
$$

The total energy stored is $0.640\mathrm{mJ}$.

(b) The current is maximum when $q = 0$ and $U_{C} = 0$. $U_{C} + U_{L} = 6.40 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{J}$ so $U_{L} = 6.40 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{J}$.

$$
\frac {1}{2} L i _ {\max } ^ {2} = 6.40 \times 10 ^ {-4} \mathrm{J} \text{ and } i _ {\max } = \sqrt {\frac {2 (6.40 \times 10 ^ {-4} \mathrm{J})}{3.75 \times 10 ^ {-3} \mathrm{H}}} = 0.584 \mathrm{A}
$$

questions.questionText: 21.52. The energy stored in a capacitor is $U_{C} = \frac{1}{2} C v^{2}$. The energy stored in an inductor is $U_{L} = \frac{1}{2} L i^{2}$. Energy conservation requires that the total stored energy be constant. The current is a maximum when the charge on the capacitor is zero.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\upsilon_{\mathrm{max}} = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{175\mu C}{15.0\mu\mathrm{F}} = 11.7\mathrm{V}$. $\frac{1}{2} C\upsilon_{\mathrm{max}}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} L i_{\mathrm{max}}^{2}$.

$$
i _ {\max } = v _ {\max } \sqrt {\frac {C}{L}} = (11.7 \mathrm{V}) \sqrt {\frac {15.0 \times 10 ^ {-6} \mathrm{F}}{5.00 \times 10 ^ {-3} \mathrm{H}}} = 0.641 \mathrm{A}
$$

At this instant $q = 0$.

(b) $\upsilon_{\mathrm{max}} = 11.7\mathrm{V}$. At this instant $i = 0$.

(c) $U_{\mathrm{max}} = \frac{1}{2} L i_{\mathrm{max}}^{2} = 1.03\mathrm{mJ}$. This is when $i = 0.641\mathrm{A}$.

questions.questionText: 21.53. This is an $R-L$ circuit and $i(t)$ is given by Eq. (21.24). When $t \to \infty$, $i \to i_t = V / R$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $R = \frac{V}{i_t} = \frac{12.0\mathrm{V}}{6.45\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{A}} = 1860\Omega$

(b) $i = i_{t}(1 - e^{-(R / L)t})$ so $\frac{Rt}{L} = -\ln (1 - i / i_t)$ and $L = \frac{-Rt}{\ln(1 - i / i_t)} = \frac{-(1860\Omega)(7.25\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{s})}{\ln(1 - (4.86 / 6.45))} = 0.963\mathrm{H}$.

Reflect: The current after a long time depends only on $R$ and is independent of $L$. The value of $R / L$ determines how rapidly the final value of $i$ is reached.

questions.questionText: 21.54. When the loop is entering or leaving the region of magnetic field, the flux through it is changing and there is an induced emf. The magnitude of this induced emf is $\mathcal{E} = BL\nu_{t}$ as in Example 21.4. The length $L$ is $0.750\mathrm{m}$. When the loop is totally within the field, the flux through the loop is not changing and there is no induced emf. The induced current has magnitude $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R}$ and direction given by Lenz's law.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = \frac{BL\nu}{R} = \frac{(1.25\mathrm{T})(0.750\mathrm{m})(3.0\mathrm{m/s})}{12.5\Omega} = 0.225\mathrm{A}$. The flux through the loop is directed out of the page and is increasing, so the magnetic field of the induced current is into the page inside the loop and the induced current is clockwise.

(b) The flux is not changing so $\mathcal{E}$ and $I$ are zero.



(c)  $I = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = 0.225\mathrm{A}$ . The flux through the loop is directed out of the page and is decreasing, so the magnetic field of the induced current is out of the page inside the loop and the induced current is counterclockwise.
(d) Let clockwise currents be positive. At  $t = 0$  the loop is entering the field. It is totally in the field at time  $t_{o}$  and beginning to move out of the field at time  $t_{b}$ . The graph of the induced current as a function of time is sketched in the figure below.

![img-98.jpeg](img-98.jpeg)

questions.questionText: 21.55. At  $t = 0$  we have  $V_{L} = \mathcal{E}$ .
