questions.questionText: 24.13. For a concave mirror, $R > 0$. $R = 32.0$ cm and $f = \frac{R}{2} = 16.0$ cm.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(12.0\mathrm{cm})(16.0\mathrm{cm})}{12.0\mathrm{cm} - 16.0\mathrm{cm}} = -48.0\mathrm{cm}$. $m = \frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{-48.0\mathrm{cm}}{12.0\mathrm{cm}} = +4.00$.

(b) $s' = -48.0 \, \text{cm}$, so the image is $48.0 \, \text{cm}$ to the right of the mirror. $s' < 0$ so the image is virtual.

(c) The principal-ray diagram is sketched in the figure below. The rules for principal rays apply only to paraxial rays. Principal ray 2, which travels to the mirror along a line that passes through the focus, makes a large angle with the optic axis and is not described well by the paraxial approximation. Therefore, principal ray 2 is not included in the sketch.

![img-144.jpeg](img-144.jpeg)

Reflect: A concave mirror forms a virtual image whenever $s < f$.

questions.questionText: 24.14. For a concave mirror, $R > 0$. $R = 22.0$ cm and $f = \frac{R}{2} = 11.0$ cm.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The principal-ray diagram is sketched in the figure below.

![img-145.jpeg](img-145.jpeg)

(b) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(16.5\mathrm{cm})(11.0\mathrm{cm})}{16.5\mathrm{cm} - 11.0\mathrm{cm}} = 33.0\mathrm{cm}$. $m = \frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{33.0\mathrm{cm}}{16.5\mathrm{cm}} = -2.00$.

$\left|y^{\prime}\right| = \left|m\right|y = (2.00)\times (0.600\mathrm{cm}) = 1.20\mathrm{cm}$. The image is $33.0~\mathrm{cm}$ to the left of the mirror. It is $1.20~\mathrm{cm}$ tall. $s^{\prime} > 0$, so the image is real. $m < 0$, so the image is inverted.

questions.questionText: 24.15. For a convex mirror, $R < 0$. $R = -22.0$ cm and $f = \frac{R}{2} = -11.0$ cm.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The principal-ray diagram is sketched in the figure below.

![img-146.jpeg](img-146.jpeg)

(b) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(16.5\,\mathrm{cm})(-11.0\,\mathrm{cm})}{16.5\,\mathrm{cm} - (-11.0\,\mathrm{cm})} = -6.6\,\mathrm{cm}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{-6.6\,\mathrm{cm}}{16.5\,\mathrm{cm}} = +0.400$.

$\left|y'\right| = \left|m\right|y = (0.400)(0.600\,\mathrm{cm}) = 0.240\,\mathrm{cm}$. The image is $6.6\,\mathrm{cm}$ to the right of the mirror. It is $0.240\,\mathrm{cm}$ tall. $s' < 0$, so the image is virtual. $m > 0$, so the image is upright.

questions.questionText: 24.16. For the orientation of the shell shown in the figure in the problem, $R = +12.0$ cm. When the glass is reversed, the seed faces a convex surface, so $R = -12.0$ cm. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{2}{R}$ and $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $R = +12.0$ cm. $\frac{1}{s'} = \frac{2}{R} - \frac{1}{s} = \frac{2s - R}{Rs}$ and

$$
s' = \frac{Rs}{2s - R} = \frac{(12.0\,\mathrm{cm})(15.0\,\mathrm{cm})}{30.0\,\mathrm{cm} - 12.0\,\mathrm{cm}} = +10.0\,\mathrm{cm}
$$

$m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{10.0\,\mathrm{cm}}{15.0\,\mathrm{cm}} = -0.667$. $y' = my = -2.20$ mm. The image is $10.0\,\mathrm{cm}$ to the left of the shell vertex and is 2.20 mm tall.

(b) $R = -12.0$ cm. $s' = \frac{(12.0\,\mathrm{cm})(15.0\,\mathrm{cm})}{30.0\,\mathrm{cm} + 12.0\,\mathrm{cm}} = -4.29\,\mathrm{cm}$. $m = -\frac{-4.29\,\mathrm{cm}}{15.0\,\mathrm{cm}} = +0.286$. $y' = my = 0.944$ mm. The image is $4.29\,\mathrm{cm}$ to the right of the shell vertex and is $0.944$ mm tall.

Reflect: In part (a), $s > R/2$ and the mirror is concave, so the image is real. In part (b) the image is virtual, because a convex mirror always forms a virtual image.

questions.questionText: 24.17. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ and $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$. $m = +2.00$ and $s = 1.25$ cm. An upright image must be virtual.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f}$ and $m = -\frac{f}{s - f}$. For a concave mirror, $m$ can be larger than 1.00. For a convex mirror, $|f| = -f$ so $m = +\frac{|f|}{s + |f|}$ and $m$ is always less than 1.00. The mirror must be concave ($f > 0$).

(b) $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{s' + s}{ss'}$. $f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = +2.00$ and $s' = -2.00s$.

$$
f = \frac{s(-2.00s)}{s - 2.00s} = +2.00s = +2.50\,\mathrm{cm}
$$



$R = 2f = +5.00\mathrm{cm}$. The principal-ray diagram is drawn in the figure below.

![img-147.jpeg](img-147.jpeg)

questions.questionText: 24.18. For a convex surface, $R > 0$. $R = +3.00$ cm. $n_a = 1.00$, $n_b = 1.60$. $\frac{n_a}{s} + \frac{n_b}{s'} = \frac{n_b - n_a}{R}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $s \to \infty$. $\frac{n_b}{s'} = \frac{n_b - n_a}{R}$. $s' = \left(\frac{n_b}{n_b - n_a}\right)R = \left(\frac{1.60}{1.60 - 1.00}\right)(+3.00\mathrm{cm}) = +8.00\mathrm{cm}$. The image is $8.00\mathrm{cm}$ to the right of the vertex.

(b) $s = 12.0 \, \text{cm}$. $\frac{1.00}{12.0 \, \text{cm}} + \frac{1.60}{s'} = \frac{1.60 - 1.00}{3.00 \, \text{cm}}$. $s' = +13.7 \, \text{cm}$. The image is $13.7 \, \text{cm}$ to the right of the vertex.

(c) $s = 2.00 \, \text{cm}$. $\frac{1.00}{2.00 \, \text{cm}} + \frac{1.60}{s'} = \frac{1.60 - 1.00}{3.00 \, \text{cm}}$. $s' = -5.33 \, \text{cm}$. The image is $5.33 \, \text{cm}$ to the left of the vertex.

questions.questionText: 24.19. $R = +3.00 \, \text{cm}$. $n_a = n$ (the refractive index of the liquid). $n_b = 1.60$. $s = 90.0 \, \text{cm}$. $s' = +160 \, \text{cm}$. $\frac{n_a}{s} + \frac{n_b}{s'} = \frac{n_b - n_a}{R}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $\frac{n}{90.0\mathrm{cm}} + \frac{1.60}{160\mathrm{cm}} = \frac{1.60 - n}{3.00\mathrm{cm}}$. $n\left(\frac{1}{90.0\mathrm{cm}} + \frac{1}{3.00\mathrm{cm}}\right) = 1.60\left(\frac{1}{3.00\mathrm{cm}} - \frac{1}{160\mathrm{cm}}\right)$. $n = 1.52$.

Solve: $\frac{1}{24.0\mathrm{cm}} + \frac{1.60}{s'} = \frac{1.60 - 1.00}{4.00\mathrm{cm}}$. $s' = +14.8\mathrm{cm}$. $m = -\frac{(1.00)(14.8\mathrm{cm})}{(1.60)(24.0\mathrm{cm})} = -0.385$.

$\left|y_{1}^{n}\right| = \left|m\right|y = (0.385)(1.50\mathrm{mm}) = 0.578\mathrm{mm}$. The image is $14.8~\mathrm{cm}$ to the right of the vertex and is $0.578~\mathrm{mm}$ tall. $m < 0$, so the image is inverted.

Reflect: The image is real.

questions.questionText: 24.21. We will apply Eq. (24.11) $\left(\frac{n_a}{s} + \frac{n_b}{s'} = \frac{n_b - n_a}{R}\right)$ and Eq. (24.12) $\left(m = -\frac{n_a s'}{n_b s}\right)$. Note that the situation is similar to what is shown in Figure 24.21 in the textbook with the shark tooth located at $P$; however—since $n_a > n_b$—the transmitted ray is refracted upward (not downward) to form a virtual image to the left of the vertex $V$. We set $n_a = 1.333$, $n_b = 1.00$, $s = 0.450 \, \text{m}$, and $R = +1.75 \, \text{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) We have $\frac{1.333}{0.450\,\mathrm{m}} + \frac{1.00}{s'} = \frac{1.00 - 1.333}{1.75\,\mathrm{m}}$. Thus $s' = -0.317\,\mathrm{m}$ and we have that the image is located inside the tank and $31.7\,\mathrm{cm}$ from the plastic.

(b) The magnification is equal to $m = -\frac{(1.333)(-0.317\,\mathrm{m})}{(1.00)(0.450\,\mathrm{m})} = +0.940$. Since $m > 0$ we know that the image is upright.

(c) Since we know that $m = +0.940$, the size of image of the shark's tooth will be $0.940(5.00\,\mathrm{cm}) = 4.70\,\mathrm{cm}$.

questions.questionText: 24.22. The image formed by refraction at the surface of the eye is located by $\frac{n_a}{s} + \frac{n_b}{s'} = \frac{n_b - n_a}{R}$. $n_a = 1.00$, $n_b = 1.35$. $R > 0$. For a distant object, $s \approx \infty$ and $\frac{1}{s} \approx 0$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $s \approx \infty$ and $s' = 2.5\,\mathrm{cm}$: $\frac{1.35}{2.5\,\mathrm{cm}} = \frac{1.35 - 1.00}{R}$ and $R = 0.648\,\mathrm{cm} = 6.48\,\mathrm{mm}$.

(b) $R = 0.648\,\mathrm{cm}$ and $s = 25\,\mathrm{cm}$: $\frac{1.00}{25\,\mathrm{cm}} + \frac{1.35}{s'} = \frac{1.35 - 1.00}{0.648}$. $\frac{1.35}{s'} = 0.500$ and $s' = 2.70\,\mathrm{cm} = 27.0\,\mathrm{mm}$. The image is formed behind the retina.

(c) Calculate $s'$ for $s \approx \infty$ and $R = 0.50\,\mathrm{cm}$: $\frac{1.35}{s'} = \frac{1.35 - 1.00}{0.50\,\mathrm{cm}}$. $s' = 1.93\,\mathrm{cm} = 19.3\,\mathrm{mm}$. The image is formed in front of the retina.

questions.questionText: 24.23. The surface is flat so $R \to \infty$ and $\frac{n_a}{s} + \frac{n_b}{s'} = 0$. The light travels from the speck to the eye, so $n_a = 1.309$ and $n_b = 1.00$. $s = 3.50\,\mathrm{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $s' = -\left(\frac{n_b}{n_a}\right)s = -\left(\frac{1.00}{1.309}\right)(3.50\,\mathrm{cm}) = -2.67\,\mathrm{cm}$. The speck appears to be $2.67\,\mathrm{cm}$ below the surface.

questions.questionText: 24.24. The object is the bird. $n_a = 1.00$ (air). $n_b = 1.333$ (water). $s'$ is the image distance, measured from the surface of the lake. $s = 7.0\,\mathrm{m}$. For refraction at a plane surface, $\frac{n_a}{s} + \frac{n_b}{s'} = 0$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $s' = -\left(\frac{n_b}{n_a}\right)s = -\left(\frac{1.333}{1.00}\right)(7.0\,\mathrm{m}) = -9.3\,\mathrm{m}$. The image of the bird is $9.3\,\mathrm{m}$ above the surface of the lake, so it is $2.0\,\mathrm{m} + 9.3\,\mathrm{m} = 11.3\,\mathrm{m}$ above the diver.

questions.questionText: 24.25. The object is the ball. $n_a = 1.00$ (air). $n_b = 1.333$ (water). The image is $3.0\,\mathrm{m}$ above the surface of the swimming pool, so $s' = -3.0\,\mathrm{m}$. Calculate $s$. For refraction at a plane surface, $\frac{n_a}{s} + \frac{n_b}{s'} = 0$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $s = -\left(\frac{n_a}{n_b}\right)s' = -\left(\frac{1.00}{1.333}\right)(-3.0\,\mathrm{m}) = 2.3\,\mathrm{m}$. The ball was dropped from $2.3\,\mathrm{m}$ above the surface of the water.

Reflect: When looking from air into water, the image seems nearer than the object actually is. When looking from water into air, the image seems farther away than the object actually is.

questions.questionText: 24.26. $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$. Since the image is upright, $y' > 0$ and $m > 0$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $m = \frac{y'}{y} = \frac{1.30 \, \text{cm}}{0.400 \, \text{cm}} = +3.25$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = +3.25$ gives $s' = -3.25s$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives

$$
\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{-3.25s} = \frac{1}{7.00 \, \text{cm}} \quad \text{and} \quad s = 4.85 \, \text{cm}
$$

$s' = -(3.25)(4.85 \, \text{cm}) = -15.8 \, \text{cm}$. The object is $4.85 \, \text{cm}$ to the left of the lens. The image is $15.8 \, \text{cm}$ to the left of the lens and is virtual.

Reflect: The image is virtual because the object distance is less than the focal length.

questions.questionText: 24.27. $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$. Since the image is inverted, $y' < 0$ and $m < 0$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $m = \frac{y'}{y} = \frac{-4.50 \, \text{cm}}{3.20 \, \text{cm}} = -1.406$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$ gives $s' = +1.406s$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{1.406s} = \frac{1}{90.0 \, \text{cm}}$ and $s = 154 \, \text{cm}$. $s' = (1.406)(154 \, \text{cm}) = 217 \, \text{cm}$. The object is $154 \, \text{cm}$ to the left of the lens. The image is $217 \, \text{cm}$ to the right of the lens and is real.

questions.questionText: 24.28. Since the image is projected onto a wall, the image is real and $s' > 0$. $s' = 2.0 \, \text{m}$, $s = 0.50 \, \text{m}$, and $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$. Both $s$ and $s'$ are positive, so $m < 0$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The focal length of the lens is $f = \left(\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{1}{0.50 \, \text{m}} + \frac{1}{2.0 \, \text{m}}\right)^{-1} = 0.40 \, \text{m}$.

(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{2.0 \, \text{m}}{0.50 \, \text{m}} = -4.0$

Reflect: Since the image is real the lens must be converging.

questions.questionText: 24.29. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. The type of lens determines the sign of $f$. $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$. The sign of $s'$ depends on whether the image is real or virtual. $s' = +15.0 \, \text{cm}$; $s'$ is positive because the image is on the side of the lens opposite to the object. $s = 35.0 \, \text{cm} - 15.0 \, \text{cm} = 20.0 \, \text{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{s + s'}{ss'}$ and $f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'} = \frac{(20.0 \, \text{cm})(15.0 \, \text{cm})}{20.0 \, \text{cm} + 15.0 \, \text{cm}} = +8.57 \, \text{cm}$. $f$ is positive so the lens is converging.

(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{15.0 \, \text{cm}}{20.0 \, \text{cm}} = -0.750$. $y' = my = (-0.750)(2.50 \, \text{mm}) = -1.88 \, \text{mm}$. The image is $1.88 \, \text{mm}$ tall. $s' > 0$ and the image is real.

questions.questionText: 24.30. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. The type of lens determines the sign of $f$. $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$. The sign of $s'$ determines whether the image is real or virtual. $s = +8.00 \, \text{cm}$. $s' = -3.00 \, \text{cm}$. $s'$ is negative because the image is on the same side of the lens as the object.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{s + s'}{ss'}$ and $f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'} = \frac{(8.00 \, \text{cm})(-3.00 \, \text{cm})}{8.00 \, \text{cm} - 3.00 \, \text{cm}} = -4.80 \, \text{cm}$. $f$ is negative so the lens is diverging.

(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{-3.00 \, \text{cm}}{8.00 \, \text{cm}} = +0.375$. $y' = my = (0.375)(6.50 \, \text{mm}) = 2.44 \, \text{mm}$. $s' < 0$ and the image is virtual.

Reflect: A converging lens can also form a virtual image, if the object distance is less than the focal length. But in that case $|s'| > s$ and the image would be farther from the lens than the object is.

questions.questionText: 24.31. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. The type of lens determines the sign of $f$. $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$. The sign of $s'$ depends on whether the image is real or virtual. $s = 16.0 \, \text{cm}$. $s' = -22.0 \, \text{cm}$; $s'$ is negative because the image is on the same side of the lens as the object.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{s + s'}{ss'}$ and $f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'} = \frac{(16.0 \, \text{cm})(-22.0 \, \text{cm})}{16.0 \, \text{cm} - 22.0 \, \text{cm}} = +58.7 \, \text{cm}$. $f$ is positive so the lens is converging.

(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{-22.0 \, \text{cm}}{16.0 \, \text{cm}} = 1.38$. $y' = my = (1.38)(3.25 \, \text{mm}) = 4.48 \, \text{mm}$. $s' < 0$ and the image is virtual.

questions.questionText: 24.32. We will apply the Lensmaker's equation Eq. [24.20]: $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$. This is a converging lens with identical surfaces — which implies that $R_1 > 0, R_2 < 0$, and $f > 0$. Thus, we have $R_1 = +22.0 \, \text{cm}, R_2 = -22.0 \, \text{cm}$, and $f = +20.0 \, \text{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Solving the thin-lens equation for $n$ we obtain $n = \frac{R_1 R_2}{f(R_2 - R_1)} + 1 = \frac{-(22.0 \, \text{cm})^2}{(20.0 \, \text{cm})(-44.0 \, \text{cm})} + 1 = 1.55$.

Reflect: According to the sign conventions, $R > 0$ if the center of curvature is on the outgoing side—and negative otherwise. Since our lens has two convex faces, the front face has a positive radius of curvature and the back face has a negative radius of curvature.

questions.questionText: 24.33. We will apply the Lensmaker's equation Eq. [24.20]: $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$. We are given that $f = 12 \, \text{cm}$, $R_1 = 15 \, \text{cm}$, and $n = 1.7$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Solving for $R_2$ gives $R_2 = \frac{R_1 f(n - 1)}{R_1 + f(n - 1)} = \frac{(15 \, \text{cm})(12 \, \text{cm})(1.7 - 1)}{15 \, \text{cm} + (12 \, \text{cm})(1.7 - 1)} = -19 \, \text{cm}$.

Reflect: According to the sign conventions, $R > 0$ if the center of curvature is on the outgoing side and negative otherwise. In this case $R_2 < 0$ because its center of curvature not on the outgoing side.

questions.questionText: 24.34. Use $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$ to calculate $f$ and then use $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ to locate the image. $s = 18.0 \, \text{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{f} = (0.5)\left(\frac{1}{10.0 \, \text{cm}} - \frac{1}{-15.0 \, \text{cm}}\right)$ and $f = +12.0 \, \text{cm}$. $\frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s} = \frac{s - f}{sf}$.

$$
s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(18.0 \, \text{cm})(12.0 \, \text{cm})}{18.0 \, \text{cm} - 12.0 \, \text{cm}} = +36.0 \, \text{cm}
$$

The image is $36.0 \, \text{cm}$ to the right of the lens.

(b) $\frac{1}{f} = (0.5)\left(\frac{1}{10.0 \, \text{cm}} - \frac{1}{\infty}\right)$ and $f = +20.0 \, \text{cm}$.

$$
s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(18.0 \, \text{cm})(20.0 \, \text{cm})}{18.0 \, \text{cm} - 20.0 \, \text{cm}} = -180 \, \text{cm}
$$

The image is $180 \, \text{cm}$ to the left of the lens.

(c) $\frac{1}{f} = (0.5)\left(\frac{1}{-15.0 \, \text{cm}} - \frac{1}{10.0 \, \text{cm}}\right)$ and $f = -12.0 \, \text{cm}$.

$$
s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(18.0 \, \text{cm})(-12.0 \, \text{cm})}{18.0 \, \text{cm} + 12.0 \, \text{cm}} = -7.2 \, \text{cm}
$$

The image is $7.2 \, \text{cm}$ to the left of the lens.



(d)  $\frac{1}{f} = (0.5)\left(\frac{1}{-10.0\mathrm{cm}} -\frac{1}{-15.0\mathrm{cm}}\right)$  and  $f = -60.0~\mathrm{cm}$

$$
s ^ {\prime} = \frac {s f}{s - f} = \frac {(18.0~\mathrm{cm}) (-60.0~\mathrm{cm})}{18.0~\mathrm{cm} + 60.0~\mathrm{cm}} = -13.8~\mathrm{cm}
$$

The image is  $13.8\mathrm{cm}$  to the left of the lens.

questions.questionText:
24.35. $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} -\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$ . If  $R$  is the radius of the lens, then  $R_{1} = R$  and  $R_{2} = -R$ .  $\frac{1}{s} +\frac{1}{s^{\prime}} = \frac{1}{f}$ .

$$
m = \frac {y ^ {\prime}}{y} = - \frac {s ^ {\prime}}{s}.
$$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} -\frac{1}{R_2}\right) = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R} -\frac{1}{-R}\right) = \frac{2(n - 1)}{R}.$

$$
R = 2 (n - 1) f = 2 (0.44) (8.0~\mathrm{mm}) = 7.0~\mathrm{mm}
$$

(b)  $\frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s} = \frac{s - f}{sf}$ .  $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(30.0~\mathrm{cm})(0.80~\mathrm{cm})}{30.0~\mathrm{cm} - 0.80~\mathrm{cm}} = 0.82~\mathrm{cm} = 8.2~\mathrm{mm}$ . The image is  $8.2~\mathrm{mm}$  from the lens,

on the side opposite the object.  $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{0.82 \, \text{cm}}{30.0 \, \text{cm}} = -0.0273$ .  $y' = my = (-0.0273)(16 \, \text{cm}) = 0.44 \, \text{cm} = 4.4 \, \text{mm}$ .

$s' > 0$  so the image is real.  $m < 0$  so the image is inverted.

Reflect: The lens is converging and has a very short focal length. As long as the object is farther than  $7.0 \mathrm{~mm}$  from the eye, the lens forms a real image.

Solve: (a)  $\frac{1}{f(n - 1)} = \frac{1}{R_1} -\frac{1}{R_2}$ .  $\frac{1}{R_2} = \frac{1}{R_1} -\frac{1}{f(n - 1)} = \frac{1}{+5.0~\mathrm{mm}} -\frac{1}{(18.0~\mathrm{mm})(0.38)}$  and  $R_{2} = 18.6~\mathrm{mm}$

(b)  $\frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s} = \frac{s - f}{sf}$ .  $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(25~\mathrm{cm})(1.8~\mathrm{cm})}{25~\mathrm{cm} - 1.8~\mathrm{cm}} = 1.9~\mathrm{cm} = 19~\mathrm{mm}$ .

(c)  $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{1.9 \, \text{cm}}{25 \, \text{cm}} = -0.076$ .  $y' = my = (-0.076)(8.0 \, \text{mm}) = -0.61 \, \text{mm}$ .  $s' > 0$  so the image is real.  $m < 0$  so the image is inverted.

questions.questionText:
24.37. $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} -\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$ .  $R_{1}\rightarrow \infty$ .  $R_{2} = -13.0~\mathrm{cm}$ . If the lens is reversed,  $R_{1} = +13.0~\mathrm{cm}$  and

$$
R _ {2} \rightarrow \infty . \frac {1}{s} + \frac {1}{s ^ {\prime}} = \frac {1}{f}. m = \frac {y ^ {\prime}}{y} = - \frac {s ^ {\prime}}{s}.
$$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $\frac{1}{f} = (0.70)\left(\frac{1}{\infty} -\frac{1}{-13.0~\mathrm{cm}}\right) = \frac{0.70}{13.0~\mathrm{cm}}$  and  $f = 18.6~\mathrm{cm}$ .  $\frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} -\frac{1}{s} = \frac{s - f}{sf}$ .

$$
s ^ {\prime} = \frac {s f}{s - f} = \frac {(22.5~\mathrm{cm}) (18.6~\mathrm{cm})}{22.5~\mathrm{cm} - 18.6~\mathrm{cm}} = 107~\mathrm{cm}
$$

$$
m = - \frac {s ^ {\prime}}{s} = - \frac {107~\mathrm{cm}}{22.5~\mathrm{cm}} = -4.76
$$

$y' = my = (-4.76)(3.75\mathrm{mm}) = -17.8\mathrm{mm}$ . The image is  $107~\mathrm{cm}$  to the right of the lens and is  $17.8~\mathrm{mm}$  tall. The image is real and inverted.



(b) $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{13.0\mathrm{cm}} -\frac{1}{s\infty}\right)$ and $f = 18.6~\mathrm{cm}$. The image is the same as in part (a).

Reflect: Reversing a lens does not change the focal length of the lens (Problem 24.44).

questions.questionText: 24.38. For a double-convex lens, $R_{1} = +R$ and $R_{2} = -R$, where $R = 2.50\mathrm{cm}$. Solve the thin lens equation for the index of refraction, then take the ratio.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} -\frac{1}{R_2}\right) = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R} -\frac{1}{-R}\right) = \frac{2(n - 1)}{R} \Rightarrow n = \frac{R}{2f} + 1$

$$
\frac {n _ {1}}{n _ {2}} = \frac {R / 2 f _ {1} + 1}{R / 2 f _ {2} + 1} = \frac {(2.50~\mathrm{cm}) / 2 (2.5~\mathrm{cm}) + 1}{(2.50~\mathrm{cm}) / 2 (1.25~\mathrm{cm}) + 1} = \frac {3 / 2}{5 / 4} = \frac {6}{5}
$$

questions.questionText: 24.39. Use the thin-lens equation to calculate the focal length of the lens: $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$. The magnification of the lens is $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{1}{24.0\mathrm{cm}} + \frac{1}{s'} = (1.52 - 1)\left(\frac{1}{-7.00\mathrm{cm}} - \frac{1}{-4.00\mathrm{cm}}\right) \Rightarrow s' = 71.2\mathrm{cm}$, to the right of the lens.

(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{71.2\mathrm{cm}}{24.0\mathrm{cm}} = -2.97$

Reflect: Since the magnification is negative, the image is inverted.

questions.questionText: 24.40. Apply $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ and $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$. We know that $f = +12.0 \, \text{cm}$ and $s' = -17.0 \, \text{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{12.0\mathrm{cm}} - \frac{1}{-17.0\mathrm{cm}} \Rightarrow s = 7.0\mathrm{cm}$.

$m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{(-17.0)}{7.0} = +2.4 \Rightarrow y = \frac{y'}{m} = \frac{0.800\mathrm{cm}}{+2.4} = +0.34\mathrm{cm}$, so the object is $0.34\mathrm{cm}$ tall and on the same side as the image. The image is upright since $m > 0$.

Reflect: When the object is closer to the lens than the focal length, a converging lens forms a virtual, enlarged image.

questions.questionText: 24.41. Apply $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ to each lens. $m_1 = \frac{y_1'}{y_1}$ and $m_2 = \frac{y_2'}{y_2}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Lens 1: $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives

$$
s _ {1} ^ {\prime} = \frac {s _ {1} f _ {1}}{s _ {1} - f _ {1}} = \frac {(50.0~\mathrm{cm}) (40.0~\mathrm{cm})}{50.0~\mathrm{cm} - 40.0~\mathrm{cm}} = + 200~\mathrm{cm}
$$

$$
m _ {1} = - \frac {s _ {1} ^ {\prime}}{s _ {1}} = - \frac {200~\mathrm{cm}}{50~\mathrm{cm}} = - 4.00
$$

$y_{1}^{\prime} = m_{1}y_{1} = (-4.00)(1.20\mathrm{cm}) = -4.80\mathrm{cm}$. The image $I_{1}$ is $200\mathrm{cm}$ to the right of lens 1, is $4.80\mathrm{cm}$ tall and is inverted.

(b) Lens 2: $y_{2} = -4.80\mathrm{cm}$. The image $I_{1}$ is $300\mathrm{cm} - 200\mathrm{cm} = 100\mathrm{cm}$ to the left of lens 2, so $s_{2} = +100\mathrm{cm}$.



$$
\begin{array}{l}
s_{2}^{\prime} = \frac{s_{2} f_{2}}{s_{2} - f_{2}} = \frac{(100 \text{ cm})(60.0 \text{ cm})}{100 \text{ cm} - 60.0 \text{ cm}} = +150 \text{ cm} \\
m_{2} = - \frac{s_{2}^{\prime}}{s_{2}} = - \frac{150 \text{ cm}}{100 \text{ cm}} = -1.50 \\
\end{array}
$$

$y_{2}^{\prime} = m_{2} y_{2} = (-1.50)(-4.80 \text{ cm}) = +7.20 \text{ cm}$. The image is $150 \text{ cm}$ to the right of the second lens, is $7.20 \text{ cm}$ tall, and is inverted with respect to the original object.

**Reflect:** The overall magnification of the lens combination is $m_{\mathrm{tot}} = m_1 m_2$.

**24.42. Set Up:** Apply $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ with $f = \pm 35.0$ cm. We know that the magnification is $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$.

**Solve:** (a) We want the size of the image to be twice that of the object and so we must have $m = \pm 2$. Since the image is real we know that $s' > 0$, which implies that $m = -2 = -\frac{s'}{s}$. Thus we conclude that $s' = 2s$. Now we can determine the location of the object: $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{2s} = \frac{3}{2s} = \frac{1}{f}$. Solving for $s$ we obtain $s = \frac{3}{2} f$. Since we know that $s > 0$ we must have that $f = +35.0$ cm, and thus $s = \frac{3}{2} f = \frac{3}{2}(35.0$ cm) = 52.5 cm.

(b) We again want the image to be twice the size as the object; however, in this case we have a virtual image so $s' < 0$ and $m = +2 = -\frac{s'}{s}$. Thus, we have $s' = -2s$. Now we can determine the location of the object: $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{-2s} = \frac{1}{2s} = \frac{1}{f}$. Solving for $s$ we obtain $s = \frac{1}{2} f$. Since we know that $s > 0$ we must have that $f = +35.0$ cm and thus $s = \frac{1}{2}(35.0$ cm) = 17.5 cm.

**Reflect:** For a diverging lens we have $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} < 0$. This can only occur if $\frac{1}{s'}$ is negative and larger in magnitude than $\frac{1}{s}$. Thus we have $|m| = \left|\frac{s'}{s}\right| < 1$. It follows that the image is always smaller than the object for a diverging lens. In this exercise $|m| = 2 > 1$, so only a converging lens will work.

**24.43. Set Up:** The image formed by the first lens serves as the object for the second lens. $m_{\mathrm{tot}} = m_1 m_2$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f}$.

**Solve:** (a) Lens 1: $f_{1} = -12.0$ cm, $s_{1} = 20.0$ cm.

$$
\begin{array}{l}
s' = \frac{(20.0 \text{ cm})(-12.0 \text{ cm})}{20.0 \text{ cm} + 12.0 \text{ cm}} = -7.5 \text{ cm} \\
m_{1} = - \frac{s_{1}^{\prime}}{s_{1}} = - \frac{-7.5 \text{ cm}}{20.0 \text{ cm}} = +0.375 \\
\end{array}
$$

Lens 2: The image of lens 1 is $7.5$ cm to the left of lens 1 so is $7.5$ cm + $9.00$ cm = $16.5$ cm to the left of lens 2. $s_{2} = +16.5$ cm. $f_{2} = +12.0$ cm.



$$
s _ {2} ^ {\prime} = \frac {(16.5 \mathrm{~cm}) (12.0 \mathrm{~cm})}{16.5 \mathrm{~cm} - 12.0 \mathrm{~cm}} = 44.0 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

$$
m _ {2} = - \frac {s _ {2} ^ {\prime}}{s _ {2}} = - \frac {44.0 \mathrm{~cm}}{16.5 \mathrm{~cm}} = -2.67
$$

The final image is 44.0 cm to the right of lens 2 so is 53.0 cm to the right of the first lens.

(b)  $s_2^{\prime} > 0$  so the final image is real.

(c)  $m_{\mathrm{tot}} = m_1m_2 = (+0.375)(-2.67) = -1.00$  . The image is  $2.50~\mathrm{mm}$  tall and is inverted.

Reflect: The light travels through the lenses in the direction from left to right. A real image for the second lens is to the right of that lens and a virtual image is to the left of the second lens.

questions.questionText: 24.44. We apply  $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$  with the conditions that  $s + s' = \pm 214$  cm and  $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Since the size of the image is greater than the size of the object we know that the image must be farther from the lens than the object. This implies that the focal length of the lens is positive and the lens is converging.

We know that the image is real so  $s' > 0$ . In this case we have  $s + s' = +214$  cm and  $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{5}{3}$ . Thus, we may write  $s' = \frac{5}{3} s$  and  $s + s' = \frac{8}{3} s = +214$  cm. Solving for  $s$  and  $s'$  we obtain  $s = 80.25$  cm and  $s' = 133.75$  cm. This gives  $f = -\frac{ss'}{s + s'} = \frac{(80.25 \text{ cm})(133.75 \text{ cm})}{214 \text{ cm}} = 50.2$  cm.

Reflect: For a diverging lens we have  $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} < 0$ . This can only occur if  $\frac{1}{s'}$  is negative and larger in magnitude than  $\frac{1}{s}$ . Thus we have  $|m| = \left|\frac{s'}{s}\right| < 1$ . It follows that the image is always smaller than the object for a diverging lens. In this exercise  $|m| = \frac{5}{3} > 1$ , so only a converging lens will work.

questions.questionText: 24.45. $f = +14.0$  cm.  $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$  gives  $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f}$ .  $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $s = 18.0$  cm

(a)  $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(18.0 \text{ cm})(14.0 \text{ cm})}{18.0 \text{ cm} - 14.0 \text{ cm}} = 63.0 \text{ cm}$ . The image is  $63.0 \text{ cm}$  to the right of the lens.

(b)  $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{63.0 \text{ cm}}{18.0 \text{ cm}} = -3.50$

(c)  $s' > 0$  so the image is real.

(d)  $m < 0$  so the image is inverted. The principal-ray diagram is sketched in Figure (a) below.



![img-148.jpeg](img-148.jpeg)
(a)

![img-149.jpeg](img-149.jpeg)
(b)

$s = 7.00\mathrm{cm}$

(a) $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(7.00\mathrm{cm})(14.0\mathrm{cm})}{7.00\mathrm{cm} - 14.0\mathrm{cm}} = -14.0\mathrm{cm}$. The image is $14.0\mathrm{cm}$ to the left of the lens.
(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{-14.0\mathrm{cm}}{7.00\mathrm{cm}} = +2.00$
(c) $s' < 0$ so the image is virtual.
(d) $m > 0$ so the image is upright. The principal-ray diagram is sketched in Figure (b) above.

**Reflect:** For a converging lens, when $s > f$ the image is real and when $s < f$ the image is virtual.

questions.questionText:
24.46. The thin-lens equation applies in this case. The thin-lens equation is $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$, and the magnification is $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = \frac{y'}{y}$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** (a) $m = \frac{y'}{y} = \frac{34.0 \, \mathrm{mm}}{8.00 \, \mathrm{mm}} = 4.25 = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{-12.0 \, \mathrm{cm}}{s} \Rightarrow s = 2.82 \, \mathrm{cm}$. The thin-lens equation gives $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow f = 3.69 \, \mathrm{cm}$.

(b) The object is located at $s = 2.82 \, \mathrm{cm}$, which is $2.82 \, \mathrm{cm}$ to the left of the lens and on the same side as the image.
(c) The principal-ray diagram is similar to Figure 24.36e in the text.

**Reflect:** Since the focal length is positive, this is a converging lens. The image distance is negative because the object is closer to the lens than the focal length of the lens.

^{}[]

questions.questionText: 24.47. Use $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ to calculate the object distance $s$. Calculate $m$ from $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$ and use that to determine the size and orientation of the image. We are given $f = -48.0 \, \text{cm}$ and we have a virtual image $17.0 \, \text{cm}$ from lens so $s' = -17.0 \, \text{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$, so $\frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s} = \frac{s - f}{sf}$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
s = \frac{s'f}{s' - f} = \frac{(-17.0 \, \text{cm})(-48.0 \, \text{cm})}{-17.0 \, \text{cm} - (-48.0 \, \text{cm})} = +26.3 \, \text{cm} \\
m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{-17.0 \, \text{cm}}{+26.3 \, \text{cm}} = +0.646 \\
m = \frac{y'}{y} \text{ so } |y| = \frac{|y|}{|m|} = \frac{8.00 \, \text{mm}}{0.646} = 12.4 \, \text{mm} \\
\end{array}
$$

(b) The principal-ray diagram is sketched in the figure below.

Reflect: We have a virtual image and a real object ($s > 0$), so image and object are on the same side of the lens. Since $m > 0$ we know that the image is upright with respect to the object. The height of the object is $12.4 \, \text{mm}$.

![img-150.jpeg](img-150.jpeg)

questions.questionText: 24.48. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. The sign of $f$ determines whether the lens is converging or diverging. $s = 16.0 \, \text{cm}$. $s' = -12.0 \, \text{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'} = \frac{(16.0 \, \text{cm})(-12.0 \, \text{cm})}{16.0 \, \text{cm} + (-12.0 \, \text{cm})} = -48.0 \, \text{cm}$. $f < 0$ so the lens is diverging.

(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{-12.0 \, \text{cm}}{16.0 \, \text{cm}} = +0.750$. $|y'| = |m|y = (0.750)(8.50 \, \text{mm}) = 6.38 \, \text{mm}$. $m > 0$ so the image is upright.

(c) The principal-ray diagram is sketched in the figure below.

![img-151.jpeg](img-151.jpeg)

questions.questionText: 24.49. $s = 12.0 \, \text{cm}$. The ray intersects the optic axis $8.0 \, \text{cm}$ to the right of the lens.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The ray is bent toward the optic axis by the lens so the lens is converging.

(b) The ray is parallel to the optic axis and is bent so that it passes through the focal point to the right of the lens; $f = 8.0 \, \text{cm}$.

(c) The principal-ray diagram is drawn in the figure below. The diagram shows that the image is $24.0 \, \text{cm}$ to the right of the lens.

![img-152.jpeg](img-152.jpeg)

(d) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(12.0 \, \text{cm})(8.0 \, \text{cm})}{12.0 \, \text{cm} - 8.0 \, \text{cm}} = +24.0 \, \text{cm}$. The calculated image position agrees with the principal-ray diagram.

Reflect: The lens is converging and $s > f$, so the image is real.

questions.questionText: 24.50. $s = 10.0 \, \text{cm}$. If extended backward, the ray comes from a point on the optic axis $18.0 \, \text{cm}$ from the lens and is parallel to the optic axis after it passes through the lens.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The ray is bent toward the optic axis by the lens so the lens is converging.

(b) The ray is parallel to the optic axis after it passes through the lens so it comes from the focal point; $f = 18.0 \, \text{cm}$.

(c) The principal-ray diagram is drawn in the figure below. The diagram shows that the image is $22.5 \, \text{cm}$ to the left of the lens.

![img-153.jpeg](img-153.jpeg)

(d) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(10.0 \, \text{cm})(18.0 \, \text{cm})}{10.0 \, \text{cm} - 18.0 \, \text{cm}} = -22.5 \, \text{cm}$. The calculated image position agrees with the principal-ray diagram.

questions.questionText: 24.51. $s = 16.0 \, \text{cm}$. The ray is initially parallel to the optic axis and is bent by the lens so it appears to come from a point $10.0 \, \text{cm}$ to the left of the lens.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The ray is bent away from the optic axis by the lens so the lens is diverging.

(b) The ray is initially parallel to the optic axis so it is bent such that if it is extended backward, it appears to come from the focus. $f = -10.0$ cm.

(c) The principal-ray diagram is drawn in the figure below. The diagram shows that the image is $6.2\,\mathrm{cm}$ to the left of the lens.

![img-154.jpeg](img-154.jpeg)

(d) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ so $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(16.0\,\mathrm{cm})(-10.0\,\mathrm{cm})}{16.0\,\mathrm{cm} - (-10.0\,\mathrm{cm})} = -6.2\,\mathrm{cm}$. The calculated image position agrees with the principal-ray diagram.

questions.questionText: 24.52. Apply $\frac{n_{d}}{s} + \frac{n_{b}}{s'} = \frac{n_{b} - n_{d}}{R}$ with $R \to \infty$, since we assume that the surfaces are flat. The image formed by the first interface serves as the object for the second interface.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: For the water-benzene interface to get the apparent water depth:

$$
\frac{n_{d}}{s} + \frac{n_{b}}{s'} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{1.33}{6.50\,\mathrm{cm}} + \frac{1.50}{s'} = 0 \Rightarrow s' = -7.33\,\mathrm{cm}
$$

For the benzene-air interface, to get the total apparent distance to the bottom:

$$
\frac{n_{d}}{s} + \frac{n_{b}}{s'} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{1.50}{(7.33\,\mathrm{cm} + 2.60\,\mathrm{cm})} + \frac{1}{s'} = 0 \Rightarrow s' = -6.62\,\mathrm{cm}
$$

Reflect: At the water-benzene interface the light refracts into material of greater refractive index and the overall effect is that the apparent depth is greater than the actual depth.

questions.questionText: 24.53. Use $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. We are given that $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = +2$. We are to find the object image $s$ and the image distance $s'$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $s' = -2s$, $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{-2s} = \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2s} = \frac{1}{f}$ and $s = \frac{f}{2}$. $s' = -2s = -f$.

Reflect: The object is in front of the mirror and the image is behind the mirror (i.e., virtual).

questions.questionText: 24.54. Use $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. A plot of $\frac{1}{f}$ versus $\frac{1}{s}$ should give a straight line with a slope of unity and a $y$ intercept of $y_0 = \frac{1}{s'}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The plot of the data is shown below.



![img-155.jpeg](img-155.jpeg)

From the best-fit line, the $y$ intercept is $0.099\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$. Thus, the image distance is $s' = \frac{1}{y_0} = \frac{1}{0.99\mathrm{cm}^{-1}} = 1.0\mathrm{cm}$.

Reflect: From the best-fit line, the slope is $1.0179 \pm 0.0126$, which is close to the expected value of unity.

questions.questionText: 24.55. Since the image is projected onto a screen the image is real and $s' > 0$. $s' = 800 \, \text{cm}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$ so $m$ is negative; $m = -\frac{36.0 \, \text{cm}}{0.600 \, \text{cm}} = -60.0$.

24.56. We apply the thin-lens equation: $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$. If the image is real then the center of curvature of each lens is on the outgoing side so the radii of curvature are both positive: $R_1 = +6.00 \, \text{cm}$ and $R_2 = +10.0 \, \text{cm}$. We are also given that $s = 35.0 \, \text{cm}$ and $s' = 50 \, \text{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Solving for the focal length gives $f = \left(\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{1}{35.0\mathrm{cm}} + \frac{1}{50\mathrm{cm}}\right)^{-1} = 21\mathrm{cm}$.

(b) Solving for the index of refraction gives $n = 1 + \frac{1}{f}\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)^{-1} = 1 + \left(\frac{1}{20.588\mathrm{cm}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{6.00\mathrm{cm}} - \frac{1}{10.0\mathrm{cm}}\right)^{-1} = 1.7$.

Reflect: If the object were on the concave side of the lens the center of curvature of each lens would be on the incoming side, and so the radius of curvatures would then both be negative and switched in order. However, since $\left(\frac{1}{6.00\mathrm{cm}} - \frac{1}{10.0\mathrm{cm}}\right) = \left(\frac{1}{-10.0\mathrm{cm}} - \frac{1}{-6.00\mathrm{cm}}\right)$ the answer would be unchanged.

questions.questionText: 24.57. The mirror surface is convex so $R = -3.00 \, \text{cm}$. $s = 24.0 \, \text{cm} - 3.00 \, \text{cm} = 21.0 \, \text{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $f = \frac{R}{2} = -1.50 \, \text{cm}$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$.



$$
s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(21.0 \text{ cm})(-1.50 \text{ cm})}{21.0 \text{ cm} - (-1.50 \text{ cm})} = -1.40 \text{ cm}
$$

The image is 1.40 cm behind the surface so it is 3.00 cm - 1.40 cm = 1.60 cm from the center of the ornament, on the same side as the object.

$$
m = - \frac{s'}{s} = - \frac{-1.40 \text{ cm}}{21.0 \text{ cm}} = +0.0667
$$

$$
|y'| = |m|y = (0.0667)(3.80 \text{ mm}) = 0.253 \text{ mm}
$$

questions.questionText: 24.58. We apply the thin-lens equation: $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$, when $n = 1.55$ and $f = 20.0$ cm.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Since $f > 0$ we choose $R_1 = R$ and $R_2 = -R$, where $R$ is the magnitude of the radius of curvature. Thus we have $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{-R}\right) = \frac{2(n - 1)}{R}$. Solving for $R$ we obtain $R = 2(n - 1)f = 2(1.55 - 1)(20.0\mathrm{cm}) = 22\mathrm{cm}$.

Reflect: For identical convex surfaces, the relation between $f$ and $R$ is $f = \left(\frac{1}{n - 1}\right)\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)$. This is reminiscent of the relation for spherical mirrors, which is $f = \frac{R}{2}$.

questions.questionText:
24.59. $f = +3.00$ cm. The positions of the object, lens, and screen are sketched in the figure below. Since the image is projected onto a screen, the image is real and $s' > 0$. The figure shows that $s' + s = 18.0$ cm and $s' = 18.0$ cm - $s$.

![img-156.jpeg](img-156.jpeg)
Problem 24.60
questions.expectedAnswer: 24.60. Set Up: Refer to Figure 24.14a in Section 24.2. $R$ and $s'$ are negative, so $R = -|R|$ and $s' = -|s'|$. $\tan \alpha = \frac{h}{s}$. $\tan \beta = \frac{h}{|s'|}$. $\sin \phi = \frac{h}{|R|}$. For small angles, $\alpha = \frac{h}{s}$, $\beta = \frac{h}{|s'|}$ and $\phi = \frac{h}{|R|}$. $\beta = \theta + \phi$ and $\theta = \alpha + \phi$. Eliminating $\theta$ gives $\beta = \alpha + 2\phi$. Substituting in the expressions for the angles gives $\frac{h}{|s'|} = \frac{h}{s} + \frac{2h}{|R|}$. Dividing by $h$ and replacing $|s'|$ by $-s'$ and $|R|$ by $-R$ gives $\frac{1}{-s'} = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{2}{-R}$. Rearranging gives $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{2}{R}$, which is Eq. (24.4). Now refer to Figure 24.14b in Section 24.2. Triangles $PVQ$ and $P'VQ'$ are similar, so $\frac{y}{s} = \frac{y'}{|s'|}$. $m = \frac{y'}{y} = \frac{|s'|}{s}$. $|s'| = -s'$ so $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$, which is Eq. (24.7).

questions.questionText: 24.61. The image formed by the first surface serves for the object for the second surface. Since the lens is thin, $s_2 = -s_1'$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{n_a}{s} + \frac{n_b}{s'} = \frac{n_b - n_a}{R}$, where $n_a = n_{\mathrm{liq}}$ and $n_b = n$ (refractive index of the lens). $\frac{n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{s_1} + \frac{n}{s_1'} = \frac{n - n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{R_1}$.

$\frac{n_b}{s_2} + \frac{n_c}{s_2'} = \frac{n_c - n_b}{R}$, where $n_b = n$ and $n_c = n_{\mathrm{liq}}$. $\frac{n}{s_2} + \frac{n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{s_2} = \frac{n_{\mathrm{liq}} - n}{R_2}$. $\frac{n}{s_1'} = \frac{n - n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{R_1}$. $\frac{n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{s_1} = \frac{n}{s_2} = \frac{n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{s_2'} + \frac{n - n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{R_2}$.

$\frac{n}{s_1'} = -\frac{n}{s_2}$ so $\frac{n - n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{R_1} - \frac{n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{s_1} = +\frac{n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{s_2'} + \frac{n - n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{R_2}$. $\frac{1}{s_1} + \frac{1}{s_2'} = (n / n_{\mathrm{liq}} - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$. Comparing to $\frac{1}{s_1} + \frac{1}{s_2'} = \frac{1}{f'}$ gives $\frac{1}{f'} = (n / n_{\mathrm{liq}} - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$, as was to be shown.

(b) $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$. $f(n - 1) = f'(n / n_{\mathrm{liq}} - 1) = f'\left(\frac{n - n_{\mathrm{liq}}}{n_{\mathrm{liq}}}\right)$. $f' = \left(\frac{n_{\mathrm{liq}}(n - 1)}{n - n_{\mathrm{liq}}}\right)f$, as was to be shown.

# Solutions to Passage Problems

questions.questionText: 24.62. We will apply the given equation: $\frac{1}{f} = \left(\frac{n}{n_{\mathrm{liq}}} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: Note that the case of a glass lens immersed in air can be obtained as a special case of the given equation by choosing $n_{\mathrm{liq}} = 1$. As the value of $n_{\mathrm{liq}}$ increases to approach $n$, we see that $\frac{1}{f}$ approaches zero—which mean that $f$ increases without bound. Thus, if we move a glass lens ($n = 1.5$) from air ($n_{\mathrm{liq}} = 1.00$) into water ($n_{\mathrm{liq}} = 1.33$) the focal length of the lens will increase. The correct answer is C.

questions.questionText: 24.63. We will apply the given equation: $\frac{1}{f} = \left(\frac{n}{n_{\mathrm{liq}}} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Since the lens is concave we know that $\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right) < 0$ and so $f < 0$ when the lens is immersed in air ($n_{\mathrm{liq}} = 1.00$). If the lens is now placed in a liquid where $n_{\mathrm{liq}} > n$, we have $\left(\frac{n}{n_{\mathrm{liq}}} - 1\right) < 0$ so that



$\frac{1}{f} = \left(\frac{n}{n_{\mathrm{liq}}} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right) > 0$. Thus, the concave lens is a converging lens when placed in a liquid with a high enough index of refraction. The correct answer is D.

questions.questionText: 24.64. The focal length of a mirror is given by $ f = \frac{R}{2} $.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: The law of reflection does not depend on the index of refraction and the focal length of the mirror is given by $ f = \frac{R}{2} $ independent of the surrounding material. The correct answer is D.

questions.questionText: 24.65. In air, the focal length of the lenses is given by $ \frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right) $. In water, this equation must be modified to account for the index of refraction of water. As given in the text, the modified equation is $ \frac{1}{f_{\mathrm{liq}}} = \left(\frac{n}{n_{\mathrm{liq}}} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{t,liq}}} - \frac{1}{R_{2,\mathrm{liq}}}\right) $, where the subscript "liq" indicates that these values are for the lenses that are to be used in water.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: To have $ f = f_{\mathrm{liq}} $, we must have $ \left( \frac{n}{n_{\mathrm{liq}}} - 1 \right) \left( \frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{t,liq}}} - \frac{1}{R_{2,\mathrm{liq}}} \right) = (n - 1) \left( \frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2} \right) $. Because $ n_{\mathrm{liq}} > 1 $, $ \left( \frac{n}{n_{\mathrm{liq}}} - 1 \right) < n - 1 $, which means that $ \left( \frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{t,liq}}} - \frac{1}{R_{2,\mathrm{liq}}} \right) > \left( \frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2} \right) $ for the equality to hold. Therefore, the radii in water must be less than the radii in air. The correct answer is B.

Reflect: As the indices of refraction of the lens approach that of the medium surrounding the lens, the radius of curvature of the lens must become smaller and smaller to produce the same effect. If we take this reasoning to the limit in which the indices are the same, the lens must have an infinitely small radius of curvature to compensate for its inability to refract light in this situation.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q1: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
1. The drawing below shows how the curved bottom of a wine glass forms an image when the wine glass is filled with wine. Before explaining how this works, however, note that no image forms if the glass is empty because the local air-glass and glass-air interfaces have essentially the same radii of curvature ( $R_{1} = R_{2}$ ) because they are concentric surfaces. As a result, Eq. 24.21 for a thin lens gives  $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} -\frac{1}{R_2}\right) \Rightarrow f = \infty$ , which means that an empty wine glass does not focus the light that passes through it. Now consider the situation where the glass is full of wine: As discussed above, the glass does not contribute to the focusing. Thus, we may ignore the glass and just consider the wine-air interface. This interface is concave and  $n_{\mathrm{wine}} > n_{\mathrm{air}}$ , so parallel rays from the light are focused by the glass full of wine, forming an image on the table below. Because the index of refraction of wine is about the same as that of water, the same image would form if the glass were full of water.

![img-157.jpeg](img-157.jpeg)

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q2: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 2. As shown in CQ 24.11, a spherical air bubble in water forms a diverging lens. The same conclusion holds for a spherical air bubble surrounded by a plastic film, because the plastic film is thin and the local interfaces (water-plastic, plastic-air and air-plastic, plastic water) have the same radii of curvature (see Conceptual Question 25.1), so the film does not focus light. For the air bubble to form a converging lens, it would have to have the shape of a diverging lens in air.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q3: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
3. The eye and a camera are similar in that they both form real images on screens. For the eye, the screen is the retina. For the camera, the screen is the film or CCD. The eye differs from the camera in that it has a fixed image distance (i.e., the lens-retina distance) and it adjusts the focal length of its lens to focus images on the retina. For the



camera, the focal length of the lens is constant, and the image distance is adjusted to focus the image on the film or CCD.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q4: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 4. A sensor placed at the position of a virtual image will not record an image because no light actually passes through the location of the virtual image—the light only appears to emanate from that position. However, any camera can record a virtual image. This is done, for example, every time you look into a mirror. Your eye focuses onto your retina the light rays that appear to be coming from the image of yourself beyond the mirror (i.e., from a virtual image), thereby forming a real image on your retina. Similarly, cameras can focus light rays from virtual images to form real images on their sensors and thereby record the virtual image.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q5: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 5. Consider Conceptual Question 24.11, which shows that a spherical air bubble in water forms a diverging lens. If the water and air are exchanged, we would have the situation of a spherical fishbowl full of water in an environment of air. The fishbowl would then form a converging lens because the rays would be bent each interface in opposite directions with respect to the air bubble in water. Thus, it is possible for a fishbowl to focus the sun's rays, although the quality of the focus is likely to be poor, which would make it difficult to get sufficient intensity in the focal spot to start a fire.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q6: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 6. No, a telescope and microscope cannot be used interchangeably. If you try to use a microscope as a telescope, the objective lens would form a real image $I$ at the focal point $F_1'$ in Fig. 25.13. Unlike a telescope, in a microscope, the second focal point of the objective lens is not at the same position as the first focal point of the eyepiece. Thus, the eyepiece of the microscope would not work as a simple magnifier. If you try to use a telescope as a microscope, the objective lens would form a virtual image, which you could not view through the eyepiece. The difference between the microscope and the telescope is that the distance $d$ between the objective and eyepiece is greater than $f_1 + f_2$ for a microscope and but equal to $f_1 + f_2$ for a telescope. A telescope works for viewing distant objects ($s \gg f_1$) whereas a microscope works for viewing near objects ($s \approx f_1$).

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q7: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 7. Angular magnification is given by Eq. 25.3, $M = \left(\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s'}\right) \times 25 \, \text{cm}$. A virtual image is formed by a diverging lens used as a magnifier, so $s' < 0$. Thus, we need to minimize $f$ to get the maximum magnification $M$. From the thin lens equation $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$ (Eq. 24.21), we see that, if $R_1 = +\infty$, then $|R_2|$ should be minimized to get the minimum focal length $f$. Thus, you should choose the lens with one highly curved surface to get the greatest magnification.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q8: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 8. The angular magnification of a refracting telescope is given by Eq. 25.5, $M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2}$, where $f_1(f_2)$ is the focal length of the objective lens (eyepiece). The distance between the objective lens and the eyepiece is $d = f_1 + f_2$. However, doubling the distance $d$ does not give any information about the magnification, so the longer telescope will not necessarily produce a greater magnification than the shorter telescope.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q9: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 9. The angular magnification of a refracting telescope is given by Eq. 25.5, $M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2}$, where $f_1(f_2)$ is the focal length of the objective lens (eyepiece). However, the diameter of the objective lens gives no indication of its focal length, so you cannot conclude that the wider telescope will give a greater magnification. That said, the wider telescope will collect more light, so the image will be brighter.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q10: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 10. The angular magnification of a refracting telescope is given by Eq. 25.5, $M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2}$, where $f_1(f_2)$ is the focal length of the objective lens (eyepiece). Thus, for maximum angular magnification, you need to choose a long-focal-length lens for the objective and a short-focal-length lens for the eyepiece. This can be done by observing the curvature of the lenses. A long-focal-length length will be "flatter" (i.e., like a plane of glass, which has a focal length of infinity), whereas a short-focal-length lens will be "rounder." Given two lenses with approximately the same focal length, you should choose the larger-diameter lens because it collects more light.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q11: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 11. Correcting for severe myopia (nearsightedness) requires a diverging lens of large power (i.e., with a short focal length). This will create at the person's far point (or closer) a virtual, upright image of objects that are beyond the person's far point. However, for objects just beyond the person's near point, it may create a virtual image inside the near point, which the person could not view clearly. Thus, a single lens could not enhance the ability to view distant objects without hindering the ability to view near objects. Doing so would require bifocal (or progressive) lenses, which are essentially multiple lenses.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q12: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 12. The angular magnification of a refracting telescope is given by Eq. 25.5, $M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2}$, where $f_1(f_2)$ is the focal length of the objective lens (eyepiece). Thus, there is only a single magnification possible for a given telescope, so there can be no "maximum" magnification. Thus, this statement is not meaningful and is misleading because it implies that the magnification of a telescope can vary.

questions.questionText: Chapter 25 — Conceptual Q13: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 13. For a normal eye, the far point is at infinity, which means that objects at infinity are focused on the retina of a normal eye. The distance between the lens and retina of a normal eye is the image distance, which in this case is the focal length of the lens (i.e., $2.5\mathrm{cm}$) because $s = \infty$. We are told that this myopic eye is longer than a normal eye, which implies that the focal length of the lens of this myopic eye is the same as that of a normal eye. We are also told that the farthest object that can be clearly viewed by this myopic eye is at $s = 40\mathrm{cm}$, which means that the image will form on the retina at an image distance of $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow s' = \left(\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{1}{2.5\mathrm{cm}} - \frac{1}{40\mathrm{cm}}\right)^{-1} = 2.67\mathrm{cm}$. Thus, the myopic eye is $2.67\mathrm{cm} - 2.5\mathrm{cm} = 1.7\mathrm{mm}$ longer than a normal eye.

questions.questionText: 25.1. The $f$-number $N$ is $N = \frac{f}{D}$ and is expressed as $f/N$. The exposure time is inversely proportional to the aperture diameter.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $f / 4$ means $\frac{f}{D} = 4$ and $D = \frac{f}{4} = \frac{300 \, \mathrm{mm}}{4} = 75 \, \mathrm{mm}$.

(b) If $t$ is the exposure time, then $\frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{\frac{1}{4}\pi D_2^2}{\frac{1}{4}\pi D_1^2} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$. For $f / 8$, $D = \frac{f}{8} = 37.5 \, \mathrm{mm}$.



$$
t _ {2} = t _ {1} \left(\frac {D _ {1}}{D _ {2}}\right) ^ {2} = \left(\frac {1}{2 5 0} \mathrm {s}\right) \left(\frac {7 5 \mathrm {m m}}{3 7 . 5 \mathrm {m m}}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {4}{2 5 0} \mathrm {s} = \frac {1}{6 2 . 5} \mathrm {s}
$$

Reflect: Larger $f$-number means smaller aperture diameter and larger exposure time.

questions.questionText: 25.2. The intensity is proportional to the aperture area, which is proportional to the aperture diameter squared. Thus, $\frac{I}{I'} = \left(\frac{D}{D'}\right)^2$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: If $I' = 2I$, then $D' = D\sqrt{\frac{I'}{I}} = (8.00\mathrm{cm})\sqrt{\frac{2I}{I}} = 11.3\mathrm{cm}$.

Reflect: Because the intensity is the power per unit area, it makes sense that the intensity transmitted by an aperture is proportional to the area of the aperture.

questions.questionText: 25.3. We have the relation: $f$-number $= \frac{f}{D}$. The notation $f/N$ means that the lens or mirror has an $f$-number of $N$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $f$-number $= \frac{f}{D} = \frac{1.325\mathrm{m}}{0.0600\mathrm{m}} = f / 22.1$

(b) $f = (f\text{-number})(\text{diameter}) = 3.3(200 \text{ inches}) = 660$ inches

(c) The minimum $f$-number is $\frac{2.50 \, \text{cm}}{0.80 \, \text{cm}} = f / 3.1$ and the maximum $f$-number is $\frac{2.50 \, \text{cm}}{0.20 \, \text{cm}} = f / 13$.

Reflect: The wide range of $f$-numbers available to the human eye (which is comparable to that of many camera lenses) allows the eye to function well under a wide range of light intensities.

questions.questionText: 25.4. $f$-number $= \frac{f}{D}$. $f/N$ means an $f$-number of $N$. $f = 135 \, \mathrm{mm}$ for the telephoto lens and $f = 50 \, \mathrm{mm}$ for the second lens. The light intensity is proportional to $D^2$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $D_{\min} = \frac{135 \, \mathrm{mm}}{22} = 6.1 \, \mathrm{mm}$; $D_{\max} = \frac{135 \, \mathrm{mm}}{2.8} = 48 \, \mathrm{mm}$. For $f / 11$, $D = \frac{135 \, \mathrm{mm}}{11} = 12 \, \mathrm{mm}$.

(b) $D_{\min} = \frac{50\mathrm{mm}}{22} = 2.3\mathrm{mm}$; $D_{\max} = \frac{50\mathrm{mm}}{2.8} = 18\mathrm{mm}$

(c) $\frac{D_{\max}^2}{D_{\max}^2} = \left(\frac{22}{2.8}\right)^2 = 62$

(d) Keep $D^2 t$ constant, where $t$ is the time the shutter is open. $D_{\min}^2 t_{\min} = D_{\max}^2 t_{\max}$ so

$$
t _ {\max } = t _ {\min } \left(\frac {D _ {\min }}{D _ {\max }}\right) ^ {2} = \left(\frac {1}{3 0 \mathrm {s}}\right) \left(\frac {2 . 8}{2 2}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {1}{1 9 0 0} \mathrm {s}
$$

The closest shutter speed setting is $\frac{1}{2000}$ s.

questions.questionText: 25.5. The image is real since it is projected onto the film. $s' = 20.4 \, \text{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $s = \frac{s'f}{s' - f} = \frac{(20.4\mathrm{cm})(20.0\mathrm{cm})}{20.4\mathrm{cm} - 20.0\mathrm{cm}} = 10.2\mathrm{m}$

Reflect: The object distance $s$ is much greater than $f$, so the image is just beyond the focal point of the lens.

^{}[]

questions.questionText: 25.6. Apply $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ and calculate $f$. We are given that $s = 1.2\,\mathrm{m}$ and $s' = 0.05\,\mathrm{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow f = \left(\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{1}{1.2\,\mathrm{m}} + \frac{1}{0.05\,\mathrm{m}}\right)^{-1} = 0.05\,\mathrm{m}$

Reflect: The focal length is the distance between the lens and the CCD sensor, so an object at infinity (which is anything greater than $50\,\mathrm{cm}$ from the lens) focuses on the CCD sensor.

questions.questionText: 25.7. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = \frac{y'}{y}$. The photocells must be at the image location.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(4.25\,\mathrm{m})(0.0750\,\mathrm{m})}{4.25\,\mathrm{m} - 0.0750\,\mathrm{m}} = 0.0763\,\mathrm{m} = 7.63\,\mathrm{cm}$

(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{0.0763\,\mathrm{m}}{4.25\,\mathrm{m}} = -0.0180$. $y' = my = (-0.0180)(1.85\,\mathrm{m}) = -0.0333\,\mathrm{m} = -3.33\,\mathrm{cm}$. The image is inverted and $3.33\,\mathrm{cm}$ tall. $s' > 0$ so the image is real.

(c) $\frac{0.0333\,\mathrm{m}}{8.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\mathrm{m/pixel}} = 4.2 \times 10^{3}\,\mathrm{pixels}$

Reflect: Since the image is projected onto the photocell, it must be real. Any real image formed by a single lens is inverted.

questions.questionText: 25.8. from the known height of the giraffe and the space available for its image we can determine the magnification, $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$. From the relation $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ we can then determine $s$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: We want the inverted image of the 4.50-m tall giraffe to fit within $4.82\,\mathrm{mm}$. Thus we have $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = \frac{y'}{y} = \frac{-0.00482}{4.50\,\mathrm{m}} = -\underline{1.071} \times 10^{-3}$. This gives, $s' = \underline{1.071} \times 10^{-3}s$. Next we apply $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ to obtain $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{\underline{1.071} \times 10^{-3}s} = \frac{934.6}{s} = \frac{1}{f}$, which gives us $s = \underline{934.6}f = \underline{934.6}(50\,\mathrm{mm}) = \underline{46.7}\,\mathrm{m}$.

questions.questionText: 25.9. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = \frac{y'}{y}$. You want $|y'| = 36\,\mathrm{mm}$. $f = 50.0\,\mathrm{mm}$. The image will be real, so $s' > 0$, $m < 0$ and $y' = -36\,\mathrm{mm}$.
25.10. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ so $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = \frac{y'}{y}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $s' = \frac{(1.50\,\mathrm{m})(6.50 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m})}{1.50\,\mathrm{m} - 6.50 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m}} = 6.53\,\mathrm{mm}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{6.53 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m}}{1.50\,\mathrm{m}} = -4.35 \times 10^{-3}$.

$$
|y'| = |m|\, y = (4.35 \times 10^{-3})(2.00\,\mathrm{m}) = 8.70\,\mathrm{mm}
$$



(b) $s' = \frac{(1.50\mathrm{~m})(19.5\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{~m})}{1.50\mathrm{~m} - 19.5\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{~m}} = 19.8\mathrm{~mm}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{19.8\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{~m}}{1.50\mathrm{~m}} = -1.32\times 10^{-2}$.

$$
\left| y ^ {\prime} \right| = \left| m \right| y = (1.32 \times 10^{-2}) (2.00 \mathrm{~m}) = 26.4 \mathrm{~mm}
$$

(c) The 19.5 mm distance is the telephoto focal length, since it produces greater magnification of the image.

questions.questionText: 25.11. $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} -\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$. From the shape of the cornea shown in the figure, $R_{1} = +7.80\mathrm{mm}$ and $R_{2} = +7.30\mathrm{mm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $\frac{1}{f} = (1.38 - 1)\left(\frac{1}{7.80\mathrm{mm}} -\frac{1}{7.30\mathrm{mm}}\right)$ and $f = -300\mathrm{mm}$. The power is $\frac{1}{-0.300\mathrm{m}} = -3.33$ diopters. $f < 0$ and the lens is diverging.

questions.questionText: 25.12. $s' = 2.50$ cm. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. From Table 25.1, the near point is $10$ cm.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'}$. For $s = 10 \, \text{cm}$, $f = \frac{(10 \, \text{cm})(2.50 \, \text{cm})}{10 \, \text{cm} + 2.50 \, \text{cm}} = 2.00 \, \text{cm}$. For $s \to \infty$, $f = s' = 2.50 \, \text{cm}$.

Reflect: The eye changes the focal length of its lens by using muscles to change the shape of the lens.

questions.questionText: 25.13. From Table 25.1, the near point is $22\mathrm{cm}$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = \frac{y'}{y}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $s = 22 \, \text{cm}$, $s' = 2.50 \, \text{cm}$. $f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'} = \frac{(22 \, \text{cm})(2.50 \, \text{cm})}{22 \, \text{cm} + 2.50 \, \text{cm}} = 2.24 \, \text{cm}$. The power is

$$
\frac{1}{0.0224 \, \mathrm{m}} = 44.6 \, \text{diopters}
$$

(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{2.50 \, \text{cm}}{22 \, \text{cm}} = -0.114$. $|y'| = my = (0.114)(6.50 \, \text{cm}) = 7.41 \, \text{mm}$. $m < 0$ so the image is inverted. $s' > 0$ so the image is real.

(c) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{2.50 \, \text{cm}}{225 \, \text{cm}} = -0.0111$. $|y'| = |m|y = (0.0111)(6.50 \, \text{cm}) = 0.722 \, \text{mm}$

questions.questionText: Problem 25.14
questions.expectedAnswer:
*25.14. Set Up: A diopter is an inverse meter, so the power in diopters of a lens is simply the inverse of its focal length, with the focal length expressed in meters. Use $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ to calculate the image distance.

(a) $f_{\min} = \frac{1}{150} \, \mathrm{m} = 6.67 \, \mathrm{mm}$. $f_{\max} = \frac{1}{115} \, \mathrm{m} = 8.70 \, \mathrm{mm}$.

(b) For a distant object $s \to \infty$ and $s' = f$. At minimum power, $f = f_{\max}$ so $s' = 8.70 \, \mathrm{mm}$.

(c) At maximum power, $f = f_{\min} = 6.67 \, \mathrm{mm}$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ so

$$
s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(25 \, \mathrm{cm})(0.667 \, \mathrm{cm})}{25 \, \mathrm{cm} - 0.667 \, \mathrm{cm}} = 0.685 \, \mathrm{cm} = 6.85 \, \mathrm{mm}
$$

Reflect: Since the distance from the lens to the retina is fixed, this eye won't focus clearly on both objects.

questions.questionText: 25.15. For an object $25.0 \, \text{cm}$ from the eye, the corrective lens forms a virtual image at the near point of the eye.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The person is farsighted.

(b) A converging lens is needed.



(c) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'} = \frac{(25.0\ \mathrm{cm})(-45.0\ \mathrm{cm})}{25.0\ \mathrm{cm} - 45.0\ \mathrm{cm}} = +56.2\ \mathrm{cm}$. The power is $\frac{1}{0.562\ \mathrm{m}} = +1.78$ diopters.

questions.questionText: 25.16. For an object at infinity, the glasses need to create an image at the person’s far point, which is 100 cm. Since the glasses are 2.0 cm from the eyeball, the image should be at 100 cm – 2.0 cm = 98 cm from the glasses, on the same side as the object. Thus, we have $s = \infty$ and $s' = -98$ cm. Use $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ to find the focal length $f$ of the lens. Take the inverse of $f$ (expressed in meters) to obtain the power of the lens.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\infty} + \frac{1}{-98\ \mathrm{cm}} = \frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow f = -98\ \mathrm{cm} = -0.98\ \mathrm{m}$. The power is $\frac{1}{-0.98\ \mathrm{m}} = -1.0$ diopters.

Reflect: In Problem 25.15 the contact lenses have power 1.78 diopters. The power of the lenses is different for ordinary glasses versus contact lenses.

questions.questionText: 25.17. For an object very far from the eye, the corrective lens forms a virtual image at the far point of the eye.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The person is nearsighted.

(b) A diverging lens is needed.

(c) $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $s \to \infty$ so $f = s' = -75.0\ \mathrm{cm}$. The power is $\frac{1}{-0.750\ \mathrm{m}} = -1.33$ diopters.

questions.questionText: 25.18. Apply $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. The near point is at infinity, so that is where the image must be formed for any objects that are close. The power in diopters is equal to $\frac{1}{f}$, with $f$ in meters.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{24\ \mathrm{cm}} + \frac{1}{-\infty} = \frac{1}{0.24\ \mathrm{m}} = 4.2$ diopters.

Reflect: To focus on closer objects, the power must be increased.

questions.questionText: 25.19. To correct the close vision, for an object $25.0\ \mathrm{cm}$ from the eye the lens forms a virtual image $35.0\ \mathrm{cm}$ from the eye. For a distant object the corrective lens forms a virtual image $50.0\ \mathrm{cm}$ from the eye.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $s = 25.0\ \mathrm{cm}$, $s' = -35.0\ \mathrm{cm}$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$.

$$
f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'} = \frac{(25.0\ \mathrm{cm})(-35.0\ \mathrm{cm})}{25.0\ \mathrm{cm} - 35.0\ \mathrm{cm}} = 87.5\ \mathrm{cm}
$$

The power is $\frac{1}{0.875\ \mathrm{m}} = 1.14$ diopters.

(b) $s \to \infty$, $s' = -50.0\ \mathrm{cm}$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives $f = s' = -50.0\ \mathrm{cm}$. The power is $\frac{1}{-0.500\ \mathrm{m}} = -2.00$ diopters.

questions.questionText: 25.20. Apply the equation $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ to determine $\frac{1}{f}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: We want the image of the computer screen that is formed by the student’s eyeglasses to be located at the far point of her vision. Assuming that the distance between her glasses and eye is negligible this means that $s' = -17\ \mathrm{cm}$ when $s = 45\ \mathrm{cm}$. Thus we have $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.45\ \mathrm{m}} + \frac{1}{-0.17\ \mathrm{m}} = -3.7$ diopters.



Reflect: The eyeglass lens forms a virtual image at your far point so that your relaxed eye can comfortably view the computer screen, which is beyond your far point.

questions.questionText: 25.21. To correct near vision, the lens forms a virtual image at the near point when the object is 25 cm from the eye. To correct far vision, the lens forms a virtual image at the far point of the eye. The power in diopters equals 1/f, when f is in meters.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{f} = +2.75$ diopters, so $f = \frac{1}{2.75} \mathrm{~m} = 36.4 \mathrm{~cm}$. Find $s'$ for $s = 25 \mathrm{~cm}$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$.

$$
s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(25.0 \mathrm{~cm})(36.4 \mathrm{~cm})}{25.0 \mathrm{~cm} - 36.4 \mathrm{~cm}} = -79.8 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

The near point is 79.8 cm from the eye.

(b) $\frac{1}{f} = -1.30$ diopters so $f = -0.769\mathrm{m} = -76.9\mathrm{cm}$. Find $s'$ for $s \to \infty$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives $s' = f = -76.9\mathrm{cm}$.

The far point is 76.9 cm from the eye.

questions.questionText: Problem 25.22
questions.expectedAnswer:
25.22. Set Up: To correct the near vision of the hyperopic eye, the corrective lens forms a virtual image at the near point for an object that is 25 cm from the eye. To correct the far vision of the myopic eye, the corrective lens forms a virtual image at the far point for an object that is very far from the eye. Power in diopters equals 1/f, when f is in meters.

(a) $s' = -60.0\mathrm{cm}$, $s = +25.0\mathrm{cm}$. $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{0.250\mathrm{m}} + \frac{1}{-0.600\mathrm{m}} = +2.33$ diopters.

(b) $s' = -60.0\mathrm{cm}$, $s \to \infty$. $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{-0.600\mathrm{m}} = -1.67$ diopters.

questions.questionText: 25.23. For a thin lens, $-\frac{s'}{s} = \frac{y'}{y}$, so $\left|\frac{y'}{s'}\right| = \left|\frac{y}{s}\right|$, and the angular size of the image equals the angular size of the object. The object has angular size $\theta = \frac{y}{f}$, with $\theta$ in radians.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\theta = \frac{y}{f} \Rightarrow f = \frac{y}{\theta} = \frac{2.00 \mathrm{~mm}}{0.025 \mathrm{rad}} = 80 \mathrm{~mm} = 8.0 \mathrm{~cm}$

Reflect: If the insect is at the near point of a normal eye, its angular size is $\frac{2.00\mathrm{mm}}{250\mathrm{mm}} = 0.00800$ rad.

questions.questionText: 25.24. When the object is at the focal point of the lens and the image therefore is at infinity, then $M = \frac{25.0 \, \text{cm}}{f}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $M = \frac{25.0\mathrm{cm}}{11.5\mathrm{cm}} = 2.17\times$

(b) The object is at the focal point of the lens, so the lens is 11.5 cm from the stamp.

questions.questionText: 25.25. When the object is at the focal point the image is at infinity and $M = \frac{25.0\mathrm{cm}}{f}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $M = \frac{25.0\mathrm{cm}}{6.00\mathrm{cm}} = 4.17\times$

(b) The object can be brought as close as the focal length of the lens, 6.00 cm.

questions.questionText: 25.26. The image is not at infinity, so the equation $ M = \frac{25.0 \, \text{cm}}{f} $ does not apply. The image is virtual, so $ s' = -25.0 \, \text{cm} $.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $ \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} $. $ s = \frac{s'f}{s' - f} = \frac{(-25.0\,\text{cm})(8.00\,\text{cm})}{-25.0\,\text{cm} - 8.00\,\text{cm}} = +6.06\,\text{cm} $

(b) $ m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{-25.0\,\text{cm}}{6.06\,\text{cm}} = +4.13 $. $ |y'| = |m|y = (4.13)(1.00\,\text{mm}) = 4.13\,\text{mm} $

Reflect: The lens allows the object to be brought much closer to the eye (6.06 cm) than the near point.

questions.questionText: 25.27. We can use $ \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} $ to determine the location of the image formed by the objective lens and $ m_1 = -\frac{s'}{s} $ to determine the magnification of the objective. The magnification of the eyepiece can then be determined by using $ M_2 = \frac{25.0\,\text{cm}}{f} $.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Solving for the location of the image we have $ s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(12.8\,\text{mm})(12.0\,\text{mm})}{12.8\,\text{mm} - 12.0\,\text{mm}} = 192\,\text{mm} $.

(b) We can find the linear magnification of the objective using $ m_1 = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{192\,\text{mm}}{12.8\,\text{mm}} = -15\times $, which may be written as $ 15\times $ if we are not interested in the orientation of the image relative to the object.

(c) The overall angular magnification is $ M = m_1M_1 = 15\frac{25\,\text{cm}}{2.5\,\text{cm}} = 150\times $.

Reflect: It is customary to ignore the sign of the magnification in cases where the relative orientation of the image relative to that of the object is not of interest.

questions.questionText: 25.28. for the objective, $ f_1 = 10.0\,\text{mm} $ and $ s_1 = f_1 + 2.0\,\text{mm} = 12.0\,\text{mm} $. $ M = m_1M_2 $.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $ \frac{1}{s_1} + \frac{1}{s_1'} = \frac{1}{f_1} $. $ s_1' = \frac{s_1 f_1}{s_1 - f_1} = \frac{(12.0\,\text{mm})(10.0\,\text{mm})}{12.0\,\text{mm} - 10.0\,\text{mm}} = 60.0\,\text{mm} $.

(b) $ m_1 = -\frac{s_1'}{s_1} = -\frac{60.0\,\text{mm}}{12.0\,\text{mm}} = -5.00 $. $ M_2 = \frac{25\,\text{cm}}{f_2} = \frac{25\,\text{cm}}{1.50\,\text{cm}} = 16.7\times $. $ M = (-5.00)(16.7) = -83.3\times $. The minus sign is customarily ignored.

Reflect: Equation (25.4) gives $ M = \frac{(25\,\text{cm})s_1'}{f_1 f_2} = \frac{(25\,\text{cm})(6.00\,\text{cm})}{(1.00\,\text{cm})(1.50\,\text{cm})} = 100\times $. Equation (25.4) assumes that $ s_1 = f_1 $ that this is only approximately the case here.

questions.questionText: 25.29. For each lens, $ \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} $. $ m = -\frac{s'}{s} = \frac{y'}{y} $. The image formed by the objective serves as the object for the eyepiece.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $ s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(13\,\text{mm})(12\,\text{mm})}{13\,\text{mm} - 12\,\text{mm}} = 156\,\text{mm} $. $ m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{156\,\text{mm}}{13\,\text{mm}} = -12 $.

$$
|y'| = |m|y = 12(1.2\,\text{mm}) = 14\,\text{mm}.
$$

(b) $ s' = \infty $ and $ f = 2.5\,\text{cm} $. $ s = 2.5\,\text{cm} $.

(c) $ m_1 = -\frac{s_1'}{s_1} = -\frac{-156\,\text{mm}}{12\,\text{mm}} = -12 $. $ M = m_1M_2 = (-12)\left(\frac{25\,\text{cm}}{2.50\,\text{cm}}\right) = -120\times $. Equation (25.4) gives



$$
M = \frac {(25 \mathrm{~cm}) s _ {1} ^ {\prime}}{f _ {1} f _ {2}} = \frac {(25 \mathrm{~cm}) (15.6 \mathrm{~cm})}{(1.2 \mathrm{~cm}) (2.5 \mathrm{~cm})} = 130 \times
$$

Equation (25.4) omits the minus sign and is only approximately correct.

questions.questionText: 25.30. We will assume that the given lateral magnification of the objective was calculated by using the equation $\left|m_1\right| = \frac{s_1'}{s_1}$ (and not the approximate equation $\left|m_1\right| \approx \frac{s_1'}{f_1}$) and the angular magnification of the eyepiece is given by $M_2 = \frac{(25.0\mathrm{cm})}{f_2}$. The overall magnification of the microscope is then $M = m_1M_2$. Because the image of the objective is located very close to the focal point of the eyepiece, we have $16.5\mathrm{cm} = s_1' + f_2$. Note that we will not use the approximation $s_1 \approx f_1$ since it is not necessary given the information we have.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The overall magnification of the microscope is $M = m_{1}M_{2} = (62)(10) = 620 \times$.

(b) We can immediately determine the focal length of the eyepiece from $f_{2} = \frac{(25\mathrm{cm})}{M_{2}} = \frac{(25\mathrm{cm})}{10} = 2.5\mathrm{cm}$.

Since the image of the objective is located very close to the focal point of the eyepiece we have $s_1' = 16.5 \, \mathrm{cm} - 2.5 \, \mathrm{cm} = 14.0 \, \mathrm{cm}$. Thus, we obtain $s_1 = \frac{s_1'}{|m_1|} = \frac{14.0 \, \mathrm{cm}}{62} = 0.226 \, \mathrm{cm}$. Finally, we obtain the focal length of the objective: $f_1 = \frac{s_1 s_1'}{s_1 + s_1'} = \frac{(0.226 \, \mathrm{cm})(14.0 \, \mathrm{cm})}{0.226 \, \mathrm{cm} + 14.0 \, \mathrm{cm}} = 0.222 \, \mathrm{cm}$.

Reflect: If we assume that the magnification of the objective was calculated by using the approximate equation $\left|m_1\right| = 62 \approx \frac{s_1'}{f_1}$ (with $s_1' = 14.0 \, \mathrm{cm}$), we obtain slightly different answers for $f_1$ and $s_1$: $f_1 = 0.226 \, \mathrm{cm}$ and $s_1 = 0.230 \, \mathrm{cm}$.

questions.questionText: 25.31. Since the image formed by the eyepiece is at infinity, the angular magnification for the eyepiece is $M_2 = \frac{25\mathrm{cm}}{f}$. The overall angular magnification of the microscope is $M = m_1M_2$. The image formed by the objective is the object for the eyepiece and is at the focal point of the eyepiece.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The image formed by the objective is $19.7\mathrm{cm} - 1.80\mathrm{cm} = 17.9\mathrm{cm}$ from the lens. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives

$$
s = \frac {s ^ {\prime} f}{s ^ {\prime} - f} = \frac {(17.9 \mathrm{~cm}) (0.800 \mathrm{~cm})}{17.9 \mathrm{~cm} - 0.800 \mathrm{~cm}} = 0.837 \mathrm{~cm} = 8.37 \mathrm{~mm}
$$

(b) $m_{1} = -\frac{s^{\prime}}{s} = -\frac{17.9\mathrm{cm}}{0.837\mathrm{cm}} = -21.4\times$

(c) $M = m_{1}M_{2} = m_{1}\left(\frac{25\mathrm{cm}}{f_{2}}\right) = (-21.4)\left(\frac{25\mathrm{cm}}{1.80\mathrm{cm}}\right) = -297\times$. Equation (25.4) gives

$$
M = \frac {(25 \mathrm{~cm}) s _ {1} ^ {\prime}}{f _ {1} f _ {2}} = \frac {(25 \mathrm{~cm}) (17.9 \mathrm{~cm})}{(0.800 \mathrm{~cm}) (1.80 \mathrm{~cm})} = 311 \times
$$

Equation (25.4) omits the minus sign and is only approximately correct.

Solve: (a) The largest $M$ is for the smallest $f_{1}$ and largest $M_{2}$. $M_{2} = 10 \times$.

$f = 1.9\mathrm{mm}$ and $s_1^{\prime} = 120\mathrm{mm} + 1.9\mathrm{mm} = 121.9\mathrm{mm}$



$$
M = \left(\frac{121.9 \text{ mm}}{1.9 \text{ mm}}\right) (10) = 640 \times
$$

(b) The smallest $M$ is for the largest $f_1$ and the smallest $M_2$. $M_2 = 5 \times$,
$f_1 = 16 \text{ mm}$ and $s_1' = 120 \text{ mm} + 16 \text{ mm} = 136 \text{ mm}$.
$$
M = \left(\frac{136 \text{ mm}}{16 \text{ mm}}\right) (5) = 43 \times
$$

Solve: (a) $M = \frac{(25 \text{ cm})(16.50 \text{ cm})}{(0.500 \text{ cm})(2.60 \text{ cm})} = 317 \times$
