(b) The minimum separation is $\frac{0.10 \text{ mm}}{M} = \frac{0.10 \text{ mm}}{317} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}$.

questions.questionText: 25.34. $M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2}$. The largest $M$ is for the smallest $f_2$, where $f_2$ is the focal length of the eyepiece.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $M_{\max} = -\frac{(16.0 \text{ in.})(2.54 \text{ cm/in.})}{1.5 \text{ cm}} = -27 \times$;
$$
M_{\min} = -\frac{(16.0 \text{ in.})(2.54 \text{ cm/in.})}{8.5 \text{ cm}} = -4.8 \times
$$

Reflect: The focal length of the objective is $f_1 = 16.0 \text{ in.} = 40.6 \text{ cm}$ and $f_1 > f_2$ for each eyepiece.

questions.questionText: 25.35. Since the final image is at infinity, $M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2}$. The length of the telescope is $f_1 + f_2$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $f_1 + f_2 = 1.80 \text{ m}$, so $f_1 = 1.80 \text{ m} - f_1 = 180 \text{ cm} - 9.00 \text{ cm} = 171 \text{ cm}$.
$$
M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2} = -\frac{171 \text{ cm}}{9.00 \text{ cm}} = -19.0 \times
$$

questions.questionText: 25.36. $M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2}$. The length of the telescope is $L = f_1 + f_2$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $M$ is largest when $f_2 < f_1$. Use the 980 mm lens as the eyepiece and the 1710 mm lens as the objective. $L = f_1 + f_2 = 1710 \text{ mm} + 980 \text{ mm} = 2690 \text{ mm} = 2.69 \text{ m}$.

(b) $M = -\frac{1710 \text{ mm}}{980 \text{ mm}} = -1.74 \times$
(c) $\theta' = M\theta = |-1.74|(0.5^{\circ}) = 0.9^{\circ}$

questions.questionText: 25.37. We use $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ to find the location of the image and $m = \frac{y'}{y} = -\frac{s'}{s}$ to determine the size of the image. From the appendix we find that the diameter of Mars is $2(3.40 \times 10^6 \text{ m}) = 6.80 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$ and that $1 \text{ mile} = 1.609 \times 10^3 \text{ m}$. At its closest approach Mars is $(35 \times 10^6 \text{ miles})\left(1.609 \times 10^3 \frac{\text{m}}{\text{mile}}\right) = 5.63 \times 10^{10} \text{ m}$ from the earth.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Note that $s$ is much larger than $f$ so that $\frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s} \approx \frac{1}{f}$ gives $s' = f = 57.6 \, \text{m}$. This gives

$$
\left| y ^ {\prime} \right| = \frac {\left| s ^ {\prime} \right.}{s} y = \frac {(57.6 \, \text{m}) (6.80 \times 10^{6} \, \text{m})}{5.63 \times 10^{10} \, \text{m}} = 7.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 7.0 \, \text{mm}.
$$

Reflect: You may easily verify the approximation used since $\frac{1}{57.6 \, \text{m}} = 0.0174 \, (\text{m})^{-1}$ and

$$
\frac {1}{5.63 \times 10^{10} \, \text{m}} = 1.8 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}^{-1}.
$$

questions.questionText:
25.38. The unaided angular size of Jupiter is $\theta = \frac{138,000 \, \text{km}}{6.28 \times 10^{8} \, \text{km}} = 2.20 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{rad} = 0.0126^{\circ}$.

$$
M = \frac {\theta^ {\prime}}{\theta} = - \frac {f _ {1}}{f _ {2}}. \quad f _ {1} = 19.4 \, \text{m}.
$$
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Need $M = \frac{0.500^{\circ}}{0.0126^{\circ}} = 39.7$. $f_{2} = \frac{f_{1}}{M} = \frac{19.4 \, \text{m}}{39.7} = 0.49 \, \text{m}$.

Reflect: The angular magnification is the ratio of the angular size of the image when the optical device is used divided by the angular size of the object.

questions.questionText: 25.39. The object is very far from the lens, so the image distance equals the focal length.

questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{1}{40.0 \, \text{cm}} + \frac{1.40}{2.60 \, \text{cm}} = \frac{0.40}{R}$ and $R = 0.710 \, \text{cm}$

Reflect: The cornea presents a convex surface to the object, so $R > 0$.

Solve: $\frac{1}{36.0 \, \text{cm}} + \frac{1.40}{s'} = \frac{0.40}{0.75 \, \text{cm}} \Rightarrow s' = 2.77 \, \text{cm}$.

Reflect: This distance is greater than the normal eye, which has a cornea vertex to retina distance of about $2.6 \, \text{cm}$.

questions.questionText: 25.42. When a single lens is used as a magnifier, a virtual image is formed and $m > 0$. $m = +6.50$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$ and $s' = -ms = -(6.50)s = -6.50s$. Then $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{-6.50s} = \frac{1}{f}$. $\frac{0.8462}{s} = \frac{1}{f}$ and $s = (0.8462)f = (0.8462)(4.00 \, \text{cm}) = 3.38 \, \text{cm}$. $s' = -6.50(3.38 \, \text{cm}) = -22.0 \, \text{cm}$. The flea is $3.38 \, \text{cm}$ from the lens and the image is $22.0 \, \text{cm}$ from the lens, on the same side of the lens as the flea.

questions.questionText: 25.43. Your near point is the closest distance at which you can clearly focus on an object and your far point is the greatest distance at which you can clearly focus on an object. To illustrate the solution, assume a near point of $75 \, \text{cm}$ and a far point of $4.0 \, \text{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Measure the near point and far point distance. The answers will vary from person to person.

(b) Assume contact lenses.



Close-up vision: For an object at $s = 25$ cm from the eye, the lens is to form a virtual image at $s' = -75$ cm. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives $f = \frac{ss'}{s + s'} = \frac{(25\mathrm{cm})(-75\mathrm{cm})}{25\mathrm{cm} + (-75\mathrm{cm})} = +38\mathrm{cm}$. The power is $\frac{1}{0.38\mathrm{m}} = +2.7$ diopters.

Distant vision: For an object at $s \to \infty$, the lens forms a virtual image at $s' = -400\mathrm{cm}$. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ gives $f = s' = -400\mathrm{cm}$. The power is $\frac{1}{-4.00\mathrm{m}} = -0.250$ diopters.

questions.questionText: 25.44. In the simplified model of the eye all of the refraction occurs at the outside surface of the eye and we can apply Eq. (24.11): $\frac{n_a}{s} + \frac{n_b}{s'} = \frac{n_b - n_a}{R}$ with $n_a = 1$ and $n_b = 1.40$. In this case, we take $s' = 2.75$ cm when $s = \infty$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: We can make the given substitutions to obtain $\frac{1.00}{\infty} + \frac{1.40}{2.75\mathrm{cm}} = \frac{1.40 - 1.00}{R}$. Solving for $R$ we obtain $R = 0.40\frac{2.75\mathrm{cm}}{1.40} = 0.786\mathrm{cm} = 0.79\mathrm{cm}$.

Reflect: In the simplified model of the eye we take advantage of the fact that the index of refraction of the crystalline lens, the aqueous humor, and the vitreous humor are all nearly equal to 1.40. This allows us to solve the problem by using Eq. (24.11).

questions.questionText: 25.45. The image is the same distance behind the mirror as the object is in front of the mirror. $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = \frac{y'}{y}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $s = 1.50 \, \text{m}$. $s' = \frac{sf}{s - f} = \frac{(1.50 \, \text{m})(19.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m})}{1.50 \, \text{m} - 19.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}} = 19.8 \, \text{mm}$

(b) $m = -\frac{s'}{s} = -\frac{19.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}}{1.50 \, \text{m}} = -0.0132$. $|y'| = |m|y = (0.0132)(8.0 \, \text{cm}) = 0.11 \, \text{cm}$

Reflect: The camera focuses on the image, behind the mirror, not on the mirror surface.

questions.questionText: 25.46. The "can distance" is the object distance $s$, and the "lens distance" is the image distance $s'$. Use $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. A plot of $\frac{1}{s'}$ as a function of $\frac{1}{s}$ gives $\frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s}$, so the $y$ intercept should be $y_0 = \frac{1}{f}$ and the slope of the line should be $\ell = -1$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The plot is shown below.



![img-158.jpeg](img-158.jpeg)

From the best-fit line, the $y$ intercept is $0.126 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$, so the focal length of the lens is $f = \frac{1}{y_0} = \frac{1}{0.126 \, \text{cm}^{-1}} = 7.97 \, \text{cm}$.

Reflect: The slope of the line is $\ell = -1.021 \pm 0.015$, which is reasonably close to the expected slope of $-1$.

questions.questionText: 25.47. The object distance is $s = 15.0 \, \text{cm}$. The focal length is $f = 2.50 \, \text{cm}$. The height of the object is $y = 1.40 \, \text{cm}$. Use $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$. For part (b), use $m = -\frac{s'}{s}$ and $|y'| = |m|y$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The image distance is $s' = \left( \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s} \right)^{-1} = \left( \frac{1}{2.50 \, \text{cm}} - \frac{1}{15.0 \, \text{cm}} \right)^{-1} = 3.00 \, \text{cm}$.

(b) The height of the image on the CCD chip is $|y'| = \left|\frac{s'}{s}\right|y = \frac{3.00 \, \text{cm}}{15.0 \, \text{cm}} (1.40 \, \text{cm}) = 0.280 \, \text{cm}$.

Reflect: This image size is reasonable to fit on a CCD chip.

questions.questionText: 25.48. The overall lateral magnification of the final image is $m = m_{1}m_{2}$. The image formed by the objective is the object for the eyepiece and the distance between the two lenses is $s_1' + s_2$.
25.49. The person's eye cannot focus on anything closer than 85.0 cm. The problem asks us to find the location of an object such that his old lenses produce a virtual image 85.0 cm from his eye.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\frac{1}{f} = 2.25$ diopters so $f = 44.4$ cm. The image is 85.0 cm from his eye so is 83.0 cm from the eyeglass lens.

$$
s = \frac {s ^ {\prime} f}{s ^ {\prime} - f} = \frac {(- 8 3 . 0 \mathrm {c m}) (4 4 . 4 \mathrm {c m})}{- 8 3 . 0 \mathrm {c m} - 4 4 . 4 \mathrm {c m}} = + 2 8. 9 \mathrm {c m}
$$

The object is 28.9 cm from the eyeglasses so is 30.9 cm from his eyes.

(c) Now $s' = -85.0$ cm.

$$
s = \frac {s ^ {\prime} f}{s ^ {\prime} - f} = \frac {(- 8 5 . 0 \mathrm {c m}) (4 4 . 4 \mathrm {c m})}{- 8 5 . 0 \mathrm {c m} - 4 4 . 4 \mathrm {c m}} = + 2 9. 2 \mathrm {c m}
$$

Reflect: The old glasses allow him to focus on objects as close as about 30 cm from his eyes. This is much better than the closest distance of 85 cm with no glasses, but his current glasses probably allow him to focus as close as 25 cm.

Solve: (a) $M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2} = -\frac{95.0\mathrm{cm}}{15.0\mathrm{cm}} = -6.33\times$

(b) $y = 60.0\mathrm{m}$. $s\gg f$ so $s^{\prime}\approx f = 95.0~\mathrm{cm}$

$$
m = - \frac {s ^ {\prime}}{s} = - \frac {0 . 9 5 0 \mathrm {m}}{3 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {3} \mathrm {m}} = - 3. 1 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}
$$

$$
\left| y ^ {\prime} \right| = \left| m \right| y = (3. 1 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4}) (6 0. 0 \mathrm {m}) = 1. 9 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} \mathrm {m} = 1. 9 0 \mathrm {c m}
$$

(c) $\theta^{\prime} = |M|\theta$. $\theta$ is the angular size of the object;

$$
\theta = \frac {y}{s} = \frac {6 0 . 0 \mathrm {m}}{3 . 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {3} \mathrm {m}} = 0. 0 2 0 0 \mathrm {r a d}
$$

$$
\theta = (6. 3 3) (0. 0 2 0 0 \mathrm {r a d}) = 0. 1 2 7 \mathrm {r a d} = 7. 2 8 ^ {\circ}.
$$

questions.questionText: 25.51. $\frac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} -\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$. For a double-convex lens, $R_{1} > 0$ and $R_{2} < 0$. $M = -\frac{f_1}{f_2}$. The length of the telescope is $f_{1} + f_{2}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Find the focal length of each lens. $L_{1}$: $\frac{1}{f} = (1.528 - 1)\left(\frac{1}{0.950\mathrm{m}} -\frac{1}{-2.700\mathrm{m}}\right)$ and $f = 133~\mathrm{cm}$. $L_{2}$: $\frac{1}{f} = (1.550 - 1)\left(\frac{1}{0.535\mathrm{m}} -\frac{1}{-50.5\mathrm{m}}\right)$ and $f = 96.3~\mathrm{cm}$.

The largest magnification would be with $L_{1}$ as the objective and $L_{2}$ as the eyepiece.

$$
M = - \frac {f _ {1}}{f _ {2}} = - \frac {1 3 3 \mathrm {c m}}{9 6 . 3 \mathrm {c m}} = - 1. 3 8 \times
$$

(b) The length of the telescope would be $133\mathrm{cm} + 96.3\mathrm{cm} = 229\mathrm{cm}$.

## Solutions to Passage Problems

questions.questionText:
25.52. When the insect is at $s_1 = 10 \, \text{cm}$, the image is at $s_1' = 8 \, \text{mm}$, so we can find the focal length of the frog lens by using $\frac{1}{s_1} + \frac{1}{s'_1} = \frac{1}{f}$. When the insect moves 5 cm farther away, it is at $s_2 = 10 \, \text{cm} + 5 \, \text{cm} = 15 \, \text{cm}$. The



new image distance can be found by using the (constant) focal length in $\frac{1}{s_2} + \frac{1}{s'_2} = \frac{1}{f}$. This new image distance can be compared to the original image distance of $s_1' = 8 \, \text{mm}$ to determine by how much the lens must move.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** The focal length of the frog lens is $f = \left(\frac{1}{s_1} + \frac{1}{s_1'}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{1}{10 \, \text{cm}} + \frac{1}{0.8 \, \text{cm}}\right)^{-1} = 0.74074 \, \text{cm}$. The new image distance is $s_2' = \left(\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{s_2}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{1}{0.74074 \, \text{cm}} - \frac{1}{15 \, \text{cm}}\right)^{-1} = 0.7792 \, \text{cm}$. The lens must therefore approach the retina by the distance $s_1' - s_2' = 0.02 \, \text{cm}$. The correct answer is A.

**25.53. Set Up:** The contact lens for the typical frog, which has a focal length of $f = \frac{1}{-6.0}$ m, should place the image of an object at infinity at the far point of the typical frog. We can therefore use $s = \infty$ in the equation $\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s'} = \frac{1}{f}$ to find the image distance $s'$, which will be the far point of the typical frog.

**Solve:** With $s = \infty$, we have $s' = f = -0.17 \, \text{m}$. Thus, the far point is 17 cm in front of the contact lens. The correct answer is D.

**Reflect:** Because the image distance is negative, it is located on the same side of the lens as the object (i.e., in front of the frog).

**25.54. Set Up:** In liquid, the lens equation becomes $\frac{1}{f} = \left(\frac{n}{n_{\mathrm{liq}}} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$, where $n_{\mathrm{liq}} = 1.33 < n$ is the index of refraction of water, and $n$ is the index of refraction of the frog lens (see previous chapter). In air, $n_{\mathrm{liq}}$ becomes $n_{\mathrm{air}} = 1.00$ so the focal length is shorter in air than in water.

**Solve:** If the focal length of the frog lens is shorter in air than in water, an object at infinity will be focused closer to the frog, and this will be the far point of the frog in air. Thus, the frog will be more nearsighted in air, and less nearsighted in water. The correct answer is B.

**Reflect:** As the index of refraction of a lens approaches that of the surrounding medium, the power of the lens decreases.

**25.55. Set Up:** Because the lenses are diverging, the lens with the shorter focal length will create an image closer to the frog.

**Solve:** Because $f \propto \frac{1}{\text{power}}$, the -15 diopter lens will oblige the frog to focus on a closer image. The correct answer is B.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q1: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 1. The analysis of Young's double-slit experiment is based on Huygens's principle (Section 23.11), which makes no distinction between longitudinal and transverse waves, so it describes sound waves just as well as electromagnetic waves. Therefore, Young's double-slit experiment would work with sound waves in the same way as for electromagnetic waves. This means that, after passing through an appropriately sized double-slit arrangement, sound waves will produce an interference pattern analogous to that produced by electromagnetic waves. A double-slit experiment with sound is therefore possible. However, sound waves have much longer wavelengths than visible light, so the barrier in which the double-slit arrangement is made would have to be much, much larger than for visible light (also to prevent the sound from diffracting around the edges of the barrier). In addition, it is hard to find a material that absorbs sound sufficiently well to ensure that no sound is transmitted through the barrier. To avoid these problems, the experiment could be done by replacing the double-slit arrangement with two speakers emitting coherently.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q2: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 2. The position of the bright fringes in Young's double-slit experiment is given by Eq. 26.6,  $y_{m} = R\frac{m\mathring{A}}{d}$ . Thus, as the separation  $d$  between the slits increases, the distance  $y_{m}$  between the bright fringes and the central maximum decreases, so the interference pattern would shrink inward toward the central bright fringe.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q3: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 3. The two headlights on cars are not mutually coherent, which means that the relative phase between the light emitted by each headlight changes continuously and randomly. In addition, headlights emit a broad range of wavelengths (i.e., they are not monochromatic). As a result, the superposition of these two beams of light produces only a constantly and randomly changing interference pattern; in other words, no interference pattern.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q4: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
4. The position of the bright fringes in Young's double-slit experiment is given by Eq. 26.6,  $y_{m} = R\frac{m\mathring{A}}{d}$ . For  $d = 0.25\mathrm{m}$ , the angular separation between the central bright fringe and the adjacent bright fringes ( $m = \pm 1$ ) would be  $\theta_{\pm 1} \approx \frac{y_{\pm 1}}{R} = \frac{(\pm 1)(633\mathrm{nm})}{0.25\mathrm{m}} \approx \pm 10^{-6}\mathrm{rad} = \pm 10^{-4}$  degrees. To obtain a separation  $y_{1} \approx 1\mathrm{cm}$  between adjacent bright fringes would require placing the screen a distance  $R \approx \frac{y_1}{\theta_1} = \frac{10^{-1}\mathrm{m}}{10^{-6}\mathrm{rad}} = 10^5\mathrm{m}$  from the slits, which would clearly not be feasible. Placing the screen at a reasonable distance of  $R \approx 0.25\mathrm{m}$  would produce a fringe spacing of



$y_{\pm 1} = (0.25\mathrm{m})\frac{(\pm 1)(633\mathrm{nm})}{0.25\mathrm{m}}\approx \pm 6\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{m}$ . This spacing between bright fringes would be very difficult to observe and would most likely be made completely unobservable by imperfections in the slit and on the screen. Thus, although the interference pattern would exist, it would not be observable.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q5: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 5. The position of the bright fringes in Young's double-slit experiment is given by Eq. 26.6,  $y_{m} = R\frac{m\lambda}{d}$ . Because white light contains all visible wavelengths ( $400\mathrm{nm} \lesssim \lambda \lesssim 700\mathrm{nm}$ ), each bright fringe will become a bright "rainbow" because the different wavelengths will produce bright fringes at different positions  $y_{m}$ . The shorter wavelengths (blue) will be nearer the central bright fringe (which will be white), whereas the longer wavelengths (red) will be farther from the central bright fringe. Dark regions will appear between adjacent bright rainbows if  $y_{m+1,\min} > y_{m,\max}$ , which means that  $R\frac{(m+1)\lambda_{\min}}{d} > R\frac{m\lambda_{\max}}{d} \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda_{\min}}{\lambda_{\max}} > \frac{m}{m+1}$ . This inequality is satisfied for  $\lambda_{\min} = 400\mathrm{nm}$ ,  $\lambda_{\max} = 700\mathrm{nm}$  only for  $m = 0$  and 1, which means that a dark band will appear between the central (white) bright fringe and the first bright rainbow fringe and between the first and second bright rainbow fringes. For  $m \geq 2$ , the  $m$ th bright fringe for red light for will overlap with the  $(m+1)$ st bright fringe for blue light, hiding the dark bands.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q6: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 6. Visible light is not diffracted by the lattice of crystals because the wavelength of visible light ( $\approx 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{m}$ ) is much greater than the lattice spacing ( $\approx 10^{-10} \, \mathrm{m}$ ). As a result, visible light interacts with about 1000 lattice points at a time and does not "feel" the spacing between lattice points. X-rays, on the other hand, have wavelengths on the order of  $10^{-10} \, \mathrm{m}$ , so x-rays are sensitive to the spacing between lattice points and diffract. In other words, the shorter wavelength of x-rays allows them to resolve the atomic structure of crystals whereas the longer wavelength of visible light prevents it from doing so.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q7: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 7. The resolution limit for a circular aperture (such as used in microscopes) is given by Eq. 26.13,  $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22\lambda /D$  where  $D$  is the diameter of the aperture. Thus, for a given aperture diameter  $D$ , better resolution (i.e., smaller  $\theta_{\mathrm{res}}$ ) will be obtained by using blue light rather than red light because blue light has a shorter wavelength than red light.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q8: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 8. The different colors reflected from an oil film on water indicate regions of different film thickness (see Eqs. 26.7 and 26.8). Light is reflected from the front and back surface of the film, so only the wavelength for which these two reflections interfere constructively survives the reflection; the other wavelengths interfere destructively and are much less intense. A fringe reflecting a single color indicates a region of constant film thickness, so these fringes must be closed just as elevation lines on a topographical map are closed.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q9: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 9. For a single-slit diffraction pattern, Eq. 26.9,  $\sin \theta = \frac{m\lambda}{a}$ , where  $a$  is the slit width, gives the angle  $\theta$  at which the dark fringes are located. Thus, as the slit width  $a$  decreases, the angular separation between the two first-order ( $m = \pm 1$ ) dark fringes increases. This means that the width of the central bright region increases and that, in general, the diffraction pattern spreads out.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q10: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer:
10. See drawing at left below for the soap film in air. Reflection 1 from the air-film interface undergoes a half-cycle phase shift at the reflection because  $n_{\mathrm{air}} < n_{\mathrm{film}}$ , whereas reflection 2 from the film-air interface undergoes zero phase shift at the reflection because  $n_{\mathrm{film}} > n_{\mathrm{air}}$ . If the film is very thin, the light travels a very short distance in the



film, which introduces only a negligible phase shift to the light reflected from the back surface (reflection 2). Thus, reflection 1 is a half-cycle out of phase with reflection 2, resulting in complete destructive interference, so the film appears black (i.e., no light is reflected from it). Now consider the drawing below at right: for this equally thin soap film on glass, the indexes of refraction get larger in going from air to film to glass, so the reflection 1 from the air-film interface and reflection 2 from the film-glass interface both undergo half-cycle phase shifts due to these reflections. Thus, the difference in phase shift between these two reflections is only due to the phase shift accumulated by the light that travels back and forth through the film. For a very thin film, this phase shift is negligible, so the reflection 1 from the air-film interface is in phase with reflection 2 from the film-glass interface, resulting in complete constructive interference. Therefore, the film appears bright.

![img-159.jpeg](img-159.jpeg)

![img-160.jpeg](img-160.jpeg)

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q11: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 11. For diffraction from a single slit, Eq. 26.9,  $\sin \theta = \frac{m\lambda}{a}$ , where  $a$  is the slit width, gives the angle  $\theta$  at which the dark fringes are located. For  $a = 4.5\lambda$ , we have  $\sin \theta = \frac{m}{4.5}$ . Because  $-1 \leq \sin \theta \leq 1$ , only orders  $m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \pm 4$  are possible. Thus, 8 dark fringes should appear on the screen behind the slit. In general, the number  $n$  of dark fringes that appear is  $n = 2\mathrm{int}\left(\frac{a}{\lambda}\right)$ , where the function "int" gives the integer part of its argument.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q12: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 12. The resolution limit for a telescope is given by Eq. 26.13,  $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22\lambda /D$ , where  $D$  is the diameter of the aperture. The wavelength of visible light is of the order of  $10^{-7}\mathrm{m}$ , whereas the wavelength of radio waves is on the order of  $10^{-3}\mathrm{m}$  or more. Thus, the aperture of a radio telescope must be about  $10^{4}$  times larger than that of an optical telescope to get the same resolution. If an optical telescope has an aperture size  $D\approx 1\mathrm{m}$ , then a radio telescope must have an aperture size  $D\approx 10^{4}\mathrm{m}$  to get a similar resolution.

questions.questionText: Chapter 26 — Conceptual Q13: (Study this explanation then answer from memory)
questions.expectedAnswer: 13. Example 26.9 gives  $D = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}$  as the size of a human pupil. Using this as the size of an eagle's pupil, and assuming that an eagle's vision is sharpest at  $500 \, \mathrm{nm}$ , the angular resolution of an eagle is  $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D} = 1.22 \frac{500 \times 10^{-9} \, \mathrm{m}}{5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}} = 1.22 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{rad}$ . At a distance of  $3 \, \mathrm{km}$ , the largest separation  $s$  between two objects that an eagle could resolve is  $\frac{s}{3 \times 10^{3} \, \mathrm{m}} = \theta_{\mathrm{res}} \Rightarrow s = (3 \times 10^{3} \, \mathrm{m}) (1.22 \times 10^{-4}) = 0.4 \, \mathrm{m}$ . The separation between the tips of a rabbit's ears is less than  $40 \, \mathrm{cm}$ , so they are too close together for an eagle to resolve from a distance of  $3 \, \mathrm{km}$  away.

questions.questionText: 26.1. Constructive interference occurs for $r_2 - r_1 = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. Destructive interference occurs for $r_2 - r_1 = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. For this problem, $r_2 = 150$ cm and $r_1 = x$. The path taken by the person ensures that $x$ is in the range $0 \leq x \leq 150$ cm.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $150\mathrm{cm} - x = m(34\mathrm{cm})$. $x = 150\mathrm{cm} - m(34\mathrm{cm})$. For $m = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$ the values of $x$ are $150\mathrm{cm}$, $116\mathrm{cm}$, $82\mathrm{cm}$, $48\mathrm{cm}$, $14\mathrm{cm}$.

(b) $150\mathrm{cm} - x = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)(34\mathrm{cm})$. $x = 150\mathrm{cm} - \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)(34\mathrm{cm})$. For $m = 0, 1, 2, 3$ the values of $x$ are $133\mathrm{cm}$, $99\mathrm{cm}$, $65\mathrm{cm}$, $31\mathrm{cm}$.

Reflect: When $x = 116$ cm the path difference is $150 \, \text{cm} - 116 \, \text{cm} = 34 \, \text{cm}$, which is one wavelength. When $x = 133$ cm the path difference is $17 \, \text{cm}$, which is one half wavelength.

questions.questionText:
26.2. For radio waves, $\upsilon = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m/s}$.

$$
\lambda = \frac {\upsilon}{f} = \frac {3.00 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m/s}}{92.0 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~Hz}} = 3.26 \mathrm{~m}
$$

Constructive interference occurs for $r_2 - r_1 = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. Destructive interference occurs for $r_2 - r_1 = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. For this problem, $r_2 = x$ and $r_1 = 50.0\mathrm{m}$. The path taken by the person ensures that $x$ is greater than $50.0\mathrm{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $x - 50.0\mathrm{m} = m(3.26\mathrm{m})$. $x = 50.0\mathrm{m} + m(3.26\mathrm{m})$. For $m = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$, $x = 50.0\mathrm{m}$, $53.3\mathrm{m}$, $56.5\mathrm{m}$, $59.8\mathrm{m}$, $63.0\mathrm{cm}$.

(b) $x - 50.0\mathrm{m} = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)(3.26\mathrm{m})$. $x = 50.0\mathrm{m} + \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)(3.26\mathrm{m})$. For $m = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$, $x = 51.6\mathrm{m}$, $54.9\mathrm{m}$, $58.2\mathrm{m}$, $61.4\mathrm{m}$, $64.7\mathrm{m}$.

questions.questionText: 26.3. The wavelength of the radiation is $\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m/s}}{15 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~Hz}} = 20 \mathrm{~m}$. Let the origin be at antenna A, with antenna B at $120 \mathrm{~m}$ in the positive $x$ direction. The distance $d_{\mathrm{A}}$ from antenna A to point $Q$ on the line joining the antennas is $d_{\mathrm{A}} = x$. The distance $d_{\mathrm{B}}$ from antenna B to point $Q$ is $d_{\mathrm{B}} = 120 \mathrm{~m} - x$. The path-length difference $\Delta$ is $\Delta = \left| d_{\mathrm{A}} - d \right|_{\mathrm{B}} = 120 - 2x$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) For constructive interference, $m\lambda = \Delta = 120\mathrm{m} - 2x \Rightarrow x = \frac{120\mathrm{m} - m\lambda}{2}$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. The smallest nonzero distance is for $m = 5$, which gives $x = 10\mathrm{m}$.

(b) For destructive interference, $\left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda = \Delta = 120\mathrm{m} - 2x \Rightarrow x = \frac{120\mathrm{m} - \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda}{2}$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. The smallest nonzero distance is for $m = 5$, which gives $x = 5\mathrm{m}$.

Reflect: Note that the sign of the path-length difference is not important, just its absolute value.

questions.questionText: 26.4. $\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$. When the path-length difference $\Delta = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$, you get constructive interference when the sources are in phase. When the path-length difference $\Delta = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$, you get destructive interference when the sources are in phase.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{340.0\ \mathrm{m/s}}{170.0\ \mathrm{Hz}} = 2.000\ \mathrm{m}$

(b) At the midpoint between the two speakers the path difference is $\Delta = 0$, so the criterion for constructive interference is satisfied for $m = 0$. Thus, the microphone detects a maximum in sound intensity.

(c) The path becomes $0.5\ \mathrm{m}$ longer for the left speaker and $0.5\ \mathrm{m}$ shorter for the right speaker, so the path-length difference is $\Delta = 1\ \mathrm{m}$. The criterion for destructive interference is thus satisfied for $m = 0$, so the microphone detects a minimum in sound intensity.

(d) The path becomes $1\ \mathrm{m}$ shorter for the left speaker and $1\ \mathrm{m}$ longer for the right speaker, so the path-length difference is $\Delta = 2\ \mathrm{m}$. The criterion for constructive interference is thus satisfied for $m = 1$, so the microphone detects a maximum in sound intensity.

Reflect: When two waves are superposed on a line joining the sources, maxima and minima occur at half wavelength intervals. We could have used this reasoning to deduce the answers for parts (b) and (c) based on the answer to part (a).

questions.questionText: 26.5. When the path-length difference $\Delta = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$, you get destructive interference when the sources are in phase. When the path-length difference $\Delta = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$, you get constructive interference when the sources are in phase. Recall that $\lambda = 2.000\ \mathrm{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (b) At the midpoint between the two speakers the path difference is $\Delta = 0$, so the criterion for destructive interference is satisfied for $m = 0$. Thus, the microphone detects a minimum in sound intensity.

(c) The path becomes $0.5\ \mathrm{m}$ longer for the left speaker and $0.5\ \mathrm{m}$ shorter for the right speaker, so the path-length difference is $\Delta = 1\ \mathrm{m}$. The criterion for constructive interference is thus satisfied for $m = 0$, so the microphone detects a maximum in sound intensity.

(d) The path becomes $1\ \mathrm{m}$ shorter for the left speaker and $1\ \mathrm{m}$ longer for the right speaker, so the path-length difference is $\Delta = 2\ \mathrm{m}$. The criterion for destructive interference is thus satisfied for $m = 1$, so the microphone detects a minimum in sound intensity.

questions.questionText: 26.6. Since the values of $m$ are rather large, don't use Eq. (26.6). $y_{m} = R\tan \theta_{m}$. For bright fringes, $d\sin \theta = m\lambda$. For dark fringes, $d\sin \theta = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$. For the fifth bright fringe (not counting the central bright spot), $m = 5$ and for the eighth dark fringe, $m = 7$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\sin \theta = 5\left(\frac{\lambda}{d}\right) = 5\left(\frac{525 \times 10^{-9}\ \mathrm{m}}{0.0415 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m}}\right) = 0.0633$ and $\theta = 3.63^{\circ}$.

$$
y = R \tan \theta = (75.0\ \mathrm{cm}) \tan 3.63^{\circ} = 4.75\ \mathrm{cm}
$$

(b) $\sin \theta = \left(7 + \frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\lambda}{d}\right) = 0.0949$ and $\theta = 5.45^{\circ}$. $y = R\tan \theta = 7.16\ \mathrm{cm}$.

questions.questionText: 26.7. The values of $y_{m}$ are much smaller than the distance $R$ to the screen, so the approximate expression $y_{m} = R\frac{m\lambda}{d}$ is accurate.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\Delta y = y_{m11} - y_m = \frac{R\lambda}{d}$. $\lambda = \frac{d\Delta y}{R} = \frac{(0.460\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m})(2.82\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m})}{2.20\mathrm{m}} = 590\mathrm{nm}$

Reflect: This is a visible wavelength.

questions.questionText: 26.8. The value of $y_{20}$ is much smaller than $R$ and the approximate expression $y_{m} = R\frac{m\lambda}{d}$ is accurate. $y_{20} = 10.6 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $d = \frac{20R\lambda}{y_{20}} = \frac{(20)(1.20\mathrm{m})(502\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m})}{10.6\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m}} = 1.14\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m} = 1.14\mathrm{mm}$

questions.questionText: 26.9. $\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3.00 \times 10^{8} \, \mathrm{m/s}}{6.32 \times 10^{14} \, \mathrm{Hz}} = 4.75 \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{m}$. Bright fringes are located at $y_{m} = R \frac{m\lambda}{d}$, when $y_{m} = R$. Dark fringes are at $d \sin \theta = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$ and $y = R \tan \theta$. For the third bright fringe (not counting the central bright spot), $m = 3$. For the third dark fringe, $m = 2$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $d = \frac{m\lambda R}{y_m} = \frac{3(4.75\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{m})(0.850\mathrm{m})}{0.0311\mathrm{m}} = 3.89\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{m} = 0.0389\mathrm{mm}$

(b) $\sin \theta = \left(2 + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda}{d} = (2.5)\left(\frac{4.75\times 10^{-7}\mathrm{m}}{3.89\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{m}}\right) = 0.0305$ and $\theta = 1.75^{\circ}$. $y = R\tan \theta = (85.0\mathrm{cm})\tan 1.75^{\circ} = 2.60\mathrm{cm}$.

Reflect: The third dark fringe is closer to the center of the screen than the third bright fringe on one side of the central bright fringe.

questions.questionText: 26.10. Use the information given about the bright fringe to find the distance $d$ between the two slits. Then use Eq. (26.5) and $y = R \tan \theta$ to calculate $\lambda$ for which there is a first-order dark fringe at this same place on the screen.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $y_{1} = \frac{R\lambda_{1}}{d}$, so $d = \frac{R\lambda_{1}}{y_{1}} = \frac{(3.00\mathrm{m})(600\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m})}{4.84\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m}} = 3.72\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{m}$. (R is much greater than $d$, so Eq. [26.6] is valid.) The dark fringes are located by $d\sin \theta = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$ (where $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$). The first-order dark fringe is located by $\sin \theta = \lambda_2 / 2d$, where $\lambda_{2}$ is the wavelength we are seeking.

$$
y = R \tan \theta \approx R \sin \theta = \frac {\lambda_ {2} R}{2 d}
$$

We want $\lambda_{2}$ such that $y = y_{1}$. This gives $\frac{R\lambda_1}{d} = \frac{R\lambda_2}{2d}$ and $\lambda_{2} = 2\lambda_{1} = 1200\mathrm{nm}$

Reflect: For $\lambda = 600\mathrm{nm}$ the path difference from the two slits to this point on the screen is $600\mathrm{nm}$. For this same path difference (point on the screen) the path difference is $\lambda /2$ when $\lambda = 1200\mathrm{nm}$

questions.questionText: 26.11. $\frac{\lambda}{d} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{0.450 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}} = 1.11 \times 10^{-3}$. $\theta$ is very small for these dark lines and the approximate expression $y_{m} = R \frac{\left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda}{d}$ is accurate. Adjacent dark lines are separated by $\Delta y = y_{m+1} - y_{m} = \frac{R \lambda}{d}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $\Delta y = \frac{(0.750\mathrm{m})(500\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m})}{(0.450\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m})} = 8.33\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{m} = 0.833\mathrm{mm}$

questions.questionText: 26.12. Use Eq. (26.6) for small angles: $y_{m} = R \frac{m \lambda}{d}$. First order means $m = 1$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The distance between corresponding bright fringes is

$$
\Delta y = \frac {R m}{d} \Delta \lambda = \frac {(5 . 0 0 \mathrm {m}) (1)}{(0 . 3 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} \mathrm {m})} (6 6 0 - 4 7 0) \times (1 0 ^ {- 9} \mathrm {m}) = 3. 1 7 \mathrm {m m}.
$$

Reflect: The separation between these fringes for different wavelengths increases when the slit separation decreases.

questions.questionText: 26.13. Bright fringes are located at angles $\theta$ given by $d\sin \theta = m\lambda$. Dark fringes are located at angles $\theta$ given by $d\sin \theta = (m + \frac{1}{2})\lambda$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) For $\theta = 20^{\circ}$, we have $\frac{d\sin\theta}{\lambda} = \frac{(0.0116\mathrm{mm})\sin 20^{\circ}}{585\mathrm{nm}} = 6.8$ so there are fringes for $m = 1,2,3,4,5,6$, which makes six fringes in all.

(b) For $\theta = 20^{\circ}$, we have $\frac{d\sin\theta}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{(0.0116\mathrm{mm})\sin 20^{\circ}}{585\mathrm{nm}} = 6.3$ so there are dark fringes for $m = 1,2,3,4,5,6$, which makes six dark fringes in all.

Reflect: For part (b), you can calculate the number of fringes by counting one dark fringe between each bright fringe, and one dark fringe between the $m = 0$ and $m = 1$ bright fringes, which makes six total.

questions.questionText:
26.14. Strongly reflected means constructive interference so we will consider the interference between rays reflected from the two surfaces of the soap film. Take into account the phase difference due to the path difference of $2t$ and any phase difference due to phase changes upon reflection. As shown in the figure below there is a $180^{\circ}$ phase change (i.e., a half cycle) when the light is reflected from the outside surface of the bubble and no phase change when the light is reflected from the inside surface.

![img-161.jpeg](img-161.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The reflections produce a net $180^{\circ}$ phase difference and for there to be constructive interference the path difference $2t$ must correspond to a half-integer number of wavelengths to compensate for the $\lambda / 2$ shift due to the reflections. Hence the condition for constructive interference is $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)(\lambda_0 / n)$ (where $m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$). Here, $\lambda_0$ is the wavelength in air and $(\lambda_0 / n)$ is the wavelength in the bubble, where the path difference occurs. Thus we



have $\lambda_0 = \frac{2tn}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{2(290\mathrm{nm})(1.33)}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{771.4\mathrm{nm}}{m + \frac{1}{2}}$. For $m = 0$, $\lambda = 1543\mathrm{nm}$; for $m = 1$, $\lambda = 514\mathrm{nm}$; for $m = 2$, $\lambda = 308\mathrm{nm}$; etc. Only $514\mathrm{nm}$ is in the visible region; the color for this wavelength is green.

(b) $\lambda_0 = \frac{2tn}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{2(340\mathrm{nm})(1.33)}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{904.4\mathrm{nm}}{m + \frac{1}{2}}$

For $m = 0, \lambda = 1809 \, \mathrm{nm}$; for $m = 1, \lambda = 603 \, \mathrm{nm}$; for $m = 2, \lambda = 362 \, \mathrm{nm}$; Only $603 \, \mathrm{nm}$ is in the visible region; the color for this wavelength is orange.

**Reflect:** The dominant color of the reflected light depends on the thickness of the film. If the bubble has varying thickness at different points, these points will appear to be different colors when the light reflected from the bubble is viewed.

questions.questionText:
26.15. The interference is destructive since the film appears black. The waves reflected from the front surface of the film (waves in air reflecting from water) undergo a half-cycle reflection phase shift. The waves reflected from the back surface of the film (waves in water reflecting from air) do not undergo a reflection phase shift. Therefore, the condition for destructive interference is $2t = m\lambda$. The thinnest film is for $m = 1$. $\lambda = \frac{\lambda_0}{n}$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:** $t = \frac{m\lambda}{2} = \frac{\lambda_0}{2n} = \frac{480\mathrm{nm}}{2(1.33)} = 180\mathrm{nm}$

questions.questionText:
26.16. For both reflections, from the top and bottom surfaces of the film, the waves reflect from a material of greater $n$ and undergo a half-cycle reflection phase shift. A nonreflective coating requires destructive interference and the condition for destructive interference is $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$. $\lambda = \frac{\lambda_0}{n}$. The minimum thickness is for $m = 0$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:** $t = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{\lambda_0}{4n} = \frac{480\mathrm{nm}}{4(1.49)} = 80.5\mathrm{nm}$

questions.questionText:
26.17. Consider light reflected at the first and second interfaces. At the first interface, light in air $(n = 1.00)$ reflects from the film $(n_{\mathrm{film}} = 1.38)$ and there is a $180^{\circ}$ phase shift due to the reflection. At the second interface, light in the film $(n_{\mathrm{film}} = 1.38)$ reflects from glass $(n_{\mathrm{glass}} = 1.5)$ and there is also a $180^{\circ}$ phase shift. Both rays have a $180^{\circ}$ phase shift due to reflection, so there is no net phase difference produced by reflection. The path difference for these two rays is $2t$, where $t$ is the thickness of the film. The wavelength in the film is $\lambda = \frac{500\mathrm{nm}}{n_{\mathrm{film}}}$.

**
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:** (a) Since the reflections produce no net phase difference, for constructive interference of the reflected light, $2t = m\lambda$, or $t = m\left(\frac{500\mathrm{nm}}{2\times 1.38}\right) = (181\mathrm{nm})m$. The minimum thickness is for $m = 1$, which gives a thickness of $181\mathrm{nm}$.

(b) The next three thicknesses that give constructive interference are for $m = 2, 3, 4: 362 \, \mathrm{nm}, 543 \, \mathrm{nm}$, and $725 \, \mathrm{nm}$.

questions.questionText: 26.18. Consider light reflected at the front and rear surfaces of the film. At the front surface of the film, light in air $(n = 1.00)$ reflects from the film $(n = 2.62)$ and there is a $180^{\circ}$ phase shift due to the reflection. At the back surface of the film, light in the film $(n = 2.62)$ reflects from glass $(n = 1.62)$ and there is no phase shift due to reflection. Therefore, there is a net $180^{\circ}$ phase difference produced by the reflections. The path difference between these two rays is $2t$, where $t$ is the thickness of the film. The wavelength in the film is $\lambda = \frac{505\mathrm{nm}}{2.62}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Since the reflection produces a net $180^{\circ}$ phase difference, destructive interference of the reflected light occurs when $2t = m\lambda$. $t = m\left(\frac{505\mathrm{nm}}{2[2.62]}\right) = (96.4\mathrm{nm})m$. The minimum thickness is $96.4\mathrm{nm}$.

(b) The next three thicknesses are for $m = 2, 3$, and 4: $192\mathrm{nm}$, $289\mathrm{nm}$, and $386\mathrm{nm}$.

Reflect: The minimum thickness is for $t = \lambda / 2n$.

questions.questionText: 26.19. Since the lenses reflect $525\mathrm{nm}$ strongly, we must have constructive interference at that wavelength. The light reflected from the air-coating interface experiences a $180^{\circ}$ phase inversion (since $n_{\mathrm{air}} < n_{\mathrm{coating}}$) and the light reflected from the coating-glass interface also experiences a $180^{\circ}$ phase inversion (since $n_{\mathrm{coating}} < n_{\mathrm{glass}}$). Thus, the condition for constructive interference is $2t = m\frac{\lambda}{n}$, where $m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$, and $\frac{\lambda}{n}$ is the wavelength of the light in the coating (which is where the path-difference occurs).
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The minimum (nonzero) required thickness occurs when $m = 1$. This gives, $t = \frac{\lambda}{2n} = \frac{525\mathrm{nm}}{2(1.38)} = 190\mathrm{nm}$.

Reflect: Since the path difference occurs within the coating, and not within the glass, the exact value of the index of refraction of the glass is not needed (provided that $n_{\mathrm{coating}} < n_{\mathrm{glass}}$).

questions.questionText:
26.20. The fringes are produced by interference between light reflected from the top and from the bottom surfaces of the air wedge. The refractive index of glass is greater than that of air, so the waves reflected from the top surface of the air wedge have no reflection phase shift and the waves reflected from the bottom surface of the air wedge do have a half-cycle reflection phase shift. The condition for constructive interference (bright fringes) therefore is $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$. The geometry of the air wedge is sketched in the figure below.

![img-162.jpeg](img-162.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\tan \theta = \frac{0.0800\mathrm{mm}}{90.0\mathrm{mm}} = 8.89\times 10^{-4}$. $\tan \theta = \frac{t}{x}$ so $t = (8.89\times 10^{-4})x$. $t_m = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda}{2}$.

$$
x _ {m} = \left(m + \frac {1}{2}\right) \frac {\lambda}{2 (8 . 8 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4})} \quad \text{and}
$$

$$
x _ {m + 1} = \left(m + \frac {3}{2}\right) \frac {\lambda}{2 (8 . 8 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4})}
$$

The distance along the plate between adjacent fringes is

$$
\Delta x = x _ {m + 1} - x _ {m} = \frac {\lambda}{2 (8 . 8 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4})} = \frac {6 5 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 9} \mathrm {m}}{2 (8 . 8 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4})} = 3. 6 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} \mathrm {m} = 0. 3 6 9 \mathrm {m m}
$$

The number of fringes per cm is $\frac{1.00}{\Delta x} = \frac{1.00}{0.0369\mathrm{cm}} = 27.1$ fringes/cm.

Reflect: As $t \to 0$ the interference is destructive and there is a dark fringe at the line of contact between the two plates.

questions.questionText:
26.21. The fringes are produced by interference between light reflected from the top and bottom surfaces of the air wedge. The refractive index of glass is greater than that of air, so the waves reflected from the top surface of the air wedge have no reflection phase shift and the waves reflected from the bottom surface of the air wedge do have a half-cycle reflection phase shift. The condition for constructive interference (bright fringes) is therefore  $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$ . The geometry of the air wedge is sketched in the figure below. At a distance  $x$  from the point of contact of the two plates, the thickness of the air wedge is  $t$ .

![img-163.jpeg](img-163.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $\tan \theta = \frac{t}{x}$  so  $t = x\tan \theta$ .  $t_m = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ .  $x_m = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda}{2\tan\theta}$  and  $x_{m + 1} = \left(m + \frac{3}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda}{2\tan\theta}$ . The distance along the plate between adjacent fringes is  $\Delta x = x_{m + 1} - x_m = \frac{\lambda}{2\tan\theta}$ . 15.0 fringes/cm = 1.00/ $\Delta x$  and

$$
\Delta x = \frac {1 . 0 0}{1 5 . 0 \text {f r i n g e s / c m}} = 0. 0 6 6 7 \mathrm {c m}
$$

$\tan \theta = \frac{\lambda}{2\Delta x} = \frac{546\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m}}{2(0.0667\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m})} = 4.09\times 10^{-4}$ . The angle of the wedge is  $4.09\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{rad} = 0.0234^{\circ}$ .

questions.questionText: 26.22. Since the film reflects  $575\mathrm{nm}$  strongly, we must have constructive interference at that wavelength. The light reflected from the air-benzene interface experiences a  $180^{\circ}$  phase inversion (since  $n_{\mathrm{air}} < n_{\mathrm{benzene}}$ ), but the light reflected from the benzene-water interface does not experience a phase inversion (since  $n_{\mathrm{benzene}} > n_{\mathrm{water}}$ ). Thus, the condition for constructive interference is  $2t = m\frac{\lambda}{2n}$ , where  $m = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$ , and  $\frac{\lambda}{n}$  is the wavelength of the light in the benzene (which is where the path-difference occurs).
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The minimum required thickness occurs when  $m = 1$ . This gives,  $t = \frac{\lambda}{4n} = \frac{575\mathrm{nm}}{4(1.50)} = 95.8\mathrm{nm}$ .

Reflect: Since the path difference occurs within the benzene, and not within the water, the exact value of the index of refraction of water is not needed (provided that  $n_{\mathrm{benzene}} > n_{\mathrm{water}}$ ).

questions.questionText: 26.23. Both reflections occur for waves in the plastic substrate reflecting from the reflective coating, so they both have the same phase shift upon reflection and the condition for destructive interference (cancellation) is  $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$ , where  $t$  is the depth of the pit.  $\lambda = \frac{\lambda_0}{n}$ . The minimum pit depth is for  $m = 0$ .
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve:  $2t = \frac{\lambda}{2}$ .  $t = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{\lambda_0}{4n} = \frac{790\mathrm{nm}}{4(1.8)} = 110\mathrm{nm} = 0.11\mu \mathrm{m}$ .

questions.questionText: 26.24. The condition for a dark fringe is  $\sin \theta = \frac{m\lambda}{a}$ ,  $m = \pm 1$ ,  $\pm 2, \ldots$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $\sin \theta = m\left(\frac{632.8\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m}}{0.00375\times 10^{-3}\mathrm{m}}\right) = m(0.1687).m = \pm 1:\theta = \pm 9.71^{\circ}.m = \pm 2:\theta = \pm 19.7^{\circ}.m = \pm 3:\theta = \pm 30.4^{\circ}.$

$m = \pm 4$  ..  $\theta = \pm 42.4^{\circ}$ $m = \pm 5$  ..  $\theta = \pm 57.5^{\circ}$



**Reflect:** There are a finite number of dark fringes because $\frac{m\lambda}{a} = \sin \theta$ cannot be larger than 1. This establishes a maximum value for $m$.

questions.questionText: 26.25. $\lambda = a$ so the approximate expression $y_{m} = R\frac{m\lambda}{a}$ is accurate.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $y_{1} = \frac{\lambda R}{a} = \frac{(546 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m})(1.75 \mathrm{~m})}{0.437 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} = 2.19 \mathrm{~mm}$

questions.questionText: 26.26. $\frac{y_m}{R}$ is very small, so the approximate expression $y_{m} = R\frac{m\lambda}{a}$ is accurate. $y_{1}$ is the distance from the center of the screen to the first dark fringe on either side of the central maximum. $2y_{1} = 4.50 \mathrm{~mm}$ and $y_{1} = 2.25 \mathrm{~mm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $y_{1} = \frac{\lambda R}{a}$. $a = \frac{\lambda R}{y_{1}} = \frac{(600 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m})(3.00 \mathrm{~m})}{2.25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}} = 8.00 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m} = 0.800 \mathrm{~mm}$

Reflect: The central bright fringe is twice as wide as the other bright fringes.

questions.questionText: 26.27. $\frac{y_m}{R}$ is very small, so the approximate expression $y_{m} = R\frac{m\lambda}{a}$ is accurate. $y_{1} = 1.35 \mathrm{~mm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $\lambda = \frac{y_1a}{R} = \frac{(1.35 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m})(0.750 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m})}{2.00 \mathrm{~m}} = 506 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.questionText:
26.28. Calculate the angular positions of the minima and use $y = x \tan \theta$ to calculate the distance on the screen between them. The central bright fringe is shown in Figure (a) below and the first bright fringe on one side of the central bright fringe is shown in Figure (b) below.

![img-164.jpeg](img-164.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The first minimum is located by $\sin \theta_{1} = \frac{\lambda}{a} = \frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{0.350 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}} = 1.809 \times 10^{-3}$. Solving we obtain $\theta_{1} = 1.809 \times 10^{-3}$ rad.

We can now find the distance between the center of the central fringe and the first minimum: $y_{1} = x \tan \theta_{1} = (3.00 \, \mathrm{m}) \tan (1.809 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{rad}) = 5.427 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}$. The width of the central bright fringe is $w = 2y_{1} = 2(5.427 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}) = 1.09 \times 10^{-2} \, \mathrm{m} = 10.9 \, \mathrm{mm}$.



![img-165.jpeg](img-165.jpeg)

(b) The width of the first bright fringe adjacent to the central fringe is $w = y_{2} - y_{1}$, where $y_{1} = 5.427 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}$ from part (a). To find $y_{2}$ we note that $\sin \theta_{2} = \frac{2\lambda}{a} = 3.618 \times 10^{-3}$ so that $\theta_{2} = 3.618 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{rad}$. Thus, $y_{2} = x \tan \theta_{2} = 1.085 \times 10^{-2} \, \mathrm{m}$ and the width of the bright fringe adjacent to the central one is $w = y_{2} - y_{1} = 1.085 \times 10^{-2} \, \mathrm{m} - 5.427 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m} = 5.4 \, \mathrm{mm}$.

Reflect: The central bright fringe is twice as wide as the other bright fringes.

questions.questionText: 26.29. $\lambda / a$ is very small, so the approximate expression $y_{m} = R \frac{m \lambda}{a}$ is accurate. The distance between the two dark fringes on either side of the central maximum is $2y_{1}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $y_{1} = \frac{\lambda R}{a} = \frac{(633 \times 10^{-9} \, \mathrm{m})(3.50 \, \mathrm{m})}{0.750 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}} = 2.95 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m} = 2.95 \, \mathrm{mm}$. $2y_{1} = 5.90 \, \mathrm{mm}$.

questions.questionText: 26.30. $\upsilon = f\lambda$ gives $\lambda$. The person hears no sound at angles corresponding to diffraction minima. The diffraction minima are located by $\sin \theta = m\lambda / a$ (where $m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$).
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\lambda = \upsilon / f = (344 \, \mathrm{m/s}) / (1250 \, \mathrm{Hz}) = 0.2752 \, \mathrm{m}$; $a = 1.00 \, \mathrm{m}$. $m = \pm 1$, $\theta = \pm 16.0^\circ$; $m = \pm 2$, $\theta = \pm 33.4^\circ$; $m = \pm 3$, $\theta = \pm 55.6^\circ$; no solution for larger $m$.

Reflect: $\lambda / a = 0.28$ so for the large wavelength sound waves diffraction by the doorway is a large effect. Diffraction would not be easily observable for visible light because its wavelength is much smaller and $\lambda / a \gg 1$.

questions.questionText: 26.31. The dark fringes are located at angles $\theta$ that satisfy $\sin \theta = \frac{m\lambda}{a}, m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. The largest value of $|\sin \theta|$ is 1.00.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Solve for $m$ that corresponds to $\sin \theta = 1$: $m = \frac{a}{\lambda} = \frac{0.0666 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}}{585 \times 10^{-9} \, \mathrm{m}} = 113.8$. The largest value $m$ can have is 113. $m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots, \pm 113$ gives 226 dark fringes.

(b) For $m = \pm 113$, $\sin \theta = \pm 113\left(\frac{585 \times 10^{-9} \, \mathrm{m}}{0.0666 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}}\right) = \pm 0.9926$ and $\theta = \pm 83.0^\circ$.

questions.questionText: 26.32. The wavelength of light in the liquid is $\lambda = \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{n}$. The dark fringes are located at $\sin \theta = \frac{m\lambda}{a}$, $m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. The first dark fringes on either side of the central bright fringe correspond to $m = +1$ and $m = -1$. If a feature is located by angle $\theta$, its distance from the center of the central maximum is $y = R\tan \theta$. In this problem $\theta$ is not small and Eq. 26.10 cannot be used.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Each dark fringe is a distance $y = 11.2 \, \text{cm}$ from the center of the screen. $\tan \theta = \frac{y}{R} = \frac{11.2 \, \text{cm}}{30.0 \, \text{cm}}$ and $\theta = 20.5^\circ$. $\lambda = \frac{a \sin \theta}{1} = \frac{(0.00125 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}) \sin 20.5^\circ}{1} = 438 \, \text{nm}$. $\lambda_{\text{air}} = n\lambda = (1.45)(438 \, \text{nm}) = 635 \, \text{nm}$.

Reflect: Since the path difference occurs in the liquid it must be compared to the wavelength in the liquid.

questions.questionText: 26.33. For a grating the bright fringes are located by $d\sin \theta = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. The spots at $\pm 15.4^{\circ}$ correspond to $m = \pm 1$. 5510 lines/cm means $d = \frac{1}{5510} \, \text{cm} = 1.81 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm} = 1.86 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$ is the spacing of adjacent lines of the grating.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $\lambda = \frac{d\sin\theta}{m} = \frac{(1.81\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{m})\sin 15.4^{\circ}}{1} = 481\mathrm{nm}$

(b) The next angle is for $m = \pm 2$. $\sin \theta = m\frac{\lambda}{d} = \pm 2\left(\frac{481\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m}}{1.81\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{m}}\right) = \pm 0.531$ and $\theta = \pm 32.1^{\circ}$.

questions.questionText: 26.34. The bright fringes are located at angles $\theta$ given by $\sin \theta = \frac{m\lambda}{d}$. The separation $d$ between adjacent slits in the grating is $d = \frac{1.00 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}}{400.0} = 2.50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $\sin \theta = m\left(\frac{600.0 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}}{2.50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}}\right) = m(0.240)$. First order $(m = 1)$: $\theta = 13.9^{\circ}$. Second order $(m = 2)$: $\theta = 28.7^{\circ}$. Third order $(m = 3)$: $\theta = 46.1^{\circ}$.

questions.questionText: 26.35. The line density in lines/cm is $1/d$, with $d$ in cm. The bright spots are located by $d\sin \theta = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $d = \frac{m\lambda}{\sin\theta} = \frac{(1)(632.8\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m})}{\sin 17.8^{\circ}} = 2.07\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{m} = 2.07\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{cm}$. $\frac{1}{d} = 4830$ lines/cm.

(b) $\sin \theta = \frac{m\lambda}{d} = m\left(\frac{632.8\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m}}{2.07\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{m}}\right) = m(0.3057)$. For $m = \pm 2$, $\theta = \pm 37.7^{\circ}$. For $m = \pm 3$, $\theta = \pm 66.5^{\circ}$.

questions.questionText: 26.36. The bright spots are located by $d\sin \theta = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. The second pair corresponds to $m = \pm 2$. $d = \frac{1}{5580 \text{ lines/cm}} = 1.79 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 1.79 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $y = R \tan \theta$. $\tan \theta = \frac{y}{R} = \frac{26.3 \, \text{cm}}{42.5 \, \text{cm}} = 0.6188$ and $\theta = 31.75^\circ$.

$$
\lambda = \frac {d \sin \theta}{m} = \frac {(1.79 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m}) (\sin 31.75^{\circ})}{2} = 471 \, \mathrm{nm}
$$

(b) The next bright spots are for $m = \pm 3$. $\sin \theta = \frac{m\lambda}{d} = \frac{3(471\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m})}{1.79\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{m}}$ and $\theta = 52.13^{\circ}$.

$$
y = R \tan \theta = (42.5 \, \mathrm{cm}) (\tan 52.13^{\circ}) = 54.7 \, \mathrm{cm}
$$



**Reflect:** $\frac{\lambda}{d} = \frac{471 \times 10^{-9} \, \mathrm{m}}{1.79 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m}} = 0.263$ and is not small. The approximation $\sin \theta \approx \tan \theta$ is not accurate.

**26.37. Set Up:** The maxima are located by $d\sin \theta = m\lambda$, where the order corresponds to the values of $m$.

**Solve:** First order: $d\sin \theta_{1} = \lambda$. Fourth order: $d\sin \theta_{4} = 4\lambda$, $\frac{d\sin\theta_4}{d\sin\theta_1} = \frac{4\lambda}{\lambda}$, $\sin \theta_4 = 4\sin \theta_1 = 4\sin 8.94^\circ$ and $\theta_4 = 38.4^\circ$.

**Reflect:** We did not have to solve for $d$.

**\*26.38. Set Up:** The maxima are located by $d\sin \theta = m\lambda$, where $d = 1.60 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m}$ for a CD and $d = 0.740 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m}$ for a DVD.

**Solve:** (a) For a CD we have: $\theta = \arcsin \left(\frac{m\lambda}{d}\right) = \arcsin \left(\frac{m(6.328 \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{m})}{1.60 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m}}\right) = \arcsin (0.396 \, \mathrm{m})$. For $m = 1$ we have $\theta_{1} = 23.3^\circ$. For $m = 2$ we have $\theta_{2} = 52.3^\circ$. There are no other maxima.

(b) For a DVD we have: $\theta = \arcsin \left(\frac{m\lambda}{d}\right) = \arcsin \left(\frac{m[6.328 \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{m}]}{0.740 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m}}\right) = \arcsin (0.855 \, \mathrm{m})$. For $m = 1$ we have $\theta_{1} = 58.8^\circ$. There are no other maxima.

**Reflect:** The reflective surface produces the same interference pattern as a grating with slit separation $d$.

**26.39. Set Up:** Maxima are located by $d\sin \theta = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. The largest value of $|\sin \theta|$ is 1. $d = \frac{1}{485 \text{ lines/mm}} = 2.06 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = 2.06 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$.

**Solve:** (a) Set $\sin \theta = 1.00$ and solve for $m$: $m = \frac{d\sin\theta}{\lambda} = \frac{(2.06 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m})(1.00)}{631 \times 10^{-9} \, \mathrm{m}} = 3.3$. The largest $|m|$ is 3, so there are seven bright spots ($m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3$).

(b) $m = 3$. $\sin \theta = \frac{m\lambda}{d} = \frac{3(631 \times 10^{-9} \, \mathrm{m})}{2.06 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m}}$ and $\theta = 66.8^\circ$.

**26.40. Set Up:** The bright spots are located by $d\sin \theta = m\lambda$. Third order means $m = 3$ and second order means $m = 2$.

**Solve:** $\frac{m\lambda}{\sin\theta} = d = \text{constant}$, so $\frac{m_{\mathrm{r}}\lambda_{\mathrm{r}}}{\sin\theta_{\mathrm{r}}} = \frac{m_{\mathrm{v}}\lambda_{\mathrm{v}}}{\sin\theta_{\mathrm{v}}}$.

$$
\sin \theta_ {\mathrm {v}} = \sin \theta_ {\mathrm {r}} \left(\frac {m _ {\mathrm {v}}}{m _ {\mathrm {r}}}\right) \left(\frac {\lambda_ {\mathrm {v}}}{\lambda_ {\mathrm {r}}}\right) = (\sin 65.0 ^ {\circ}) \left(\frac {2}{3}\right) \left(\frac {400 \, \mathrm{nm}}{700 \, \mathrm{nm}}\right) = 0.345 \text{ and } \theta_ {\mathrm {v}} = 20.2 ^ {\circ}.
$$

**Reflect:** The third-order line for a particular $\lambda$ occurs at a larger angle than the second-order line. In a given order, the line for violet light (400 nm) occurs at a smaller angle than the line for red light (700 nm).

**\*26.41. Set Up:** Rayleigh’s criterion says $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22\frac{\lambda}{D}$. $D = 7.20 \, \mathrm{cm}$. $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = \frac{y}{s}$, where $s$ is the distance of the object from the lens and $y = 4.00 \, \mathrm{mm}$.

**Solve:** $\frac{y}{s} = 1.22\frac{\lambda}{D}$. $s = \frac{yD}{1.22\lambda} = \frac{(4.00 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m})(7.20 \times 10^{-2} \, \mathrm{m})}{1.22(550 \times 10^{-9} \, \mathrm{m})} = 429 \, \mathrm{m}$.

**Reflect:** The focal length of the lens does not enter into the calculation. In practice, it is difficult to achieve resolution that is at the diffraction limit.

questions.questionText: 26.42. Rayleigh’s criterion says $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22\frac{\lambda}{D}$, where $D = 7.0\,\mathrm{cm}$ is the diameter of the aperture (i.e., the mirror in this case). For objects with small angular separation, $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = \frac{y}{s}$, where $s$ is the distance of the object from the mirror and $y = 2.50\,\mathrm{m}$ is the distance between the objects.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: Combining these two equations and solving for $s$ gives $s = \frac{yD}{1.22\lambda} = \frac{(2.50\,\mathrm{m})(0.070\,\mathrm{m})}{1.22(600\times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{m})} = 2.4\times 10^{5}\,\mathrm{m}$.

questions.questionText: 26.43. Apply $\sin \theta = 1.22\frac{\lambda}{D}$, where $\theta = \frac{W}{h}$, $W = 28\,\mathrm{km}$, and $h = 1200\,\mathrm{km}$. Since $\theta$ is small we have $\sin \theta = \theta$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $D = \frac{1.22\lambda}{\sin\theta} = 1.22\lambda \frac{h}{W} = 1.22(0.036\,\mathrm{m}) \frac{1.2 \times 10^{6}\,\mathrm{m}}{2.8 \times 10^{4}\,\mathrm{m}} = 1.88\,\mathrm{m}$

Reflect: $D$ must be significantly larger than the wavelength, so a much larger diameter is needed for microwaves than for visible wavelengths.

questions.questionText: 26.44. Rayleigh’s criterion says $\sin \theta = 1.22\frac{\lambda}{D}$. The best resolution is 0.3 arcseconds, which is about $(8.33\times 10^{-5})^{\circ}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) $D = \frac{1.22\lambda}{\sin\theta} = \frac{1.22(5.5\times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{m})}{\sin(8.33\times 10^{-5})} = 0.46\,\mathrm{m}$

Reflect: (b) The Keck telescopes are able to gather more light than the Hale telescope; so they can detect fainter objects. However, their larger size does not allow them to have greater resolution—atmospheric conditions limit the resolution.

questions.questionText: 26.45. $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22\frac{\lambda}{D}$. The angle is given by the ratio of the separation between the point-like objects and the distance to the observer: $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = \frac{y}{s}$. $D = 2.00\,\mathrm{mm}$. $s = 25\,\mathrm{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Combining these two equations and solving for the separation $y$ gives $y = 1.22\frac{s\lambda}{D}$

$$
= 1.22 \frac{(25 \times 10^{-2}\,\mathrm{m})(550 \times 10^{-9}\,\mathrm{m})}{2.00 \times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{m}} = 8.4 \times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{m} = 84\,\mu\mathrm{m}
$$

Reflect: Diffraction does not play a significant role.

questions.questionText: 26.46. $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22\frac{\lambda}{D}$. The angular size of the object is its height divided by its distance from the eye.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The angular size of the object is

$$
\theta = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}\,\mathrm{m}}{25 \times 10^{-2}\,\mathrm{m}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{rad}
$$

$$
\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D} = 1.22 \left(\frac{550 \times 10^{-9}\,\mathrm{m}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m}}\right) = 3.4 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{rad}
$$

$\theta < \theta_{\mathrm{res}}$ so the object cannot be resolved.

(b) $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = \frac{y}{s}$ and $y = s\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = (25\,\mathrm{cm})(3.4\times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{rad}) = 8.5\times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{cm} = 85\,\mu\mathrm{m}$.

(c) $\theta = \theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 3.4 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{rad} = 0.019^{\circ} = 1.1\,\mathrm{min}$. This is very close to the experimental value of $1\,\mathrm{min}$.



(d) Diffraction is more important.

questions.questionText: 26.47. Take the width of a typical license plate to be about $30~\mathrm{cm}$. $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22\frac{\lambda}{D}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The angular size of the object is $\theta = \frac{0.30\mathrm{m}}{400\times 10^3\mathrm{m}} = 7.5\times 10^{-7}$ rad. Setting $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = \theta$ gives

$$
D = \frac {1.22 \lambda}{\theta} = \frac {(1.22) (500 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m})}{7.5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{rad}} = 81 \mathrm{cm}
$$

Reflect: A mirror of this diameter (less than one meter) is feasible.

questions.questionText: 26.48. $\upsilon = f\lambda$. Constructive interference occurs when the path difference $r_2 - r_1$ from the two sources is $r_2 - r_1 = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$. Destructive interference occurs when the path difference $r_2 - r_1$ is $r_2 - r_1 = m\lambda$, $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) The path difference from the two speakers is a half-integer number of wavelengths and the interference is destructive.

(b) The path difference changes by $\frac{\lambda}{2}$, so $\frac{\lambda}{2} = 0.398\mathrm{m}$ and $\lambda = 0.796\mathrm{m}$. $f = \frac{\upsilon}{\lambda} = \frac{340\mathrm{m/s}}{0.796\mathrm{m}} = 427\mathrm{Hz}$.

(c) The speaker must be moved a distance $\lambda = 0.796\mathrm{m}$, so the path difference will change by $\lambda$.

questions.questionText: 26.49. The interference minima are located by $d\sin \theta = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$. For a liquid with refractive index $n$, $\lambda_{\mathrm{liq}} = \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{n}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\frac{\sin\theta}{\lambda} = \frac{\left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)}{d} = \text{constant}$, so $\frac{\sin\theta_{\mathrm{air}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}} = \frac{\sin\theta_{\mathrm{liq}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{liq}}}$. $\frac{\sin\theta_{\mathrm{air}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}} = \frac{\sin\theta_{\mathrm{liq}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} / n}$ and $n = \frac{\sin\theta_{\mathrm{air}}}{\sin\theta_{\mathrm{liq}}} = \frac{\sin 35.20^{\circ}}{\sin 19.46^{\circ}} = 1.730$.

Reflect: In the liquid the wavelength is shorter and $\sin \theta = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda}{d}$ gives a smaller $\theta$ than in air, for the same $m$.

questions.questionText: 26.50. Consider the interference of the rays reflected from each side of the film. At the front of the film light in air reflects off the film ($n = 1.432$) and there is a $180^{\circ}$ phase shift. At the back of the film light in the film ($n = 1.432$) reflects off the glass ($n = 1.62$) and there is a $180^{\circ}$ phase shift. Therefore, the reflections introduce no net phase shift. The path difference $2t$, where $t$ is the thickness of the film. The wavelength in the film is $\lambda = \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{n}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Since there is no net phase difference produced by the reflections, the condition for destructive interference is $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$. $t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ and the minimum thickness is $t = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{4} = \frac{550\mathrm{nm}}{4\times 1.432} = 96.0\mathrm{nm}$.

(b) For destructive interference, $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{n}$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} = \frac{2tn}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{275\mathrm{nm}}{m + \frac{1}{2}}$. $m = 0$: $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} = 550\mathrm{nm}$. $m = 1$: $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} = 183\mathrm{nm}$. All other $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}$ values are shorter. The only visible wavelength in air for which there is destructive interference is $550\mathrm{nm}$. For constructive interference, $2t = m\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{n}$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} = \frac{2tn}{m} = \frac{275\mathrm{nm}}{m}$. For $m = 1$, $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} = 275\mathrm{nm}$ and all other $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}$ values are shorter. There are no visible wavelengths in air for which there is constructive interference.

questions.questionText: 26.51. Consider reflection from either side of the film.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) At the front of the film, light in air ($n = 1.00$) reflects off the film ($n = 1.45$) and there is a $180^{\circ}$ phase shift. At the back of the film, light in the film ($n = 1.45$) reflects off the cornea ($n = 1.38$) and there is no phase shift. The reflections produce a net $180^{\circ}$ phase difference and the condition for constructive interference is $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$, where $\lambda = \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{n}$. $t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{2n}$. Minimum thickness is for $m = 0$, and is given by $t = \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{4n} = \frac{600 \mathrm{~nm}}{4(1.45)} = 103 \mathrm{~nm}$.

(b) $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} = \frac{2nt}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{2(1.45)(103\mathrm{nm})}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{299\mathrm{nm}}{m + \frac{1}{2}}$. For $m = 0$, $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} = 598\mathrm{nm}$. For $m = 1$, $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} = 199\mathrm{nm}$ and all other values are smaller. No other visible wavelengths are reinforced. The condition for destructive interference is $2t = m\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{air}}}{n}$. $\lambda = \frac{2m}{m} = \frac{299\mathrm{nm}}{m}$. For $m = 1$, $\lambda_{\mathrm{air}} = 299\mathrm{nm}$ and all other values are shorter. There are no visible wavelengths for which there is destructive interference.

(c) Now both rays have a $180^{\circ}$ phase change on reflection and the reflections don't introduce any net phase shift. The expression for constructive interference in parts (a) and (b) now gives destructive interference and the expression in (a) and (b) for destructive interference now gives constructive interference. The only visible wavelength for which there will be destructive interference is $600\mathrm{nm}$ and there are no visible wavelengths for which there will be constructive interference.

Reflect: Changing the net phase shift due to the reflections can convert the interference for a particular thickness from constructive to destructive, and vice versa.

questions.questionText: 26.52. Rayleigh’s criterion limits the angular resolution according to $\sin \theta \approx \theta = 1.22\lambda /D$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a) Using Rayleigh’s criterion we have $\sin \theta \approx \theta = 1.22\lambda /D = (1.22)(550\mathrm{nm}) / (135 / 4\mathrm{mm}) = 1.99\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{rad}$. On the bear this angle subtends a distance $x$. $\theta = x / R$ and $x = R\theta = (11.5\mathrm{m})(1.99\times 10^{-5}\mathrm{rad}) = 2.29\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{m} = 0.23\mathrm{mm}$.

(b) At $f / 22$, $D$ is 4/22 times as large as at $f / 4$. Since $\theta$ is proportional to $1 / D$, and $x$ is proportional to $\theta$, $x$ is $1 / (4 / 22) = 22 / 4$ times as large as it was at $f / 4$. Thus, $x = (0.229\mathrm{mm})(22 / 4) = 1.3\mathrm{mm}$.

Reflect: A wide-angle lens, such as one having a focal length of $28\mathrm{mm}$, would have a much smaller opening at $f / 22$ and thus would have even less resolving ability.

questions.questionText: 26.53. The dark fringes are located by $\sin \theta = m\frac{\lambda}{a}$. The first dark fringes are for $m = \pm 1$. $y = R\tan \theta$ is the distance from the center of the screen. From the center to one minimum is $2.61\mathrm{cm}$.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: $\tan \theta = \frac{y}{R} = \frac{2.61\mathrm{cm}}{125\mathrm{cm}} = 0.02088$ and $\theta = 1.20^{\circ}$. $a = \frac{\lambda}{\sin\theta} = \frac{632.8\times 10^{-9}\mathrm{m}}{\sin 1.20^{\circ}} = 30.2\mu \mathrm{m}$.

questions.questionText: 26.54. For the waves reflected at the top surface of the oil film there is a half-cycle reflection phase shift. For the waves reflected at the bottom surface of the oil film there is no reflection phase shift. The condition for constructive interference is $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$. The condition for destructive interference is $2t = m\lambda$. Wavelengths that are predominant in the transmitted light are those for which there is destructive interference in the reflected light. The range of visible wavelengths is approximately $400\mathrm{nm}$ to $700\mathrm{nm}$. In the oil film, $\lambda = \frac{\lambda_0}{n}$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda_0}{n}.$ $\lambda_0 = \frac{2tn}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{2(380\mathrm{nm})(1.45)}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{1102\mathrm{nm}}{m + \frac{1}{2}}.$ $m = 0:\quad \lambda_0 = 2200\mathrm{nm}.$

$m = 1:\lambda_0 = 735\mathrm{nm}.$ $m = 2:\lambda_0 = 441\mathrm{nm}.$ $m = 3:\lambda_0 = 315\mathrm{nm}.$  The visible wavelength for which there is constructive interference in the reflected light is  $441~\mathrm{nm}$

(b)  $2t = m\lambda = m\frac{\lambda_0}{n}$ .  $\lambda_0 = \frac{2tn}{m} = \frac{1102\mathrm{nm}}{m}$ .  $m = 1$ :  $\lambda_0 = 1102\mathrm{nm}$ .  $m = 2$ :  $\lambda_0 = 551\mathrm{nm}$ .  $m = 3$ :  $\lambda_0 = 367\mathrm{nm}$ . The visible wavelength for which there is destructive interference in the reflected light is  $551\mathrm{nm}$ . This is the visible wavelength predominant in the transmitted light.

Reflect: At a particular wavelength the sum of the intensities of the reflected and transmitted light equals the intensity of the incident light.

questions.questionText: 26.55. The waves reflected from the first surface of the slide undergo a half-cycle phase shift upon reflection and the waves reflected at the second surface of the slide have zero phase shift upon reflection. The condition for constructive interference is thus  $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda$  and the condition for destructive interference is  $2t = m\lambda$ .  $\lambda = \frac{\lambda_0}{n}$ .  $f = \frac{c}{\lambda_0}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: (a)  $2t = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\frac{\lambda_0}{n}.$ $\lambda_0 = \frac{2tn}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{2(0.485\times 10^{-6}\mathrm{m})(1.53)}{m + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{1484\mathrm{nm}}{m + \frac{1}{2}}.$ $m = 0$

$\lambda_0 = 2968 \mathrm{~nm}$ . The lowest frequency to give an intensified reflection is thus  $f = \frac{3.00 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m/s}}{2968 \mathrm{~nm}} = 1.01 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$ .

(b)  $2t = m\lambda = m\frac{\lambda_0}{n}$ .  $\lambda_0 = \frac{2tn}{m} = \frac{1484\mathrm{nm}}{m}$ .  $m = 1$ :  $\lambda_0 = 1484\mathrm{nm}$ . The lowest frequency to give a cancelled reflection is thus  $f = \frac{3.00\times 10^{8}\mathrm{m / s}}{1484\mathrm{nm}} = 2.02\times 10^{14}\mathrm{Hz}$ .

Reflect: Recall that the extra half-wavelength phase shift added at the first reflection means that we must add a half wavelength to the difference in path length. This is why the extra term of  $\frac{1}{2}$  appears in the equation for constructive interference.

questions.questionText: 26.56. The diameter of Jupiter is  $1.38 \times 10^{8} \, \mathrm{m}$ .  $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D}$ . The angular size of an object is  $\theta = \frac{y}{s}$ . 1 light-year = 9.461 × 10 $^{15}$  m.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve:  $\theta = \frac{y}{s} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m}}{4.3(9.461 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~m})} = 3.39 \times 10^{-9}$  rad. Setting  $\theta_{\mathrm{res}} = \theta$  gives

$$
D = \frac {1.22\lambda}{\theta} = 1.22 \left(\frac {550 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{3.39 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~rad}}\right) = 198 \mathrm{~m}
$$

questions.questionText: 26.57. The bright fringes are located by $d \sin \theta = m\lambda$. First-order means $m = 1$. The line density is $1/d$. The largest $\lambda$ appears at the largest $\theta$. If the $\lambda_1 = 400 \, \mathrm{nm}$ line is at $\theta$, then the $\lambda_2 = 700 \, \mathrm{nm}$ line is to be at $\theta + 30.0^\circ$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: $\sin \theta = \frac{\lambda_1}{d}$. $\sin (\theta + 30.0^\circ) = \frac{\lambda_2}{d}$. $\sin (\theta + 30.0^\circ) = \sin \theta \cos 30.0^\circ + \cos \theta \sin 30.0^\circ$, so $(\sin \theta)(0.866) + (\cos \theta)(0.500) = \frac{\lambda_2}{d}$. $\cos \theta = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{\lambda_1^2}{d^2}}$, so $0.866 \frac{\lambda_1}{d} + 0.500 \sqrt{1 - \frac{\lambda_1^2}{d^2}} = \frac{\lambda_2}{d}$. This gives $700 \, \mathrm{nm} - (0.866)(400 \, \mathrm{nm}) = 0.500 \sqrt{d^2 - (400 \, \mathrm{nm})^2}$ and $d = 812 \, \mathrm{nm} = 8.12 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{mm}$. The line density is $\frac{1}{d} = 1230$ lines/mm.

Reflect: $2\frac{\lambda_2}{d} > 1$ and the second-order pattern does not include the entire visible spectrum. The first order is the only order that displays the entire visible spectrum.

questions.questionText: 26.58. Interference occurs due to the path difference of light in the thin film. Originally the path difference was an odd number of half-wavelengths for cancellation to occur. If the path difference decreases by one half wavelength, it will be a multiple of the wavelength, so constructive interference will occur.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: Calling $\Delta T$ the thickness that must be removed, we have the path difference $= 2\Delta T = \frac{1}{2} \lambda / \mathrm{n}$ and $\Delta T = \lambda / 4\mathrm{n} = (525\mathrm{nm}) / (4 \times 1.40) = 93.75\mathrm{nm}$.
