At $4.20\mathrm{nm}$/year, we have $(4.20\mathrm{nm}/\mathrm{year})t = 93.75\mathrm{nm}$ and $t = 22.3$ years.

Reflect: If you were giving a warranty on this film, you certainly could not give it a “lifetime guarantee”!

questions.questionText: 26.59. The maxima are given by $d \sin \theta = m\lambda$. On the screen, the distance $y$ from the central maximum to the other maxima is $y = R \tan \theta$. In this experiment, $y$ is on the order of tens of mm, whereas $R = 2$ m, so the small-angle approximation may be used. This gives $d\theta = m\lambda$ and $y = R\theta$. Combining these equations gives $y = \frac{Rm\lambda}{d}$. Thus, a plot of the distance to the $m = 3$ bright fringe as a function of the inverse slit separation should give a straight line that goes through the origin and has a slope $\ell = Rm\lambda$.
questions.expectedAnswer:
Solve: The data are plotted in the graph below.

![img-166.jpeg](img-166.jpeg)



The best-fit line has a slope of  $\ell = 2.65 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2$ , so the wavelength is  $\lambda = \frac{\ell}{Rm} = \frac{2.65 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2}{(2 \mathrm{~m})(3)} = 442 \mathrm{~nm}$ .

Reflect: Note that the best-fit line goes through the origin, as expected.

# Solutions to Passage Problems

questions.questionText: 26.60. If two speakers are connected to a single oscillator it is likely that some students will hear constructive interference and others will hear destructive interference.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: Changing either the volume or the phase relationship of the speakers does not alter the fact that some students will hear constructive interference while others will hear destructive interference. However, if one speaker is unplugged the interference effects will disappear. Any student who was originally located at a point of destructive interference will now be able to hear a tone, and any student who was originally at a point of constructive interference will hear the intensity of the sound diminish. The correct answer is D.

questions.questionText: 26.61. If two speakers are connected to a single oscillator it is likely that some students will hear constructive interference and others will hear destructive interference.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: Both speakers must still be connected since some students are still unable to hear a tone. Furthermore, if the volume were simply increased or decreased the points of destructive interference would not change. However, if the phase relationship between the speakers is changed (e.g., the polarity of the wires on one speaker is switched so that that speaker vibrates  $180^{\circ}$  out of phase with the other speaker) then points of destructive and constructive interference will be switched. The correct answer is C.

questions.questionText: 26.62. For constructive interference the path difference must be a multiple of the wavelength and for destructive interference the path difference must be an odd multiple of a half-wavelength (this is reversed if the speakers are driven out of phase). The relation between frequency and wavelength is given by  $f\lambda = v$ , where we are given the speed of sound to be  $v = 340 \, \mathrm{m/s}$ .
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: To switch from constructive to destructive interference the speaker must be moved so that its sound travels an additional half-wavelength to reach you. Thus  $\frac{\lambda}{2} = 0.34\mathrm{m}$  and we have  $\lambda = 0.68\mathrm{m}$ . The frequency must then be  $f = \frac{340\mathrm{m / s}}{0.68\mathrm{m}} = 500\mathrm{Hz}$ . The correct answer is C.

questions.questionText: 26.63. $\nu = f\lambda$ , so if the frequency is doubled, then the wavelength must decrease by half to keep the wave speed constant.
questions.expectedAnswer: Solve: If the wavelength decreases by half, then the distance between neighboring zones of constructive interference will also decrease by half. Thus, all the students who originally heard the tone will still hear the tone. However, of the students that did not hear the tone, half of them will now hear the tone. The correct answer is D.
